topics.title: Population and Sample
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GDMAT401 - MATHEMATICS FOR LIFE SCIENCE – 4
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Population and Sample

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Population (Statistical Definition):** In statistics, a **population** is the entire set of individuals, objects, events, or measurements that share at least one common characteristic of interest to the researcher. It is the complete collection about which we seek information. The population may be **finite** (a countable, bounded set) or **infinite** (an unbounded, uncountable set or a process that can produce an unlimited number of observations).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sample:** A **sample** is a finite subset of the population, carefully selected according to a defined procedure, whose characteristics are studied in order to draw valid conclusions (statistical inferences) about the entire population. Every element of the population should have a known, non-zero probability of being chosen.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a doctor studying the **average blood pressure of all diabetic patients in Kerala**. It is practically impossible to examine every single diabetic patient in the state. So, you pick **5,000 patients** from different hospitals and measure their blood pressure. Here:

- **Population** = All diabetic patients in Kerala (huge, possibly millions).
- **Sample** = The 5,000 patients you actually measured.
- **Goal** = Use the sample's average (called a **statistic**) to estimate the population's true average (called a **parameter**).

> [!NOTE]
> Think of the **population** as the entire ocean and the **sample** as a bucket of water drawn from that ocean. By analyzing the bucket, you make conclusions about the ocean.

### 1.3 Key Terminology (Bold Highlights)

- **Element / Individual:** A single member of the population.
- **Sampling Frame:** A list, map, or other device that enumerates every element in the population so that each can be identified for sampling.
- **Census:** A study that attempts to collect data from the **entire population** (not a sample).
- **Parameter:** A numerical measure that describes a **population** (denoted by Greek letters like $\mu$, $\sigma$, $\pi$).
- **Statistic:** A numerical measure that describes a **sample** (denoted by Roman letters like $\bar{x}$, $s$, $p$).
- **Variable:** A characteristic of interest whose value can vary from one element to another.

### 1.4 Types of Population

> [!NOTE]
> **Finite Population:** The number of elements is countable and fixed, e.g., the number of leaves on a tree, the patients in a hospital, voters in a constituency. The size $N$ is known and limited.
>
> **Infinite Population:** Theoretically, the number of elements is uncountable or unlimited, e.g., the number of red blood cells in a human body, the outcomes of repeatedly tossing a coin, the number of bacteria colonies growing over time.

### 1.5 KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For the course GDMAT401 (Mathematics for Life Science – 4), students are expected to distinguish clearly between **descriptive statistics** of a sample and **inferential statistics** that generalize to a population. The concepts of population, sample, parameter, statistic, and sampling variability form the **conceptual foundation** for all subsequent modules on estimation and hypothesis testing.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Sampling? — The Underlying Logic

1. **Practicality:** Measuring every individual in a population is often impossible due to time, cost, or logistical constraints.
2. **Destructiveness:** In life sciences, some measurements destroy the specimen (e.g., blood glucose assay, biopsy, bacterial lysis), so we cannot test all units.
3. **Speed:** A well-designed sample can yield a useful conclusion much faster than a full census.
4. **Accuracy:** A skilled statistician can, with proper sampling, produce more accurate information from a well-selected sample than from a poorly conducted census.

### 2.2 The Sampling Process — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Define the Population:** Clearly state who/what the study concerns. (A vague population ⇒ meaningless conclusions.)
2. **Construct the Sampling Frame:** A practical list of all population elements.
3. **Choose a Sampling Method:** Decide between probability (random) sampling and non-probability sampling.
4. **Determine Sample Size ($n$):** The minimum number of observations needed to achieve a desired precision and confidence.
5. **Collect the Data:** Execute the sampling plan.
6. **Analyze the Data:** Compute sample statistics and use them to **infer** population parameters.

### 2.3 Probability (Random) Sampling Methods

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Simple Random Sampling (SRS):** Every possible sample of size $n$ has an equal chance of being selected, and every individual in the population has an equal chance of being in the sample. Selection is typically done using a **Random Number Table** or a **computer-based pseudo-random generator**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stratified Sampling:** The population is divided into mutually exclusive and collectively exhaustive sub-groups called **strata** (e.g., age groups, gender, disease severity). A random sample is drawn from each stratum. This method **reduces sampling error** when strata are internally homogeneous and heterogeneous between each other.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Systematic Sampling:** Every $k^{\text{th}}$ element of the sampling frame is selected, where the sampling interval is $k = N/n$. If $N = 1000$ and $n = 50$, then $k = 20$; the starting point is chosen randomly between $1$ and $k$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cluster Sampling:** The population is divided into clusters (often geographic), and a random selection of clusters is made. Either *all* elements in chosen clusters are studied (one-stage) or a sub-sample is drawn from each (two-stage). Useful when a complete sampling frame is unavailable.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Multistage Sampling:** A combination approach. For example: (i) select districts randomly, (ii) within each district select hospitals randomly, (iii) within each hospital select patients randomly.

### 2.4 Non-Probability Sampling Methods

- **Convenience Sampling:** Subjects are chosen because they are readily available (e.g., students in a single class). Risk of **selection bias** is high.
- **Judgment / Purposive Sampling:** The researcher uses expert judgment to select "typical" cases.
- **Snowball Sampling:** Existing study subjects recruit future subjects. Useful for hard-to-reach populations (e.g., rare disease patients, drug users).
- **Quota Sampling:** The sample is structured to reflect certain demographic proportions, but selection within quotas is non-random.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Symbol | Formula / Definition | Population vs Sample | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Population size | $N$ | Total number of elements | Population | A natural number |
| Sample size | $n$ | Number of observations in the sample | Sample | A natural number, $n < N$ |
| Mean (Average) | $\mu$ | $\mu = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i}{N}$ | Population parameter | Same units as $x$ |
| Mean (Average) | $\bar{x}$ | $\bar{x} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i}{n}$ | Sample statistic | Same units as $x$ |
| Variance | $\sigma^2$ | $\sigma^2 = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \mu)^2}{N}$ | Population parameter | Squared units of $x$ |
| Variance | $s^2$ | $s^2 = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{x})^2}{n-1}$ | Sample statistic | Squared units of $x$ |
| Std. Deviation | $\sigma$ | $\sigma = \sqrt{\sigma^2}$ | Population parameter | Same units as $x$ |
| Std. Deviation | $s$ | $s = \sqrt{s^2}$ | Sample statistic | Same units as $x$ |
| Proportion | $\pi$ | $\pi = \dfrac{X}{N}$ | Population parameter | Dimensionless, $0 \le \pi \le 1$ |
| Proportion | $p$ | $p = \dfrac{x}{n}$ | Sample statistic | Dimensionless, $0 \le p \le 1$ |
| Sampling Interval | $k$ | $k = \dfrac{N}{n}$ | Systematic sampling | A natural number |

> [!NOTE]
> **Greek letters = Parameters (describe population). Roman letters = Statistics (describe sample).** This convention is universal in statistics and is **frequently asked** in KTU exams.

### 2.6 Real-World Utility in Life Sciences & Engineering

- **Clinical Trials:** Stratified sampling ensures that treatment and control groups are balanced in age, gender, and disease stage.
- **Epidemiology:** Cluster sampling identifies disease hotspots in large geographic regions.
- **Quality Control (Pharma / Food):** Systematic sampling of every $k^{\text{th}}$ product on an assembly line monitors defects.
- **Environmental Science:** Multistage sampling estimates pollution levels across a state.
- **Genomics:** Random sampling of DNA sequences enables unbiased population genetics studies.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Population Parameter to Sample Statistic

We want to show how a **sample statistic** $\bar{x}$ serves as a **point estimator** of the **population parameter** $\mu$.

**Step 1: The Population Mean (Definition).**
Let the population consist of $N$ elements with values $x_1, x_2, \dots, x_N$. The true population mean is defined as the arithmetic average of all elements:

$$
\mu \;=\; \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i
$$

This $\mu$ is a **fixed (but usually unknown) constant** for the population.

**Step 2: Drawing a Random Sample.**
A sample of $n$ elements is drawn at random: $X_1, X_2, \dots, X_n$. Because the sample is random, each $X_i$ is a **random variable** with the same distribution as the population, with

$$
E(X_i) = \mu, \quad \text{Var}(X_i) = \sigma^2
$$

**Step 3: The Sample Mean (Statistic).**
The sample mean is computed from the realized sample values $x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n$:

$$
\bar{x} \;=\; \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i
$$

**Step 4: Expected Value of $\bar{x}$ (Unbiasedness).**
Using the linearity of expectation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E(\bar{x}) &= E\!\left(\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i\right) \\
&= \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} E(X_i) \\
&= \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \mu \\
&= \frac{1}{n}\cdot n \mu \\
&= \mu
\end{aligned}
$$

This proves that $\bar{x}$ is an **unbiased estimator** of $\mu$.

**Step 5: Variance of $\bar{x}$ (Sampling Variability).**
Assuming the sample is drawn **with replacement** (or from a large population, so selections are approximately independent):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Var}(\bar{x}) &= \text{Var}\!\left(\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i\right) \\
&= \frac{1}{n^2}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\text{Var}(X_i) \\
&= \frac{1}{n^2}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \sigma^2 \\
&= \frac{1}{n^2}\cdot n\sigma^2 \\
&= \frac{\sigma^2}{n}
\end{aligned}
$$

So the **standard error of the mean** is

$$
\text{SE}(\bar{x}) \;=\; \sqrt{\text{Var}(\bar{x})} \;=\; \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key insight:** Increasing the sample size $n$ by a factor of $4$ reduces the standard error by a factor of $2$. This is the **square-root law of sampling**.

**Step 6: Finite Population Correction (FPC).**
If sampling is done **without replacement** from a finite population, the variance becomes:

$$
\text{Var}(\bar{x}) \;=\; \frac{\sigma^2}{n}\cdot\frac{N-n}{N-1}
$$

The factor $\dfrac{N-n}{N-1}$ is called the **Finite Population Correction (FPC)**. It is significant when the **sampling fraction** $n/N > 5\%$.

### 3.2 Worked Example: Numerical Illustration

**Problem:** A pharmaceutical company wants to estimate the mean recovery time (in days) of patients taking a new drug. The population of all such patients has $\mu = 14$ days and $\sigma = 3$ days. A simple random sample of $n = 36$ patients is drawn. Compute the standard error of the mean.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SE}(\bar{x}) &= \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} \\
&= \frac{3}{\sqrt{36}} \\
&= \frac{3}{6} \\
&= 0.5 \text{ days}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** If we repeatedly drew samples of $36$ patients, the sample mean $\bar{x}$ would vary around the true mean $\mu = 14$ with a standard deviation of $0.5$ days. So most sample means would fall in the range $14 \pm 1$ day, i.e., between $13$ and $15$ days.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Sampling Techniques

| Sampling Method | When to Use | Strength | Weakness | Bias Risk |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Simple Random** | Homogeneous population, frame available | Easiay to analyze, unbiased | May be impractical for large $N$ | Low |
| **Stratified** | Distinct sub-groups exist | Reduces variance, ensures subgroup representation | Requires accurate strata info | Low |
| **Systematic** | Ordered sampling frame available | Easy to execute, spreads sample evenly | Periodic frame $\Rightarrow$ bias | Medium |
| **Cluster** | Geographic spread, no full frame | Cost-effective, logistically simple | Higher variance than SRS | Medium |
| **Multistage** | Large-scale national surveys | Flexible, practical | Complex analysis | Medium |
| **Convenience** | Exploratory pilot studies | Quick, cheap | High selection bias | **High** |
| **Snowball** | Hidden / rare populations | Reaches hard-to-find subjects | Not representative | **High** |

### 3.4 Effect of Sample Size on Precision

Suppose we wish the standard error to be at most $0.25$ days. Using $\sigma = 3$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} &\le 0.25 \\
\frac{3}{\sqrt{n}} &\le 0.25 \\
\sqrt{n} &\ge \frac{3}{0.25} = 12 \\
n &\ge 144
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> We need at least $n = 144$ patients to achieve a standard error no greater than $0.25$ days.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Map: From Population to Inference

The following Mermaid flowchart captures the logical pipeline from a defined population to statistical inference. Every node uses purely alphanumeric IDs and clean uppercase labels, with no internal markdown formatting.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[POPULATION N Elements] --> B[SAMPLING FRAME Constructed List]
    B --> C{Choose Sampling Method}
    C --> C1[Simple Random]
    C --> C2[Stratified]
    C --> C3[Systematic]
    C --> C4[Cluster Multistage]
    C --> C5[Non Probability]
    C1 --> D[SAMPLE n Elements with n less than N]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    C5 --> D
    D --> E[COLLECT DATA Observations]
    E --> F[COMPUTE SAMPLE STATISTICS x bar, s squared, p]
    F --> G{INFERENCE Procedure}
    G --> G1[POINT ESTIMATION Estimate Parameter]
    G --> G2[CONFIDENCE INTERVAL Range for Parameter]
    G --> G3[HYPOTHESIS TESTING Decision about Claim]
    G1 --> H[CONCLUSION about POPULATION]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Relationship Between Parameter and Statistic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[POPULATION Parameter mu sigma squared pi] -->|Unknown Fixed Constant| S[SAMPLE Statistic x bar s squared p]
    S -->|Sample Statistic approximates Parameter| I[INFERENTIAL STATISTICS]
    I -->|Estimation| E1[Point and Interval Estimates]
    I -->|Testing| E2[Hypothesis Tests]
    E1 --> D[DECISIONS about POPULATION]
    E2 --> D
```

### 4.3 Process Architecture: The Five-Stage Sampling Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[STAGE 1 Define the Target Population] --> S2[STAGE 2 Build the Sampling Frame]
    S2 --> S3[STAGE 3 Select Sampling Design and Compute n]
    S3 --> S4[STAGE 4 Draw the Sample and Collect Data]
    S4 --> S5[STAGE 5 Analyze Data and Report with Uncertainty]
```

### 4.4 Comparison Tree: Probability vs Non-Probability Sampling

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[SAMPLING METHODS] --> R1[PROBABILITY SAMPLING]
    R --> R2[NON PROBABILITY SAMPLING]
    R1 --> R1A[Simple Random]
    R1 --> R1B[Stratified]
    R1 --> R1C[Systematic]
    R1 --> R1D[Cluster]
    R1 --> R1E[Multistage]
    R2 --> R2A[Convenience]
    R2 --> R2B[Judgment Purposive]
    R2 --> R2C[Quota]
    R2 --> R2D[Snowball]
```

> [!NOTE]
> Each block above corresponds to a row in Section 3.3. Together, the Mermaid maps and the analytical matrix form a complete **functional architecture** of the population-sample system.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Dec 2024 Style]**
> Distinguish between a **population** and a **sample** with a suitable example from the life sciences. State why samples are studied instead of populations.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **population** is the entire group of individuals or items about which we want information, while a **sample** is a subset of the population that is actually studied. (1 mark)

*Example:* To study the average cholesterol level of all diabetic patients in India, the population is *all* diabetic patients in India, while a sample might consist of 2,000 patients drawn from various hospitals. (1 mark)

Samples are studied because: (i) it is often impossible, too costly, or too time-consuming to measure the entire population; (ii) some measurements are destructive; (iii) a well-chosen sample yields accurate and timely inferences about the population. (1 mark)

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, June 2024 Style]**
> Explain the difference between a **parameter** and a **statistic**. Give one example of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **parameter** is a numerical summary that describes a **population**; it is usually a fixed but unknown constant and is conventionally denoted by **Greek letters** such as $\mu$ (population mean) and $\sigma^2$ (population variance). (1 mark)

A **statistic** is a numerical summary computed from a **sample**; it varies from sample to sample and is conventionally denoted by **Roman letters** such as $\bar{x}$ (sample mean) and $s^2$ (sample variance). (1 mark)

*Example:* The average lifespan of all Wistar rats in a research colony is a **parameter** $\mu$. The average lifespan of the 30 rats in a particular sub-study is a **statistic** $\bar{x}$. (1 mark)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Module 1 Internal Choice: Answer ANY ONE of the following.**

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Dec 2024 Style]**
> (a) Explain the following sampling methods with one life-science example each: (i) Simple Random Sampling, (ii) Stratified Sampling, (iii) Systematic Sampling, (iv) Cluster Sampling. (7 marks)
>
> (b) The systolic blood pressure (in mmHg) of a population of $N = 5000$ hypertensive patients has a mean $\mu = 145$ mmHg and a standard deviation $\sigma = 12$ mmHg. A simple random sample of $n = 64$ patients is drawn. Find the expected value and standard error of the sample mean $\bar{x}$. If sampling is done **without replacement**, recalculate the standard error using the finite population correction. (7 marks)

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**(i) Simple Random Sampling (SRS) (2 marks):**
Every individual in the population has an equal chance of being selected, and every possible sample of size $n$ has an equal chance of being chosen. The sample is selected using a **random number table** or a **computer-generated random sequence**. *Life-science example:* Selecting 100 patient records from a hospital registry of 5,000 cardiac patients by random number generation to estimate the mean ejection fraction.

**(ii) Stratified Sampling (2 marks):**
The population is first divided into mutually exclusive sub-groups called **strata** based on a characteristic thought to affect the variable of interest (e.g., age, gender, disease stage). A random sample is then drawn from **each** stratum, often proportionally. *Life-science example:* In a clinical trial, dividing patients into age strata (18–35, 36–55, 56–75) and randomly assigning treatment within each stratum to ensure balanced age representation.

**(iii) Systematic Sampling (1.5 marks):**
A starting point is chosen at random between $1$ and $k$, and then every $k^{\text{th}}$ element is selected, where the sampling interval $k = N/n$. *Life-science example:* A microbiologist testing every 10th agar plate from a production line of 200 plates to check for bacterial contamination.

**(iv) Cluster Sampling (1.5 marks):**
The population is divided into **clusters** (often based on geography or natural groupings), and a random sample of clusters is selected. Either all members of the chosen clusters are studied (one-stage) or a sub-sample is drawn from each chosen cluster (two-stage). *Life-science example:* To estimate the prevalence of anemia in rural Kerala, randomly select 20 out of 150 Primary Health Centres and study all schoolchildren in those PHCs.

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Given:** $N = 5000$, $n = 64$, $\mu = 145$ mmHg, $\sigma = 12$ mmHg.

**Step 1: Expected value of $\bar{x}$ (3 marks).**
The sample mean $\bar{x}$ is an unbiased estimator of $\mu$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E(\bar{x}) &= \mu \\
&= 145 \text{ mmHg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Standard error of $\bar{x}$ with replacement (2 marks).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SE}(\bar{x}) &= \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} \\
&= \frac{12}{\sqrt{64}} \\
&= \frac{12}{8} \\
&= 1.5 \text{ mmHg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Standard error without replacement using the FPC (2 marks).**

The sampling fraction is $n/N = 64/5000 = 0.0128 = 1.28\%$, which is below the 5% threshold, but we apply the FPC anyway as required:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SE}_{\text{FPC}}(\bar{x}) &= \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{N-n}{N-1}} \\
&= 1.5 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{5000-64}{5000-1}} \\
&= 1.5 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{4936}{4999}} \\
&= 1.5 \cdot \sqrt{0.98740} \\
&= 1.5 \cdot 0.99368 \\
&\approx 1.4905 \text{ mmHg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** $E(\bar{x}) = 145$ mmHg; $\text{SE}(\bar{x}) = 1.5$ mmHg; $\text{SE}_{\text{FPC}}(\bar{x}) \approx 1.49$ mmHg.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, June 2024 Style]**
> (a) Define a **sampling distribution** of the sample mean. State and explain the **Central Limit Theorem (CLT)**. A population has mean $\mu = 50$ and standard deviation $\sigma = 10$. If samples of size $n = 25$ are drawn, find the probability that the sample mean $\bar{x}$ lies between $48$ and $52$. (Assume normal population.) (7 marks)
>
> (b) Explain the difference between **probability sampling** and **non-probability sampling**. Discuss the strengths and limitations of **convenience sampling** versus **stratified sampling** in a clinical research setting. (7 marks)

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Definition (1 mark):**
The **sampling distribution of the sample mean** is the probability distribution of the statistic $\bar{x}$ computed from all possible samples of size $n$ that could be drawn from the population.

**Central Limit Theorem (2 marks):**
When simple random samples of size $n$ are drawn from a population with mean $\mu$ and finite standard deviation $\sigma$, then as $n$ becomes large, the sampling distribution of the sample mean $\bar{x}$ approaches a **normal distribution** with mean $\mu$ and standard deviation $\sigma/\sqrt{n}$, *regardless of the shape of the original population distribution*. The rule of thumb is $n \ge 30$ for non-normal populations, but if the population is itself normal, the sampling distribution is normal for **any** $n$.

**Step 1: Mean and Standard Error of $\bar{x}$ (1 mark).**

$$
E(\bar{x}) = \mu = 50, \qquad \text{SE}(\bar{x}) = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} = \frac{10}{\sqrt{25}} = \frac{10}{5} = 2
$$

**Step 2: Standardize the bounds (1.5 marks).**

For $\bar{x} = 48$:

$$
z_1 = \frac{48 - 50}{2} = \frac{-2}{2} = -1
$$

For $\bar{x} = 52$:

$$
z_2 = \frac{52 - 50}{2} = \frac{2}{2} = +1
$$

**Step 3: Compute the probability (1.5 marks).**

$$
P(48 \le \bar{x} \le 52) = P(-1 \le Z \le 1) = \Phi(1) - \Phi(-1) = 0.8413 - 0.1587 = 0.6826
$$

**Final Answer:** The probability is approximately $0.6826$ or **68.26%**.

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Probability vs Non-Probability Sampling (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Probability Sampling | Non-Probability Sampling |
|---|---|---|
| Selection mechanism | Known, non-zero chance for every element | Chance of selection is unknown or zero for some elements |
| Bias | Minimized by design | Selection bias is likely |
| Generalizability | High (results extend to population) | Limited (results are suggestive only) |
| Examples | SRS, Stratified, Systematic, Cluster | Convenience, Judgment, Quota, Snowball |
| Cost & Time | Higher | Lower |

**Convenience Sampling (2 marks):**
*Strengths:* Quick, inexpensive, useful for pilot studies and hypothesis generation.
*Limitations:* Subjects are non-random, so the sample is unlikely to represent the population. Risk of **selection bias**, **volunteer bias**, and **non-generalizable results**. Not acceptable for definitive clinical trials.

**Stratified Sampling (2 marks):**
*Strengths:* Ensures representation of all key sub-groups (e.g., gender, age, disease stage). **Reduces sampling error** by controlling variance within strata. Allows subgroup analysis with adequate power.
*Limitations:* Requires accurate prior knowledge of the population structure to define strata. More complex and expensive than SRS.

**Conclusion (implicit):** For confirmatory clinical research, **stratified sampling** is the preferred method when the population is heterogeneous, while convenience sampling is reserved only for early-stage exploratory work.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $\sigma$ and $s$:** Use $\sigma / \sqrt{n}$ only when the **population** standard deviation $\sigma$ is given. If you are given the **sample** standard deviation $s$ and asked to estimate the SE, the formula is $s / \sqrt{n}$ (a $t$-based confidence interval is then appropriate, not a $z$-based one).
> 2. **Forgetting the FPC:** When $n/N > 0.05$, you **must** apply the finite population correction. Examiners specifically test this.
> 3. **Mixing parameter and statistic notation:** Always use $\mu$ (not $\bar{x}$) for population mean and $\bar{x}$ (not $\mu$) for sample mean. The examiner checks this strictly.
> 4. **Skipping the "without replacement" condition:** In SRS, the textbook default is **without replacement**, so apply the FPC unless told otherwise.
> 5. **No units in final answer:** Always attach the unit (mmHg, days, kg, etc.) to numerical answers. Marks are deducted for missing units.
> 6. **CLT misapplication:** The Central Limit Theorem applies to the distribution of $\bar{x}$, not to the original population. Writing "the population becomes normal" loses marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Population** = entire group of interest; **Sample** = a subset actually studied.
- **Parameter** (Greek letter, e.g., $\mu, \sigma, \pi$) describes a **population**; **Statistic** (Roman letter, e.g., $\bar{x}, s, p$) describes a **sample**.
- **Finite population** has a countable $N$; **infinite population** does not.
- **Sampling frame** is the operational list of all population elements.
- **Census** studies the whole population; **Sample survey** studies a part.
- **Probability sampling methods:** Simple Random, Stratified, Systematic, Cluster, Multistage.
- **Non-probability methods:** Convenience, Judgment, Quota, Snowball.
- **Stratified sampling** reduces sampling error when strata are internally homogeneous and heterogeneous between each other.
- **Systematic sampling** uses the interval $k = N/n$ with a random start.
- **Cluster sampling** is preferred when a complete sampling frame is unavailable or geography dominates.
- **Unbiasedness:** $E(\bar{x}) = \mu$.
- **Standard error of the mean:** $\text{SE}(\bar{x}) = \sigma / \sqrt{n}$ (with replacement).
- **Finite Population Correction (FPC):** $\text{SE}_{\text{FPC}}(\bar{x}) = (\sigma / \sqrt{n}) \cdot \sqrt{(N-n)/(N-1)}$.
- **Square-root law:** Quadrupling $n$ halves the SE; to halve the SE, you must **quadruple** $n$.
- **Central Limit Theorem:** For large $n$, $\bar{x}$ is approximately normal with mean $\mu$ and standard deviation $\sigma / \sqrt{n}$, regardless of the population's shape. The rule of thumb is $n \ge 30$ for non-normal populations.
- **Notation rule:** Greek letters = parameters, Roman letters = statistics — this convention is **universally** followed and **frequently** tested in KTU exams.
- **Rule of 5%:** Apply the FPC when the sampling fraction $n/N > 0.05$.
- **Sample size formula:** To achieve SE $\le E$, the required $n \ge (\sigma / E)^2$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Sample Mean
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** GDMAT401 - MATHEMATICS FOR LIFE SCIENCE – 4 > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** The Sample Mean # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition of the Sample Mean In the language of **Probability and Inferential Statistics**, the **Sample Mean** (denoted mathematically as $\bar{X}$ or $\bar{x}$ depending on whether it is treated as a random variable or a numerical value) is the arithmetic average of a finite collection of observations drawn randomly from a larger population. Mathematically, for a sample of size $n$ containing observations $X_1, X_2, X_3, \ldots, X_n$: $$\bar{X} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i = \frac{X_1 + X_2 + X_3 + \cdots + X_n}{n}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology** > The sample mean $\bar{X}$ is a **random variable** when considered before the sample is drawn (since it depends on the random outcomes $X_i$). Once the sample is actually drawn and the numerical values are known, it becomes a specific observed value, denoted $\bar{x}$, which is a realization of the random variable $\bar{X}$. ## 1.2 Population Mean vs. Sample Mean To fully appreciate the sample mean, we must distinguish it from the population mean: | Quantity | Symbol | Definition | Nature | |----------|--------|------------|--------| | Population Mean | $\mu$ | $\mu = E(X)$ | Fixed, but usually unknown parameter | | Sample Mean | $\bar{X}$ | $\bar{X} = \frac{1}{n}\sum X_i$ | Random variable (varies sample to sample) | > [!NOTE] > The sample mean $\bar{X}$ serves as a **point estimator** of the population mean $\mu$. One of the most important questions in statistics is: *How good is this estimator?* ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition > [!NOTE] > **"The Soup Tasting Analogy"** 🍲 > > Imagine a massive industrial kettle containing **10,000 litres of soup**. You want to know the average salt concentration (in grams per litre) of the entire kettle, but testing all 10,000 litres is impractical. > > So, you stir the kettle thoroughly (to ensure randomness) and ladle out **10 small tasting cups** of exactly 100 ml each. You measure the salt in each cup and average them: $\bar{x} = \frac{x_1 + x_2 + \cdots + x_{10}}{10}$. > > This $\bar{x}$ is your **Sample Mean** — a single number that *summarizes* the whole kettle. The kettle's true average is the **Population Mean** $\mu$, which is fixed but unknown. The sample mean is your best educated guess. ## 1.4 Why the Sample Mean Matters in Life Sciences In life sciences (B.Tech Biotechnology, Food Technology, Biomedical Engineering contexts), the sample mean is used in: - Measuring average drug efficacy across patient cohorts. - Estimating mean bacterial growth rates in microbiology. - Average pollutant concentration in environmental samples. - Average nutritional content in food science testing. The **law of large numbers** guarantees that as $n$ grows, $\bar{X} \to \mu$, making the sample mean the most reliable single-number summary of a population. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Properties of the Sample Mean The sample mean possesses several statistically powerful properties that make it the canonical estimator of central tendency. Below, each property is broken into its operational logic. ### Property 1: Unbiasedness - **Why it matters:** An estimator is *unbiased* if its expected value equals the true parameter it estimates. - **How it works:** Since each $X_i$ is drawn from the same population, $E(X_i) = \mu$ for all $i$. Linearity of expectation then yields $E(\bar{X}) = \mu$. - **Conclusion:** $\bar{X}$ is an **unbiased estimator** of $\mu$. ### Property 2: Minimum Variance Among Unbiased Linear Estimators - **Why it matters:** Among all unbiased estimators that are linear combinations of $X_i$, the sample mean has the *smallest possible variance*. - **How it works:** Any other linear unbiased estimator $T = \sum c_i X_i$ must have $\sum c_i = 1$, and the variance $Var(T)$ is minimized uniquely when all $c_i = \frac{1}{n}$. ### Property 3: Consistency - **Why it matters:** A consistent estimator converges in probability to the true parameter as $n \to \infty$. - **How it works:** By the Weak Law of Large Numbers, $\bar{X} \xrightarrow{P} \mu$. ### Property 4: Efficiency (Cramér-Rao Bound) - The sample mean attains the **Cramér-Rao Lower Bound** for the variance of unbiased estimators of $\mu$, making it the *most efficient* unbiased estimator. ## 2.2 Expected Value and Variance of the Sample Mean ### Expected Value (Mean of $\bar{X}$) $$E(\bar{X}) = E\!\left(\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i\right) = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} E(X_i) = \frac{1}{n}(n\mu) = \mu$$ ### Variance of $\bar{X}$ (Independent Samples) If $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n$ are **mutually independent** with common variance $\sigma^2$: $$Var(\bar{X}) = Var\!\left(\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i\right) = \frac{1}{n^2}\sum_{i=1}^{n} Var(X_i) = \frac{1}{n^2}(n\sigma^2) = \frac{\sigma^2}{n}$$ ### Standard Error of the Mean (SEM) The standard deviation of $\bar{X}$ is called the **Standard Error of the Mean (SEM)**: $$SEM = \sigma_{\bar{X}} = \sqrt{Var(\bar{X})} = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **The $\sqrt{n}$ Rule** > The standard error of the mean decreases as the **square root of the sample size**. To halve the SEM, you must *quadruple* the sample size. This is one of the most exam-tested facts in KTU statistics modules. ## 2.3 Sampling Distribution of the Sample Mean The **sampling distribution** is the probability distribution of $\bar{X}$ considered as a random variable over all possible samples of size $n$. ### Case A: Normal Population If $X_i \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$ independently, then exactly: $$\bar{X} \sim N\!\left(\mu, \frac{\sigma^2}{n}\right)$$ Equivalently, the **standardized sample mean**: $$Z = \frac{\bar{X} - \mu}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}} \sim N(0, 1)$$ ### Case B: Non-Normal Population — Central Limit Theorem (CLT) If the population is *not* normal but $n$ is sufficiently large (the rule of thumb is $n \geq 30$): $$\bar{X} \dot\sim N\!\left(\mu, \frac{\sigma^2}{n}\right)$$ > [!NOTE] > The CLT is the **cornerstone** of inferential statistics. It allows us to use normal-probability methods even when the underlying population is heavily skewed (e.g., exponential, Poisson), provided $n$ is large. ### Case C: Finite Population Correction (FPC) If the sample is drawn *without replacement* from a finite population of size $N$, the variances are reduced by the **finite population correction factor** $\frac{N-n}{N-1}$: $$Var(\bar{X}) = \frac{\sigma^2}{n} \cdot \frac{N-n}{N-1}$$ ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Formula | Expression | Conditions / Notes | |---------|-----------|--------------------| | Sample Mean | $\bar{X} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i$ | Always valid for any sample | | Mean of $\bar{X}$ | $E(\bar{X}) = \mu$ | Unbiasedness property | | Variance of $\bar{X}$ | $Var(\bar{X}) = \frac{\sigma^2}{n}$ | Requires *independent* samples | | Standard Error | $SE(\bar{X}) = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$ | The "error" in $\bar{X}$ as estimator | | Standardized $\bar{X}$ | $Z = \frac{\bar{X} - \mu}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}} \sim N(0,1)$ | Normal population, or large $n$ | | Sampling Distribution (Normal Pop.) | $\bar{X} \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2 / n)$ | Exact for any $n$ | | Sampling Distribution (CLT) | $\bar{X} \dot\sim N(\mu, \sigma^2 / n)$ | Approximate, $n \geq 30$ typical | | Finite Population Correction | $Var(\bar{X}) = \frac{\sigma^2}{n}\cdot\frac{N-n}{N-1}$ | Without replacement, $n/N > 0.05$ | | Sample Variance (Unbiased) | $S^2 = \frac{1}{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(X_i - \bar{X})^2$ | Used when $\sigma^2$ is unknown | | $\sigma_{\bar{X}}$ vs. $S_{\bar{X}}$ | $S_{\bar{X}} = \frac{S}{\sqrt{n}}$ | Estimated SE when $\sigma$ unknown | | t-Statistic | $T = \frac{\bar{X} - \mu}{S / \sqrt{n}} \sim t_{n-1}$ | When $\sigma$ unknown, normal population | > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Utility in Engineering & Life Sciences** > The sample mean and its standard error underpin every confidence interval, every t-test, every ANOVA table, and every quality-control chart (X-bar charts in Six-Sigma manufacturing) used in pharmaceutical, biotech, and food industries. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Expected Value of the Sample Mean **Statement:** Prove that $E(\bar{X}) = \mu$. **Proof:** $$ \begin{aligned} E(\bar{X}) &= E\!\left(\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i\right) && \text{(Substituting the definition of } \bar{X}\text{)} \\ &= \frac{1}{n}\, E\!\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i\right) && \text{(Constant } \tfrac{1}{n} \text{ pulled outside the expectation)} \\ &= \frac{1}{n}\, \sum_{i=1}^{n} E(X_i) && \text{(Linearity of the expectation operator)} \\ &= \frac{1}{n}\, \sum_{i=1}^{n} \mu && \text{(Each } X_i \text{ is drawn from a population with mean } \mu\text{)} \\ &= \frac{1}{n}\,(n\mu) && \text{(Summing } \mu \text{ exactly } n \text{ times)} \\ &= \mu && \text{(Simplification)} \end{aligned} $$ **Conclusion:** The sample mean is an **unbiased estimator** of the population mean $\mu$. $\blacksquare$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Variance of the Sample Mean (Independent Case) **Statement:** Prove that $Var(\bar{X}) = \dfrac{\sigma^2}{n}$ when $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n$ are independent with common variance $\sigma^2$. **Proof:** $$ \begin{aligned} Var(\bar{X}) &= Var\!\left(\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i\right) && \text{(Definition of } \bar{X}\text{)} \\ &= \frac{1}{n^2}\, Var\!\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i\right) && \text{(Variance of a constant multiple: } Var(cY) = c^2 Var(Y)\text{)} \\ &= \frac{1}{n^2}\, \sum_{i=1}^{n} Var(X_i) && \text{(Independence } \Rightarrow \text{Covariances vanish)} \\ &= \frac{1}{n^2}\, \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sigma^2 && \text{(Each } X_i \text{ has variance } \sigma^2\text{)} \\ &= \frac{1}{n^2}\,(n\sigma^2) && \text{(Summing } \sigma^2 \text{ exactly } n \text{ times)} \\ &= \frac{\sigma^2}{n} && \text{(Simplification)} \end{aligned} $$ **Conclusion:** The variance of the sample mean equals the population variance divided by $n$. $\blacksquare$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Standard Error from the Variance We take the positive square root of the variance to obtain the standard deviation of $\bar{X}$: $$ \begin{aligned} \sigma_{\bar{X}} &= \sqrt{Var(\bar{X})} \\ &= \sqrt{\frac{\sigma^2}{n}} \\ &= \frac{\sqrt{\sigma^2}}{\sqrt{n}} \\ &= \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} \end{aligned} $$ This quantity is what KTU examiners call the **Standard Error of the Mean (SEM)**. --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Distribution of $\bar{X}$ for a Normal Population **Statement:** If $X_i \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$ independently for $i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$, then $\bar{X} \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2/n)$. **Proof Sketch (using MGFs):** The moment generating function of $X_i$ is $M_{X_i}(t) = e^{\mu t + \frac{1}{2}\sigma^2 t^2}$. For a sum of independent normals, the MGF of $S_n = \sum X_i$ is the product of individual MGFs: $$ \begin{aligned} M_{S_n}(t) &= \prod_{i=1}^{n} M_{X_i}(t) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} e^{\mu t + \frac{1}{2}\sigma^2 t^2} \\ &= e^{n\mu t + \frac{1}{2}n\sigma^2 t^2} \end{aligned} $$ This is the MGF of a normal distribution with mean $n\mu$ and variance $n\sigma^2$. So $S_n \sim N(n\mu, n\sigma^2)$. For $\bar{X} = S_n / n$, we use the scaling property of MGFs: $M_{cX}(t) = M_X(ct)$. $$ \begin{aligned} M_{\bar{X}}(t) &= M_{S_n}\!\left(\frac{t}{n}\right) = e^{n\mu \cdot (t/n) + \frac{1}{2}n\sigma^2 \cdot (t/n)^2} \\ &= e^{\mu t + \frac{1}{2}(\sigma^2/n) t^2} \end{aligned} $$ This is the MGF of $N(\mu, \sigma^2/n)$. Hence: $$\bar{X} \sim N\!\left(\mu, \frac{\sigma^2}{n}\right) \qquad \blacksquare$$ --- ## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Z-Score Transformation Starting from $\bar{X} \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2/n)$, we standardize to obtain a standard normal: $$ \begin{aligned} Z &= \frac{\bar{X} - E(\bar{X})}{\sqrt{Var(\bar{X})}} \\ &= \frac{\bar{X} - \mu}{\sqrt{\sigma^2/n}} \\ &= \frac{\bar{X} - \mu}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}} \end{aligned} $$ By the property that linear transformations of normal random variables remain normal, and the scaling is precisely chosen to make the variance equal to 1, we conclude: $$Z = \frac{\bar{X} - \mu}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}} \sim N(0, 1)$$ This $Z$-statistic is used for computing probabilities like $P(\bar{X} > c)$ and for constructing confidence intervals. --- ## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Problem:** The lifetimes (in hours) of electric bulbs produced in a factory follow a normal distribution with mean $\mu = 1200$ hours and standard deviation $\sigma = 100$ hours. A random sample of $n = 25$ bulbs is taken. Find the probability that the sample mean lifetime exceeds 1220 hours. **Solution:** **Step 1:** Identify the sampling distribution. Since the population is normal and samples are independent: $$\bar{X} \sim N\!\left(\mu, \frac{\sigma^2}{n}\right) = N\!\left(1200, \frac{100^2}{25}\right) = N(1200, 400)$$ **Step 2:** Compute the standard error of the mean. $$\sigma_{\bar{X}} = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} = \frac{100}{\sqrt{25}} = \frac{100}{5} = 20 \text{ hours}$$ **Step 3:** Standardize to a Z-score. $$ \begin{aligned} P(\bar{X} > 1220) &= P\!\left(\frac{\bar{X} - 1200}{20} > \frac{1220 - 1200}{20}\right) \\ &= P(Z > 1.0) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4:** Look up the standard normal table. $$P(Z > 1.0) = 1 - \Phi(1.0) = 1 - 0.8413 = 0.1587$$ **Conclusion:** There is approximately a **15.87%** chance that the sample mean lifetime of 25 bulbs exceeds 1220 hours. $\blacksquare$ --- ## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Sample Size on SEM | Sample Size $n$ | Standard Error $SEM = \sigma/\sqrt{n}$ (with $\sigma = 100$) | Reduction Factor vs. $n=1$ | |-----------------|---------------------------------------------------------------|-----------------------------| | 1 | $100.00$ | 1.000× | | 4 | $50.00$ | 0.500× | | 9 | $33.33$ | 0.333× | | 16 | $25.00$ | 0.250× | | 25 | $20.00$ | 0.200× | | 36 | $16.67$ | 0.167× | | 49 | $14.29$ | 0.143× | | 64 | $12.50$ | 0.125× | | 81 | $11.11$ | 0.111× | | 100 | $10.00$ | 0.100× | > [!NOTE] > This table is a **high-yield KTU visual**. Examiners frequently ask: *"How does the standard error change when sample size is multiplied by $k$?"* Answer: It decreases by a factor of $\sqrt{k}$. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Conceptual Flow — From Population to Sample Mean ```mermaid flowchart TD A["POPULATION
Mean mu, Variance sigma-squared
Size N (often large or infinite)"] -->|Random Sampling n observations| B["SAMPLE
X1, X2, X3, ..., Xn
Size n"] B -->|Arithmetic Average| C["SAMPLE MEAN
Xbar = sum Xi / n
= Realization of random variable"] A -.->|True but unknown| D["POPULATION MEAN mu"] C -->|Unbiased Estimator of| D C -->|Has Sampling Distribution| E["DISTRIBUTION OF Xbar
Mean = mu
Variance = sigma-squared / n"] E -->|Standardize| F["Z STATISTIC
Z = Xbar - mu / sigma/sqrt(n)
N(0,1)"] E -->|sigma unknown, normal pop| G["T STATISTIC
T = Xbar - mu / S/sqrt(n)
t-distribution, df = n-1"] F --> H["INFERENCE
Confidence Intervals
Hypothesis Tests"] G --> H ``` ## 4.2 Block Architecture — The Estimation Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph A["STAGE 1: DATA ACQUISITION"] A1["Define Population of Interest"] --> A2["Choose Sample Size n"] A2 --> A3["Draw Random Sample X1 to Xn"] end subgraph B["STAGE 2: COMPUTATION"] B1["Sum all Xi values"] --> B2["Divide by n"] B2 --> B3["Obtain Xbar (point estimate)"] end subgraph C["STAGE 3: UNCERTAINTY QUANTIFICATION"] C1["Compute sample variance S-squared"] --> C2["Calculate SEM = S / sqrt(n)"] C2 --> C3["Form Confidence Interval Xbar ± Z SEM"] end A --> B B --> C C --> D["DECISION OUTPUT
Estimate of mu with stated confidence"] ``` ## 4.3 Decision Tree — Which Sampling Distribution to Use? ```mermaid flowchart TD Start["Starting: Need P Xbar in interval"] --> Q1{"Is population
normal?"} Q1 -->|YES| Q2{"Is sigma
known?"} Q1 -->|NO| Q3{"Is n large
(n >= 30)?"} Q3 -->|YES| Q4["Use CLT:
Xbar approx N mu, sigma-sq/n
Z = Xbar - mu / sigma/sqrt(n)"] Q3 -->|NO| Q5["Use exact distribution of Xbar
or bootstrap methods"] Q2 -->|YES| Q6["Z = Xbar - mu / sigma/sqrt(n)
Standard Normal N(0,1)"] Q2 -->|NO| Q7["T = Xbar - mu / S/sqrt(n)
t-distribution, df = n-1"] Q4 --> End["Compute probability"] Q5 --> End Q6 --> End Q7 --> End ``` ## 4.4 Visual Comparison — Population vs. Sampling Distribution ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph POP["POPULATION DISTRIBUTION X ~ N(mu, sigma-sq)"] POP1["Wide spread
SD = sigma = 100"] end subgraph SAM["SAMPLING DISTRIBUTION Xbar ~ N(mu, sigma-sq/n)"] SAM1["Narrow spread
SD = sigma/sqrt(n) = 20
(for n = 25)"] end POP -->|Take n=25 independent obs
Average them| SAM POP -.->|Centered at mu| SAM SAM -.->|Centered at mu| POP ``` > [!NOTE] > **Pedagogical Insight:** The sampling distribution of $\bar{X}$ is *always* centered at the same mean $\mu$ as the population, but it has a **dramatically smaller spread** (smaller standard deviation) by a factor of $\sqrt{n}$. This is the geometric intuition behind why averaging gives more precise estimates than single observations. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`** **Q:** Define the *sample mean*. State and prove its expected value. **Model Answer:** The **sample mean** is the arithmetic average of $n$ observations $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n$ drawn randomly from a population, defined as: $$\bar{X} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i$$ **Expected Value Proof:** $$ \begin{aligned} E(\bar{X}) &= E\!\left(\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i\right) \\ &= \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} E(X_i) \quad \text{(Linearity of expectation)} \\ &= \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \mu \quad \text{(Each } X_i \text{ has mean } \mu\text{)} \\ &= \mu \end{aligned} $$ Hence $E(\bar{X}) = \mu$, confirming that the sample mean is an **unbiased estimator** of the population mean. **(3 marks)** --- ### Question 2 **`[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`** **Q:** What is the *standard error of the mean*? Why is it smaller than the population standard deviation? **Model Answer:** The **Standard Error of the Mean (SEM)** is the standard deviation of the sample mean $\bar{X}$ as a random variable, given by: $$SEM = \sigma_{\bar{X}} = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$$ where $\sigma$ is the population standard deviation and $n$ is the sample size. **Why it is smaller:** Since the divisor is $\sqrt{n} \geq 1$ (for $n \geq 1$), we have $SEM = \sigma / \sqrt{n} \leq \sigma$. The factor $\sqrt{n}$ in the denominator arises because averaging $n$ independent observations *partially cancels out* the random fluctuations in individual $X_i$ values. As $n$ grows, the sample mean becomes a more precise estimator of $\mu$, and its variability shrinks. **Example:** If $\sigma = 100$ and $n = 25$, then $SEM = 100/5 = 20$, which is **5 times smaller** than $\sigma$. **(3 marks)** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) ### Question A (Choice 1) **`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Adaptation]`** **Q:** The blood cholesterol levels (in mg/dL) of adults in a city follow a normal distribution with mean $\mu = 190$ mg/dL and standard deviation $\sigma = 25$ mg/dL. **(a) [7 Marks]** A random sample of $n = 16$ adults is selected. Find the probability that the sample mean cholesterol level lies between 185 mg/dL and 195 mg/dL. **(b) [7 Marks]** How large a sample size is required so that the probability that the sample mean differs from $\mu$ by less than 5 mg/dL is at least 0.95? --- #### Model Solution to (a) **Step 1:** Since the population is normal, the sample mean is exactly normally distributed: $$\bar{X} \sim N\!\left(\mu, \frac{\sigma^2}{n}\right) = N\!\left(190, \frac{25^2}{16}\right) = N(190, 39.0625)$$ **Step 2:** Compute the standard error: $$\sigma_{\bar{X}} = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} = \frac{25}{\sqrt{16}} = \frac{25}{4} = 6.25 \text{ mg/dL}$$ **Step 3:** Standardize the bounds. Lower bound: $$Z_1 = \frac{185 - 190}{6.25} = \frac{-5}{6.25} = -0.80$$ Upper bound: $$Z_2 = \frac{195 - 190}{6.25} = \frac{5}{6.25} = 0.80$$ **Step 4:** Compute the probability using the standard normal table: $$ \begin{aligned} P(185 \leq \bar{X} \leq 195) &= P(-0.80 \leq Z \leq 0.80) \\ &= \Phi(0.80) - \Phi(-0.80) \\ &= \Phi(0.80) - [1 - \Phi(0.80)] \\ &= 2\Phi(0.80) - 1 \\ &= 2(0.7881) - 1 \\ &= 0.5762 \end{aligned} $$ **Answer:** $P(185 \leq \bar{X} \leq 195) \approx 0.5762$ or about **57.62%**. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to (b) **Step 1:** We require: $$P(|\bar{X} - \mu| < 5) \geq 0.95$$ **Step 2:** Standardize: $$P\!\left(\left|\frac{\bar{X} - \mu}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}}\right| < \frac{5}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}}\right) \geq 0.95$$ $$P\!\left(|Z| < \frac{5\sqrt{n}}{25}\right) \geq 0.95$$ $$P\!\left(|Z| < \frac{\sqrt{n}}{5}\right) \geq 0.95$$ **Step 3:** From the standard normal table, for the central probability to be 0.95, we need: $$\frac{\sqrt{n}}{5} \geq z_{0.025} = 1.96$$ **Step 4:** Solve for $n$: $$\sqrt{n} \geq 5 \times 1.96 = 9.80$$ $$n \geq 9.80^2 = 96.04$$ Since $n$ must be an integer, $n = 97$. **Answer:** A sample size of at least **$n = 97$** adults is required. **(7 marks)** --- ### Question B (Choice 2) **`[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Retest Pattern]`** **Q:** Let $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_{36}$ be a random sample from a distribution with mean $\mu = 80$ and variance $\sigma^2 = 144$. **(a) [7 Marks]** Using the Central Limit Theorem, find the approximate probability that the sample mean exceeds 83. **(b) [7 Marks]** If the population is *not* normal, can we still compute this probability? Justify using the CLT and state the conditions. --- #### Model Solution to (a) **Step 1:** Identify the given parameters: $n = 36$, $\mu = 80$, $\sigma^2 = 144 \Rightarrow \sigma = 12$. **Step 2:** By the Central Limit Theorem, since $n = 36 \geq 30$: $$\bar{X} \dot\sim N\!\left(80, \frac{144}{36}\right) = N(80, 4)$$ **Step 3:** Compute the standard error: $$\sigma_{\bar{X}} = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} = \frac{12}{\sqrt{36}} = \frac{12}{6} = 2$$ **Step 4:** Standardize: $$ \begin{aligned} P(\bar{X} > 83) &= P\!\left(\frac{\bar{X} - 80}{2} > \frac{83 - 80}{2}\right) \\ &= P(Z > 1.5) \\ &= 1 - \Phi(1.5) \\ &= 1 - 0.9332 \\ &= 0.0668 \end{aligned} $$ **Answer:** $P(\bar{X} > 83) \approx 0.0668$ or about **6.68%**. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to (b) **Yes**, we can still compute the probability using the Central Limit Theorem (CLT), even if the population is non-normal, *provided the sample size is sufficiently large* (typically $n \geq 30$). **Justification:** The **Central Limit Theorem** states that if $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n$ are independent and identically distributed random variables with finite mean $\mu$ and finite variance $\sigma^2$, then as $n \to \infty$: $$Z_n = \frac{\bar{X} - \mu}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}} \xrightarrow{d} N(0, 1)$$ In other words, the standardized sample mean *converges in distribution* to a standard normal variable, *regardless of the shape of the underlying population distribution* (skewed, bimodal, exponential, etc.), as long as $n$ is large enough. **Conditions for CLT application:** 1. **Independence:** The $X_i$ must be independent (e.g., drawn via simple random sampling with replacement, or from an infinite/large population). 2. **Identical Distribution:** All $X_i$ must be drawn from the same population (or at least have the same mean and variance). 3. **Finite Variance:** The population variance $\sigma^2$ must be finite. 4. **Sample Size:** A rule of thumb is $n \geq 30$, though for highly skewed populations, $n \geq 50$ may be safer. In this problem, $n = 36 \geq 30$, so the CLT applies and the calculation in part (a) is valid even for a non-normal population. **(7 marks)** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls** > > 1. **Forgetting the $\sqrt{n}$ in the denominator:** The single most common error is writing $Var(\bar{X}) = \sigma^2$ instead of $\sigma^2/n$. This single mistake cascades through the entire problem. > > 2. **Confusing $\sigma$ and $S$:** When the population standard deviation is *unknown*, you must use the sample standard deviation $S$ and the $t$-distribution with $n-1$ degrees of freedom, **not** the $Z$-distribution. > > 3. **Ignoring the CLT condition:** The CLT is an *approximation*. Do not blindly apply it for $n < 30$ unless the population is known to be normal. > > 4. **Sign errors in Z-scores:** Always use $Z = (X - \mu)/\sigma$, not $Z = (\mu - X)/\sigma$. The order matters. > > 5. **Rounding too early:** Carry 4 decimal places until the final answer. Premature rounding leads to off-by-one errors in the last digit, costing 1 mark. > > 6. **Forgetting to state the formula for $E(\bar{X})$ and $Var(\bar{X})$ explicitly:** KTU examiners award marks for stating these *before* plugging in numbers. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — "The Sample Mean"** - **Definition:** $\bar{X} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i$. Always state this *before* solving any problem. - **Random Variable Nature:** $\bar{X}$ is a *random variable* before sampling; $\bar{x}$ is a *number* after sampling. - **Unbiasedness:** $E(\bar{X}) = \mu$. Always prove this using linearity of expectation. - **Variance:** $Var(\bar{X}) = \dfrac{\sigma^2}{n}$ (independent case) and $\dfrac{\sigma^2}{n}\cdot\dfrac{N-n}{N-1}$ (finite population, without replacement). - **Standard Error:** $SEM = \sigma / \sqrt{n}$. This is the *standard deviation of the sampling distribution*. - **Sampling Distribution (Normal Population):** $\bar{X} \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2/n)$ — exact for *any* $n$. - **Central Limit Theorem:** $\bar{X} \dot\sim N(\mu, \sigma^2/n)$ when the population is non-normal but $n$ is large (rule: $n \geq 30$). - **Z-Statistic:** $Z = \dfrac{\bar{X} - \mu}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}} \sim N(0, 1)$ — used when $\sigma$ is *known*. - **T-Statistic:** $T = \dfrac{\bar{X} - \mu}{S / \sqrt{n}} \sim t_{n-1}$ — used when $\sigma$ is *unknown* but the population is normal. - **$\sqrt{n}$ Rule:** To halve SEM, you must *quadruple* $n$. To reduce SEM by factor $k$, multiply $n$ by $k^2$. - **Practical Significance:** The sample mean is the **best linear unbiased estimator (BLUE)** of $\mu$ by the Gauss-Markov theorem. - **Life-Science Applications:** Drug efficacy trials, microbial growth averages, food quality testing, environmental monitoring. - **Key Mistake to Avoid:** $Var(\bar{X}) = \sigma^2$ (wrong) vs. $Var(\bar{X}) = \sigma^2/n$ (correct).

topics.title: Population Mean and Variance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** GDMAT401 - MATHEMATICS FOR LIFE SCIENCE – 4 > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Population Mean and Variance # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) In the context of **Biostatistics and Life Sciences**, statistical data is broadly classified into two categories: **Population** and **Sample**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Population (Universe):** The entire set of individuals, items, or measurements that share at least one common observable or measurable characteristic under investigation. The population can be **finite** (e.g., total number of patients in a hospital) or **infinite** (e.g., all possible bacteria cultures that could be grown). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Parameter:** A numerical measure that describes a characteristic of an entire population (e.g., Population Mean $\mu$, Population Variance $\sigma^2$). The **Population Mean** and **Population Variance** are the two most fundamental parameters used to summarize the central tendency and dispersion of a population, respectively. - The **Population Mean** (denoted by the Greek letter **$\mu$**, pronounced "mu") represents the **arithmetic average** of every single value in the entire population. - The **Population Variance** (denoted by **$\sigma^2$**, the Greek letter sigma squared) measures the **average of the squared deviations** of every population value from the population mean. It quantifies the spread or variability in the population. > [!NOTE] > **Key Distinction for KTU Exams:** Population parameters are constants (fixed but usually unknown), whereas sample statistics (denoted by $\bar{x}$ and $s^2$) are random variables that vary from sample to sample. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Explanation Imagine you are a **marine biologist** studying the length of a rare species of fish in a specific lake. - The **Population** is **every single fish** of that species living in the lake. - The **Population Mean ($\mu$)** is the average length if you could somehow measure **all** the fish. It is the "true" average length of the entire fish community. - The **Population Variance ($\sigma^2$)** tells you how much the lengths of the fish *deviate* from the average. If all fish are nearly the same size, the variance is small. If some are tiny juveniles and some are huge adults, the variance is large. > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy:** Think of the population as the **entire roll call of a university** (say, 5,000 students). The population mean is the average GPA of all 5,000 students calculated perfectly. Since measuring everyone is often impossible, we take a *sample*, but the population mean remains the theoretical "truth" we aim to estimate. ## 1.3 Standard Symbols and Notation (KTU Convention) | Symbol | Meaning | Type | |:------:|:--------|:-----| | $N$ | Total number of observations in the population | Constant | | $X_i$ | The $i$-th individual observation in the population | Random Variable | | $\mu$ | Population Mean (Arithmetic Average) | Parameter | | $\sigma^2$ | Population Variance | Parameter | | $\sigma$ | Population Standard Deviation (positive square root of $\sigma^2$) | Parameter | | $f_i$ | Frequency of the $i$-th class/observation | Frequency | | $\sum$ | Summation operator (sum of all values) | Operator | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight:** Life Science applications (biology, pharmacology, epidemiology) frequently deal with **large or infinite populations**. Mastery of population parameters is essential before progressing to sample-based inference (Module 2 onwards). # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Structured Logic: Building the Concept Step-by-Step ### Step 1 — Identify the Population Dataset Begin with a clearly defined population dataset consisting of $N$ observations: $$\{X_1, X_2, X_3, \dots, X_N\}$$ Each $X_i$ is a measurable quantity (e.g., blood pressure in mmHg, height in cm, bacterial count, drug dosage in mg). ### Step 2 — Compute the Central Value (Mean) The population mean $\mu$ is the single number that best represents the "center of mass" of all observations. It is the balance point of the data — the sum of all deviations from this point equals zero. ### Step 3 — Quantify the Spread (Variance) Once we have the center, we measure how far, on average, each observation lies from it. We **square** these deviations to: - Eliminate the sign (positive vs. negative deviations cancel otherwise). - Penalize large deviations more heavily (giving importance to outliers). ### Step 4 — The "Why" Behind Division by $N$ We divide by $N$ to obtain an *average* squared deviation. This gives a per-observation measure of spread, allowing fair comparison between populations of different sizes. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Master these formulas. They form the foundation of all statistical inference in life sciences.** | # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Condition / Use Case | |:-:|:-------------|:------------------------|:---------------------| | 1 | Population Mean (Raw Data) | $\mu = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} X_i$ | Ungrouped/discrete raw data | | 2 | Population Mean (Frequency Data) | $\mu = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} f_i X_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{k} f_i}$ | When data is grouped or frequency distribution is given | | 3 | Population Variance (Definition) | $\sigma^2 = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (X_i - \mu)^2$ | Definitional (mean-based) form | | 4 | Population Variance (Computational) | $\sigma^2 = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} X_i^2 - \mu^2$ | Raw-score computational shortcut | | 5 | Population Standard Deviation | $\sigma = \sqrt{\sigma^2} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(X_i - \mu)^2}$ | Always $\sigma \geq 0$ | | 6 | Linear Transformation of Mean | $\mu_{aX+b} = a\mu_X + b$ | If $Y = aX + b$ | | 7 | Linear Transformation of Variance | $\sigma^2_{aX+b} = a^2 \sigma^2_X$ | Constants $b$ don't affect variance | | 8 | Combined Mean (Two Populations) | $\mu = \dfrac{N_1 \mu_1 + N_2 \mu_2}{N_1 + N_2}$ | Merging two populations | | 9 | Combined Variance | $\sigma^2 = \dfrac{N_1 \sigma^2_1 + N_2 \sigma^2_2}{N_1 + N_2} + \dfrac{N_1 N_2(\mu_1 - \mu_2)^2}{(N_1+N_2)^2}$ | Merging two populations | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Exam Note (Avoid the Common Trap):** The population variance uses **$N$** in the denominator, NOT $N-1$. The $N-1$ denominator is reserved for the **sample variance** ($s^2$), which will be covered in subsequent modules. Using the wrong denominator is a guaranteed mark-deduction error. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering & Life Science Utility - **Pharmacology & Drug Development:** $\mu$ gives the average effective dose; $\sigma^2$ quantifies patient-to-patient variability in drug response. - **Epidemiology:** Population parameters describe the spread of diseases (e.g., mean cholesterol level of a country). - **Genetics & Bioinformatics:** Used to model allele frequencies and phenotypic variation in populations. - **Quality Control in Medical Devices:** Mean diameter of a syringe and variance of manufacturing tolerance must meet regulatory standards. - **Environmental Biology:** Mean concentration of a pollutant and its variance across sampling sites. ## 2.4 Properties of Population Mean and Variance 1. **Effect of Constant Shift:** If $Y = X + c$, then $\mu_Y = \mu_X + c$ and $\sigma^2_Y = \sigma^2_X$. 2. **Effect of Scaling:** If $Y = aX$, then $\mu_Y = a\mu_X$ and $\sigma^2_Y = a^2 \sigma^2_X$. 3. **Combined Effect:** If $Y = aX + b$, then $\mu_Y = a\mu_X + b$ and $\sigma^2_Y = a^2 \sigma^2_X$. 4. **Minimum Property:** The mean $\mu$ is the unique value that minimizes $\sum_{i=1}^{N} (X_i - c)^2$. Any other constant $c$ produces a larger sum of squared deviations. 5. **Variance is Non-Negative:** $\sigma^2 \geq 0$ always, with equality if and only if all $X_i$ are identical. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: From the Definition of Mean to the Computational Formula > [!NOTE] > **Why this derivation matters:** KTU examiners often ask students to prove the computational formula from the definitional one. This is a high-frequency 7-mark question type. ### Starting Point We begin with the definition of population variance: $$\sigma^2 = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (X_i - \mu)^2$$ ### Step 1: Expand the Squared Term Apply the algebraic identity $(a-b)^2 = a^2 - 2ab + b^2$ to each term inside the summation: $$\sigma^2 = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( X_i^2 - 2 X_i \mu + \mu^2 \right)$$ ### Step 2: Distribute the Summation Use the linearity of summation: $\sum (A + B + C) = \sum A + \sum B + \sum C$: $$\sigma^2 = \frac{1}{N} \left[ \sum_{i=1}^{N} X_i^2 - 2\mu \sum_{i=1}^{N} X_i + \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mu^2 \right]$$ ### Step 3: Apply the Definition of Mean By definition, $\mu = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} X_i$. Therefore, $\sum_{i=1}^{N} X_i = N\mu$. ### Step 4: Evaluate the Constant Sum The term $\mu^2$ is a constant with respect to the summation index $i$. Summing it $N$ times gives: $$\sum_{i=1}^{N} \mu^2 = N \mu^2$$ ### Step 5: Substitute and Simplify Replace the simplified terms into the equation: $$\sigma^2 = \frac{1}{N} \left[ \sum_{i=1}^{N} X_i^2 - 2\mu (N\mu) + N\mu^2 \right]$$ $$\sigma^2 = \frac{1}{N} \left[ \sum_{i=1}^{N} X_i^2 - 2N\mu^2 + N\mu^2 \right]$$ $$\sigma^2 = \frac{1}{N} \left[ \sum_{i=1}^{N} X_i^2 - N\mu^2 \right]$$ ### Step 6: Final Computational Formula Distribute the $\frac{1}{N}$: $$\sigma^2 = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} X_i^2 - \mu^2$$ This is the **computational (or shortcut) formula** for population variance, preferred for hand calculations and computational work. ## 3.2 Worked Analytical Example: Frequency Distribution > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Pattern:** The following is a fully solved problem typical of a 7-mark sub-question in the End Semester Examination. **Problem:** The following data represents the hemoglobin levels (g/dL) of **$N=50$ patients** (the entire patient population of a small clinic). Compute the population mean and population variance. | Hemoglobin ($X_i$) | Frequency ($f_i$) | |:------------------:|:-----------------:| | 10 | 8 | | 12 | 15 | | 14 | 17 | | 16 | 7 | | 18 | 3 | | **Total** | **50** | ### Step 1: Construct the Computational Table | $X_i$ | $f_i$ | $f_i X_i$ | $X_i^2$ | $f_i X_i^2$ | |:-----:|:------:|:----------:|:-------:|:-----------:| | 10 | 8 | 80 | 100 | 800 | | 12 | 15 | 180 | 144 | 2160 | | 14 | 17 | 238 | 196 | 3332 | | 16 | 7 | 112 | 256 | 1792 | | 18 | 3 | 54 | 324 | 972 | | **$\sum$** | **50** | **664** | — | **9056** | ### Step 2: Calculate the Population Mean Using the frequency-based formula: $$\mu = \frac{\sum f_i X_i}{\sum f_i} = \frac{664}{50} = 13.28 \text{ g/dL}$$ ### Step 3: Calculate the Mean of Squares $$\frac{1}{N} \sum f_i X_i^2 = \frac{9056}{50} = 181.12$$ ### Step 4: Apply the Computational Formula for Variance $$\sigma^2 = \frac{1}{N} \sum f_i X_i^2 - \mu^2$$ $$\sigma^2 = 181.12 - (13.28)^2$$ $$\sigma^2 = 181.12 - 176.3584$$ $$\sigma^2 = 4.7616 \text{ (g/dL)}^2$$ ### Step 5: Calculate the Standard Deviation $$\sigma = \sqrt{4.7616} \approx 2.1821 \text{ g/dL}$$ ### Step 6: Interpretation (Life Science Context) - **Mean:** The average hemoglobin level of the patient population is **$13.28$ g/dL**. - **Standard Deviation:** The hemoglobin levels typically vary by about **$\pm 2.18$ g/dL** from the mean. A small standard deviation indicates that most patients have hemoglobin levels clustered near the average, which is desirable in a controlled clinical setting. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Conceptual Flow Diagram: From Data to Parameters ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Raw Population Data
X1, X2, X3, ..., XN"] --> B["Identify N
Total Observations"] B --> C["Compute Sum of Values
Sum of Xi"] C --> D["Divide by N
to obtain Mu"] D --> E["Population Mean
Mu equals Sum Xi divided by N"] A --> F["Compute Deviations
Xi minus Mu"] F --> G["Square Each Deviation
Xi minus Mu squared"] G --> H["Sum the Squares
Sum of Xi minus Mu squared"] H --> I["Divide by N
to obtain Sigma squared"] I --> J["Population Variance
Sigma squared"] J --> K["Take Square Root
to obtain Sigma"] K --> L["Population Std Deviation
Sigma"] E --> M["Final Parameter Set
Mu and Sigma squared"] L --> M ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Population vs. Sample Inference ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph P["POPULATION DOMAIN"] P1["Entire Population
N observations"] --> P2["Population Mean
Mu"] P1 --> P3["Population Variance
Sigma squared"] P2 --> P4["Parameters
Fixed but Unknown"] P3 --> P4 end subgraph S["SAMPLE DOMAIN"] S1["Random Sample
n observations less than N"] --> S2["Sample Mean
x bar"] S1 --> S3["Sample Variance
s squared"] S2 --> S4["Statistics
Random Variables"] S3 --> S4 end P4 -->|Infer or Estimate| S4 S4 -->|Generalize back| P4 ``` ## 4.3 Decision Tree: Choosing the Correct Formula ```mermaid flowchart TD Q1["What type of data is given?"] --> Q2["Raw Ungrouped Data"] Q1 --> Q3["Frequency or Grouped Data"] Q2 --> F1["Use Mu equals Sum Xi divided by N"] Q2 --> F2["Use Sigma squared equals Sum Xi squared divided by N minus Mu squared"] Q3 --> F3["Use Mu equals Sum fi Xi divided by Sum fi"] Q3 --> F4["Use Sigma squared equals Sum fi Xi squared divided by Sum fi minus Mu squared"] F1 --> R["Compute Variance"] F2 --> R F3 --> R F4 --> R R --> S["Square root gives Standard Deviation"] ``` ## 4.4 Visualization: Mean as the Balance Point ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Axis["Number Line Representation"] A1["X1"] --- A2["X2"] --- A3["Mu
Balance Point"] --- A4["X4"] --- A5["X5"] end A1 -.->|"Deviation d1"| A3 A2 -.->|"Deviation d2"| A3 A3 -.->|"Deviation d3"| A4 A3 -.->|"Deviation d4"| A5 Axis --> Note["Sum of all deviations equals zero
Sum of Xi minus Mu equals 0"] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Interpretation:** The population mean $\mu$ is the **pivot point** such that the sum of all signed distances from it equals zero. This geometric intuition explains why squaring is necessary for variance — to convert all signed deviations into positive quantities for averaging. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model QP]` **Define the population mean and population variance. State the difference between a parameter and a statistic with one example each.** **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > **Population Mean ($\mu$):** The arithmetic average of all $N$ observations in a population, defined as $\mu = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} X_i$. > [!NOTE] > **Population Variance ($\sigma^2$):** The average of the squared deviations of all population values from the population mean, defined as $\sigma^2 = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(X_i - \mu)^2$. **Parameter vs Statistic:** - A **parameter** is a numerical measure describing a **population** characteristic (e.g., $\mu$ = mean hemoglobin of all 5,000 patients in a state). - A **statistic** is a numerical measure describing a **sample** characteristic (e.g., $\bar{x}$ = mean hemoglobin of 50 sampled patients). - Parameters are fixed constants; statistics are random variables that vary from sample to sample. ### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model QP]` **If the population variance of a dataset is $\sigma^2 = 25$, what happens to the variance if every observation is (a) increased by 10, (b) multiplied by 3?** **Model Answer:** **(a) Adding a constant 10:** - Variance is **unchanged**. Adding a constant shifts the location but not the spread. - New variance $= \sigma^2 = 25$. **(b) Multiplying by 3:** - Variance is **multiplied by $3^2 = 9$**. - New variance $= 9 \times 25 = 225$. ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question 1A `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper, ESE Module 1]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the computational formula for population variance $\sigma^2 = \frac{1}{N}\sum X_i^2 - \mu^2$ from the definitional formula $\sigma^2 = \frac{1}{N}\sum (X_i - \mu)^2$. **(b) [7 Marks]** The weights (in kg) of 10 patients in a clinical trial are: 55, 62, 58, 70, 65, 60, 72, 68, 64, 66. Calculate the population mean and population variance. --- ### Model Solution for 1A(a) — Derivation **Step 1:** Write the definition of population variance: $$\sigma^2 = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (X_i - \mu)^2$$ **Step 2:** Expand using the identity $(X_i - \mu)^2 = X_i^2 - 2X_i\mu + \mu^2$: $$\sigma^2 = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (X_i^2 - 2X_i\mu + \mu^2)$$ **Step 3:** Distribute the summation: $$\sigma^2 = \frac{1}{N} \left[ \sum_{i=1}^{N} X_i^2 - 2\mu \sum_{i=1}^{N} X_i + \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mu^2 \right]$$ **Step 4:** Apply $\sum X_i = N\mu$ and $\sum \mu^2 = N\mu^2$: $$\sigma^2 = \frac{1}{N} \left[ \sum_{i=1}^{N} X_i^2 - 2\mu \cdot N\mu + N\mu^2 \right]$$ **Step 5:** Simplify inside the brackets: $$\sigma^2 = \frac{1}{N} \left[ \sum_{i=1}^{N} X_i^2 - 2N\mu^2 + N\mu^2 \right] = \frac{1}{N} \left[ \sum_{i=1}^{N} X_i^2 - N\mu^2 \right]$$ **Step 6:** Final result: $$\boxed{\sigma^2 = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} X_i^2 - \mu^2}$$ --- ### Model Solution for 1A(b) — Numerical Computation **Step 1:** Compute the sum of weights. $$\sum_{i=1}^{10} X_i = 55 + 62 + 58 + 70 + 65 + 60 + 72 + 68 + 64 + 66 = 640$$ **Step 2:** Calculate the population mean. $$\mu = \frac{1}{N}\sum X_i = \frac{640}{10} = 64 \text{ kg}$$ **Step 3:** Compute the sum of squared values. | $X_i$ | $X_i^2$ | |:-----:|:-------:| | 55 | 3025 | | 62 | 3844 | | 58 | 3364 | | 70 | 4900 | | 65 | 4225 | | 60 | 3600 | | 72 | 5184 | | 68 | 4624 | | 64 | 4096 | | 66 | 4356 | | **Sum = 640** | **Sum = 41218** | **Step 4:** Apply the computational formula. $$\sigma^2 = \frac{1}{N}\sum X_i^2 - \mu^2 = \frac{41218}{10} - (64)^2$$ $$\sigma^2 = 4121.8 - 4096 = 25.6 \text{ kg}^2$$ **Step 5:** Standard deviation. $$\sigma = \sqrt{25.6} \approx 5.06 \text{ kg}$$ **Final Answer:** $\mu = 64$ kg, $\sigma^2 = 25.6$ kg², $\sigma \approx 5.06$ kg. --- ### Question 1B (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper, ESE Module 1]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Define population mean and population variance. Prove that the population mean minimizes the sum of squared deviations, i.e., show that $\sum_{i=1}^{N}(X_i - \mu)^2 \leq \sum_{i=1}^{N}(X_i - c)^2$ for any constant $c$. **(b) [7 Marks]** Two diagnostic centers report the following glucose readings (mg/dL) for a fasting blood test: - **Center A:** Mean $\mu_1 = 105$, Variance $\sigma_1^2 = 16$, $N_1 = 80$ patients - **Center B:** Mean $\mu_2 = 110$, Variance $\sigma_2^2 = 25$, $N_2 = 120$ patients Find the combined mean and combined variance of all 200 patients. --- ### Model Solution for 1B(a) — Minimum Property of Mean **Step 1:** Consider the function $f(c) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} (X_i - c)^2$, where $c$ is any constant. **Step 2:** Expand the squared term. $$f(c) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} (X_i^2 - 2X_i c + c^2) = \sum X_i^2 - 2c \sum X_i + N c^2$$ **Step 3:** Differentiate with respect to $c$ and set equal to zero to find the minimum. $$\frac{df}{dc} = -2\sum X_i + 2Nc = 0$$ **Step 4:** Solve for $c$. $$c = \frac{\sum X_i}{N} = \mu$$ **Step 5:** Verify it is a minimum (second derivative test). $$\frac{d^2 f}{dc^2} = 2N > 0 \quad (\text{since } N > 0)$$ This confirms that $c = \mu$ gives the **global minimum** of the sum of squared deviations. **Step 6:** Therefore, for any $c \neq \mu$: $$\sum_{i=1}^{N}(X_i - \mu)^2 \leq \sum_{i=1}^{N}(X_i - c)^2$$ The mean is the unique value that minimizes the total squared deviation from the data. --- ### Model Solution for 1B(b) — Combined Mean and Variance **Step 1:** Compute the combined mean using the weighted formula. $$\mu = \frac{N_1 \mu_1 + N_2 \mu_2}{N_1 + N_2} = \frac{(80)(105) + (120)(110)}{80 + 120}$$ $$\mu = \frac{8400 + 13200}{200} = \frac{21600}{200} = 108 \text{ mg/dL}$$ **Step 2:** Compute the combined variance using the formula. $$\sigma^2 = \frac{N_1 \sigma_1^2 + N_2 \sigma_2^2}{N_1 + N_2} + \frac{N_1 N_2 (\mu_1 - \mu_2)^2}{(N_1 + N_2)^2}$$ **Step 3:** Substitute the values. $$\sigma^2 = \frac{(80)(16) + (120)(25)}{200} + \frac{(80)(120)(105 - 110)^2}{(200)^2}$$ $$\sigma^2 = \frac{1280 + 3000}{200} + \frac{9600 \times 25}{40000}$$ $$\sigma^2 = \frac{4280}{200} + \frac{240000}{40000}$$ $$\sigma^2 = 21.4 + 6.0 = 27.4 \text{ (mg/dL)}^2$$ **Final Answer:** Combined mean $\mu = 108$ mg/dL; Combined variance $\sigma^2 = 27.4$ (mg/dL)². > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting the second term in combined variance:** Students often compute only the weighted average of variances and miss the cross-term $\frac{N_1 N_2 (\mu_1 - \mu_2)^2}{(N_1+N_2)^2}$, losing **3 marks** out of 7. > 2. **Confusing population vs sample variance formula:** Using $N-1$ instead of $N$ in the denominator is a critical conceptual error worth **2 marks**. > 3. **Forgetting to take the square root for $\sigma$:** The question may ask for standard deviation. If you only provide $\sigma^2$, deduct **1 mark**. > 4. **Arithmetic slip in $\mu_1 - \mu_2$:** Be careful with the sign and squaring — $(105-110)^2 = 25$, not $-25$. Squaring errors cost marks. ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Master These Before the Exam:** - **Population Mean $\mu$:** Arithmetic average of the *entire* population. Formula: $\mu = \frac{1}{N}\sum X_i$ (or frequency-weighted form). - **Population Variance $\sigma^2$:** Average squared deviation from $\mu$. Formula: $\sigma^2 = \frac{1}{N}\sum(X_i - \mu)^2$. - **Computational Shortcut:** $\sigma^2 = \frac{1}{N}\sum X_i^2 - \mu^2$ — preferred for hand calculations. - **Standard Deviation $\sigma$:** Always non-negative. $\sigma = \sqrt{\sigma^2}$. - **Linear Transformations:** Adding a constant $b$ does not change variance; multiplying by $a$ multiplies variance by $a^2$. - **Minimum Property:** The mean is the unique value that minimizes the sum of squared deviations. - **Population Denominator is $N$:** Not $N-1$. Save $N-1$ for the sample variance in the next module. - **Combined Mean:** Weighted average using population sizes as weights. - **Combined Variance:** Weighted average of variances **plus** a correction term for the difference in means. - **Interpret in Context:** Always conclude numerical answers with a real-life interpretation in life science terms (e.g., "average hemoglobin level is X g/dL with a typical variation of Y g/dL"). - **Squaring is Mandatory:** The sum of *raw* deviations from the mean is always zero. Variance is built by **squaring** deviations to make them all positive. - **Sign Convention:** $\sigma^2 \geq 0$ for any real dataset, with equality only if all values are identical.

topics.title: The Central Limit Theorem - Distribution of Sum of Random Variables
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GDMAT401 - MATHEMATICS FOR LIFE SCIENCE – 4
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** The Central Limit Theorem - Distribution of Sum of Random Variables

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sum of Independent Random Variables (KTU 2024 Definition)**
>
> Let $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n$ be a finite collection of **independent random variables**, each with its own probability distribution. The **sum** of these random variables is defined as:
>
> $$S_n = X_1 + X_2 + X_3 + \cdots + X_n = \sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i$$
>
> The **Moment Generating Function (MGF)** of the sum equals the **product** of the individual MGFs:
>
> $$M_{S_n}(t) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} M_{X_i}(t) = M_{X_1}(t) \cdot M_{X_2}(t) \cdots M_{X_n}(t)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Central Limit Theorem (CLT) — KTU Board Standard Statement**
>
> If $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n$ are **independent and identically distributed (i.i.d.)** random variables, each having **finite mean** $\mu$ and **finite variance** $\sigma^2 > 0$, then the standardized sum
>
> $$Z_n = \frac{S_n - n\mu}{\sigma\sqrt{n}} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i - n\mu}{\sigma\sqrt{n}}$$
>
> converges in distribution to the **standard normal random variable** $Z \sim N(0, 1)$ as $n \to \infty$.
>
> Formally:
>
