A **point estimate** is like throwing the harpoon once — you either hit the fish or you don't, but you have no idea of your accuracy.

A **two-sided confidence interval** is like deciding: *"I will throw a harpoon and announce that the fish is somewhere within a net of width $2 \cdot z_{\alpha/2} \cdot (S/\sqrt{n})$ around my landing point."* If I repeat this experiment many times, **95% of my nets (for a 95% CI) will cover the fish**. Notice the beautiful subtlety:

- The *fish* ($\mu$) is **fixed** — it does not move.
- The *net* (interval) is **random** — it shifts with each sample.
- We make a probability statement about the **net covering the fish**, *not* about the fish being inside any particular net.

> [!TIP]
> **The frequentist interpretation (KTU expected answer):** *If we were to repeat the sampling procedure a very large number of times and construct a CI from each sample, then approximately $(1-\alpha)\times 100\%$ of those intervals would contain the true parameter $\mu$.*

---

## 1.3 The Two Cases Encountered in Life-Science Applications

| Scenario | Variance Status | Test Statistic | Typical Use in Life Science |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Case 1** | Population variance $\sigma^2$ is **known** | Standard Normal $Z$ | Quality-control of calibrated lab instruments |
| **Case 2** | Population variance $\sigma^2$ is **unknown** (most realistic) | Student's $t$ with $(n-1)$ d.f. | Blood pressure, enzyme kinetics, clinical trials |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Emphasis:** In real life-science problems, you will almost always be in **Case 2** (unknown variance, small sample). The exam will, however, test **both cases** to ensure you can identify which pivot statistic to use.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

---

## 2.1 The Logic Behind Constructing a Confidence Interval (5-Step Logic Chain)

The construction of any CI for $\mu$ follows a rigorous 5-step roadmap that the KTU examiner expects you to articulate:

1. **Step 1 — Identify the parameter of interest.**
   Here it is the population mean $\mu$ of a continuous variable (e.g., systolic BP, glucose level, reaction time).

2. **Step 2 — Choose a sample statistic (point estimator).**
   The sample mean $\bar{X} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i$ is the unique *uniformly minimum variance unbiased estimator (UMVUE)* of $\mu$ under normal assumptions.

3. **Step 3 — Find the sampling distribution of the statistic.**
   By the Central Limit Theorem (CLT) or exact normal theory:
   - If $X_i \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$ exactly $\Longrightarrow \bar{X} \sim N\!\left(\mu, \dfrac{\sigma^2}{n}\right)$.
   - For large $n$ (rule of thumb $n \ge 30$), CLT makes $\bar{X}$ approximately normal even when $X_i$ are not.

4. **Step 4 — Construct a Pivotal Quantity.**
   A *pivotal quantity* $Q(X_1,\dots,X_n;\mu)$ is a function of the data and $\mu$ whose distribution **does not depend on any unknown parameter**. This is the "Rosetta Stone" of interval estimation.

5. **Step 5 — Invert the probability statement to isolate $\mu$.**
   Rewrite $P(a \le Q \le b) = 1-\alpha$ as $P(L \le \mu \le U) = 1-\alpha$ by algebra.

---

## 2.2 The Two Key Pivotal Quantities

### 🔹 Case A — $\sigma^2$ **Known** (Z-pivot)

$$Z \;=\; \frac{\bar{X} - \mu}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}} \;\sim\; N(0,1)$$

### 🔹 Case B — $\sigma^2$ **Unknown** (t-pivot)

$$T \;=\; \frac{\bar{X} - \mu}{S / \sqrt{n}} \;\sim\; t_{(n-1)}$$

where $S^2 = \dfrac{1}{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(X_i - \bar{X})^2$ is the sample variance.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All the formulas you will need for Module 2 questions on this topic. Memorize this table.**

| # | Situation | Pivotal Quantity | $(1-\alpha)\times 100\%$ Two-Sided CI for $\mu$ | Critical Value Definition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma^2$ known, $X$ normal **or** $n \ge 30$ (CLT) | $Z = \dfrac{\bar{X}-\mu}{\sigma/\sqrt{n}}$ | $\bar{X} \pm z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$ | $P(Z > z_{\alpha/2}) = \alpha/2$ |
| 2 | $\sigma^2$ unknown, $X$ normal, $n < 30$ | $T = \dfrac{\bar{X}-\mu}{S/\sqrt{n}}$ | $\bar{X} \pm t_{\alpha/2,\, n-1} \cdot \dfrac{S}{\sqrt{n}}$ | $P(T > t_{\alpha/2,\,n-1}) = \alpha/2$ |
| 3 | Margin of Error (common to both) | — | $E = z_{\alpha/2}\cdot\dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$ or $E = t_{\alpha/2}\cdot\dfrac{S}{\sqrt{n}}$ | $E$ is the half-width of the interval |
| 4 | Sample-size formula (for desired half-width $E$) | — | $n = \left(\dfrac{z_{\alpha/2}\cdot\sigma}{E}\right)^{2}$ (round **up**) | Use when $\sigma$ is known/piloted |

**Standard Critical-Value Quick-Reference (Kerala Board favourite values):**

| Confidence $(1-\alpha)$ | $\alpha$ | $\alpha/2$ | $z_{\alpha/2}$ (Normal) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 90% | 0.10 | 0.05 | 1.645 |
| 95% | 0.05 | 0.025 | 1.960 |
| 99% | 0.01 | 0.005 | 2.576 |

> [!TIP]
> For the $t$-distribution, you **must read the table** at the intersection of row $(n-1)$ and column $\alpha/2$. The KTU 2024 syllabus provides the $t$-table inside the question paper.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Life-Science & Bio-Engineering

- **Pharmacology / Clinical Trials:** Estimating the *true mean reduction* in systolic blood pressure for a new anti-hypertensive drug with 95% confidence.
- **Bio-process Engineering:** Estimating the *mean yield* of an enzyme from a fermentation batch.
- **Environmental Biostatistics:** Estimating the *mean pollutant concentration* in a water body to compare with safety thresholds.
- **Reliability Engineering (Medical Devices):** Estimating the *mean lifetime* of a pacemaker battery.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In regulatory submissions (FDA, CDSCO), pharmaceutical companies **must** report a two-sided 95% CI for the primary efficacy endpoint. A two-sided CI is the gold standard for "equivalence" or "non-inferiority" claims.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

---

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Case A ($\sigma^2$ Known)

**Goal:** Derive the CI from the pivot $Z$.

We begin with the standard normal probability statement covering the central $(1-\alpha)$ mass:

$$P\!\left(-z_{\alpha/2} \;\le\; Z \;\le\; +z_{\alpha/2}\right) \;=\; 1 - \alpha$$

Substitute the pivot $Z = \dfrac{\bar{X}-\mu}{\sigma/\sqrt{n}}$ into the inequality:

$$P\!\left(-z_{\alpha/2} \;\le\; \frac{\bar{X}-\mu}{\sigma/\sqrt{n}} \;\le\; +z_{\alpha/2}\right) \;=\; 1 - \alpha$$

Multiply every term inside by the positive quantity $\dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$ (this preserves the inequality direction):

$$P\!\left(-z_{\alpha/2}\cdot\frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} \;\le\; \bar{X}-\mu \;\le\; +z_{\alpha/2}\cdot\frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}\right) \;=\; 1 - \alpha$$

Subtract $\bar{X}$ from every term:

$$P\!\left(-\bar{X} - z_{\alpha/2}\cdot\frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} \;\le\; -\mu \;\le\; -\bar{X} + z_{\alpha/2}\cdot\frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}\right) \;=\; 1 - \alpha$$

Multiply through by $-1$ (this **reverses** the inequality signs):

$$P\!\left(\bar{X} - z_{\alpha/2}\cdot\frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} \;\le\; \mu \;\le\; \bar{X} + z_{\alpha/2}\cdot\frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}\right) \;=\; 1 - \alpha$$

**Reading off the limits:**

$$\boxed{\;L = \bar{X} - z_{\alpha/2}\cdot\dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}\;, \quad U = \bar{X} + z_{\alpha/2}\cdot\dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}\;}$$

This is the canonical $\bar{X} \pm E$ form, with **margin of error** $E = z_{\alpha/2}\cdot\dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$. $\quad \blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — Case B ($\sigma^2$ Unknown, Small Sample)

The derivation is structurally **identical** to Case A, except $Z$ is replaced by the $t$-pivot, and $\sigma$ is replaced by the sample standard deviation $S$:

$$P\!\left(-t_{\alpha/2,\,n-1} \;\le\; T \;\le\; +t_{\alpha/2,\,n-1}\right) \;=\; 1-\alpha$$

Substituting $T = \dfrac{\bar{X}-\mu}{S/\sqrt{n}}$:

$$P\!\left(-t_{\alpha/2,\,n-1} \;\le\; \frac{\bar{X}-\mu}{S/\sqrt{n}} \;\le\; +t_{\alpha/2,\,n-1}\right) \;=\; 1-\alpha$$

Multiplying by $S/\sqrt{n}$:

$$P\!\left(-t_{\alpha/2,\,n-1}\cdot\frac{S}{\sqrt{n}} \;\le\; \bar{X}-\mu \;\le\; +t_{\alpha/2,\,n-1}\cdot\frac{S}{\sqrt{n}}\right) \;=\; 1-\alpha$$

Rearranging to isolate $\mu$ (subtract $\bar{X}$ then multiply by $-1$):

$$P\!\left(\bar{X} - t_{\alpha/2,\,n-1}\cdot\frac{S}{\sqrt{n}} \;\le\; \mu \;\le\; \bar{X} + t_{\alpha/2,\,n-1}\cdot\frac{S}{\sqrt{n}}\right) \;=\; 1-\alpha$$

$$\boxed{\;\bar{X} \pm t_{\alpha/2,\,n-1}\cdot\dfrac{S}{\sqrt{n}}\;} \quad \blacksquare$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 1 — Case A (Known Variance, $n \ge 30$)

> **Problem.** The reaction time (in ms) of patients on a new drug is modelled as $N(\mu, 25)$. A random sample of $n=36$ patients yields $\bar{x} = 240$ ms. Construct a 95% two-sided confidence interval for $\mu$.

**Solution:**

Given: $\sigma = \sqrt{25} = 5$, $n = 36$, $\bar{x} = 240$, $1-\alpha = 0.95 \Rightarrow \alpha/2 = 0.025$, $z_{0.025} = 1.96$.

Standard error:

$$SE = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} = \frac{5}{\sqrt{36}} = \frac{5}{6} \approx 0.8333$$

Margin of error:

$$E = z_{0.025} \cdot SE = 1.96 \times 0.8333 \approx 1.633$$

Lower limit:

$$L = \bar{x} - E = 240 - 1.633 = 238.367$$

Upper limit:

$$U = \bar{x} + E = 240 + 1.633 = 241.633$$

$$\boxed{\;238.37 \le \mu \le 241.63 \;\text{ms}\;}$$

**Interpretation:** We are 95% confident that the true mean reaction time of the population lies between 238.37 ms and 241.63 ms.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 2 — Case B (Unknown Variance, Small Sample)

> **Problem.** The blood-glucose levels (mg/dL) of 9 diabetic patients are: 142, 138, 150, 145, 140, 148, 143, 146, 152. Assuming normality, find a 99% two-sided CI for the true mean glucose level $\mu$.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Compute the sample mean:

$$\bar{x} = \frac{142+138+150+145+140+148+143+146+152}{9} = \frac{1304}{9} \approx 144.889$$

Step 2 — Compute the sample standard deviation $S$ (we list deviations for clarity):

| $x_i$ | $x_i - \bar{x}$ | $(x_i - \bar{x})^{2}$ |
|---|---|---|
| 142 | $-2.889$ | $8.346$ |
| 138 | $-6.889$ | $47.457$ |
| 150 | $+5.111$ | $26.123$ |
| 145 | $+0.111$ | $0.012$ |
| 140 | $-4.889$ | $23.902$ |
| 148 | $+3.111$ | $9.679$ |
| 143 | $-1.889$ | $3.568$ |
| 146 | $+1.111$ | $1.234$ |
| 152 | $+7.111$ | $50.567$ |
| **Sum** |  | **$\sum = 170.889$** |

$$S^{2} = \frac{\sum (x_i-\bar{x})^{2}}{n-1} = \frac{170.889}{8} \approx 21.361$$

$$S = \sqrt{21.361} \approx 4.622$$

Step 3 — Find the critical value. Degrees of freedom $= n-1 = 8$, $\alpha/2 = 0.005$ (for 99% CI). From $t$-tables:

$$t_{0.005,\, 8} = 3.355$$

Step 4 — Standard error and margin of error:

$$SE = \frac{S}{\sqrt{n}} = \frac{4.622}{\sqrt{9}} = \frac{4.622}{3} \approx 1.541$$

$$E = t_{0.005,\, 8} \cdot SE = 3.355 \times 1.541 \approx 5.169$$

Step 5 — Confidence limits:

$$L = 144.889 - 5.169 = 139.720$$

$$U = 144.889 + 5.169 = 150.058$$

$$\boxed{\;139.72 \le \mu \le 150.06 \;\text{mg/dL}\;}$$

**Interpretation:** We are 99% confident that the true mean blood-glucose level of the patient population lies between 139.72 and 150.06 mg/dL.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example 3 — Sample-Size Determination (Board Favourite)

> **Problem.** A lab wants the 95% CI for the mean concentration of a biomarker to have a half-width not exceeding $E = 2$ units. From a pilot study, $\sigma \approx 8$. Find the required sample size.

**Solution:**

Using the formula $n = \left(\dfrac{z_{\alpha/2}\cdot \sigma}{E}\right)^{2}$ with $z_{0.025} = 1.96$:

$$n = \left(\frac{1.96 \times 8}{2}\right)^{2} = (7.84)^{2} = 61.4656$$

Always round **up** to guarantee the desired precision:

$$\boxed{\;n = 62\;}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 📕 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

---

## 4.1 Decision Flowchart — "Which Test Should I Use?"

The following Mermaid flowchart captures the exact decision tree a KTU student must follow to choose between the Z-procedure and the t-procedure.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["START: Need a 2-sided CI for the population mean mu"] --> B{"Is population variance sigma squared KNOWN?"}
    B -- "YES" --> C{"Is n greater than or equal to 30 OR is X exactly Normal?"}
    B -- "NO" --> D{"Is X exactly Normal AND n is less than 30?"}
    C -- "YES" --> E["USE Z-PROCEDURE: CI is X-bar plus or minus z alpha/2 times sigma over root n"]
    C -- "NO" --> F["Use CLT approximation: Z-procedure is still valid for large n"]
    D -- "YES" --> G["USE T-PROCEDURE: CI is X-bar plus or minus t alpha/2, n-1 times S over root n"]
    D -- "NO" --> H["Increase sample size to n greater than or equal to 30, then use Z"]
    E --> I["Compute LOWER and UPPER limits, then write final interval"]
    F --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I["END: Report the confidence interval in the form L less than or equal to mu less than or equal to U"]
```

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Anatomy of a Confidence Interval

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SAMPLE["SAMPLE DATA LAYER"]
        A1["Random Sample X1, X2, ..., Xn of size n"] --> A2["Compute sample mean X-bar"]
        A1 --> A3["Compute sample standard deviation S"]
    end
    subgraph CRIT["CRITICAL VALUE LAYER"]
        B1["Choose confidence level 1 minus alpha"] --> B2{"Variance known?"}
        B2 -- "YES" --> B3["Look up z alpha/2 from Normal table"]
        B2 -- "NO" --> B4["Look up t alpha/2, n-1 from t-table"]
    end
    subgraph CALC["COMPUTATION LAYER"]
        C1["Margin of Error E equals z or t times SE"] --> C2["Lower limit L equals X-bar minus E"]
        C1 --> C3["Upper limit U equals X-bar plus E"]
    end
    subgraph OUT["OUTPUT LAYER"]
        D1["Final 2-sided CI: L less than or equal to mu less than or equal to U"]
        D2["Interpretation: We are 1 minus alpha times 100 percent confident..."]
    end
    A2 --> C1
    A3 --> C1
    B3 --> C1
    B4 --> C1
    C2 --> D1
    C3 --> D1
    D1 --> D2
```

---

## 4.3 Concept Map — Linking the Building Blocks

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["Point Estimation: X-bar estimates mu"] --> Q1["Pivotal Quantity Z or T"]
    P2["Standard Error SE equals sigma over root n or S over root n"] --> Q1
    Q1 --> R1["Probability Statement P(minus crit less than or equal to pivot less than or equal to plus crit) equals 1 minus alpha"]
    R1 --> R2["Algebraic Inversion to isolate mu"]
    R2 --> S1["Two-Sided Confidence Interval: X-bar plus or minus Margin of Error"]
    S1 --> T1["Decision: is parameter inside the interval?"]
    T1 -- "Yes" --> U1["No significant difference from hypothesized value"]
    T1 -- "No" --> U2["Significant difference exists"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define a two-sided $(1-\alpha)\times 100\%$ confidence interval for the population mean $\mu$. State the assumptions under which the standard normal-based CI is valid.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A two-sided $(1-\alpha)\times 100\%$ confidence interval for $\mu$ is a random interval $[L, U]$ constructed from a sample such that

$$P(L \le \mu \le U) = 1-\alpha.$$

**Assumptions for the Z-based CI:**
1. The sample is a simple random sample (SRS) of size $n$.
2. The population variance $\sigma^2$ is known.
3. Either the population is normal, or $n \ge 30$ so the Central Limit Theorem applies.

The interval is

$$\bar{X} \pm z_{\alpha/2}\cdot\frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}.$$

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Distinguish between a point estimate and an interval estimate. Why is an interval estimate considered more informative in life-science research?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Point Estimate | Interval Estimate |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | A single value that estimates the parameter | A range of plausible values for the parameter |
| Information content | Only the best guess | Combines the estimate with a measure of its precision |
| Uncertainty | Not captured | Captured via the margin of error and confidence level |

In life-science research (e.g., estimating mean drug efficacy), a point estimate alone is misleading because it gives no sense of variability. A 95% CI communicates both the magnitude **and** the precision of the estimate, enabling better clinical and regulatory decisions. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Format

> **KTU 2024 ESE Format:** Attempt **any one** of the two full questions. Each question has sub-parts (a) and (b) of 7 marks each.

---

### 🅰️ **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Model Question]

**(a)** A random sample of $n = 25$ observations from a normal population with unknown mean and variance yields $\bar{x} = 78.4$ and $s = 6.2$. Construct a **95% two-sided confidence interval** for the population mean $\mu$. Given $t_{0.025, 24} = 2.064$. (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain with reasoning what happens to the width of the CI when (i) the sample size is quadrupled, and (ii) the confidence level is increased from 95% to 99%. (7 marks)

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Question A

**(a) Solution:**

Given: $n = 25$, $\bar{x} = 78.4$, $s = 6.2$, $1-\alpha = 0.95$, $\alpha/2 = 0.025$, $t_{0.025,\,24} = 2.064$.

Since $\sigma^2$ is unknown and $n = 25 < 30$, use the **$t$-procedure** with $n-1 = 24$ degrees of freedom.

Standard error:

$$SE = \frac{s}{\sqrt{n}} = \frac{6.2}{\sqrt{25}} = \frac{6.2}{5} = 1.24$$

Margin of error:

$$E = t_{0.025,\,24} \cdot SE = 2.064 \times 1.24 = 2.5594 \approx 2.559$$

Lower limit:

$$L = 78.4 - 2.559 = 75.841$$

Upper limit:

$$U = 78.4 + 2.559 = 80.959$$

$$\boxed{\;(75.84,\; 80.96)\;}$$

**Conclusion:** We are 95% confident that the true population mean $\mu$ lies between **75.84** and **80.96**. (7 marks)

**(b) Solution:**

**Case (i): Sample size quadrupled ($n \to 4n$).**

The new margin of error becomes:

$$E_{new} = t_{\alpha/2,\, 4n-1} \cdot \frac{s}{\sqrt{4n}} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot t_{\alpha/2,\, 4n-1} \cdot \frac{s}{\sqrt{n}}$$

For moderate-to-large new $n$, $t_{\alpha/2,\,4n-1} \approx z_{\alpha/2}$, so approximately the new half-width is **half** the old one. Hence the CI width becomes roughly **half** the original — i.e., quadrupling $n$ **halves the width** (increases precision). (3 marks)

**Case (ii): Confidence level raised from 95% to 99%.**

The critical value **increases** from $t_{0.025,\,24} = 2.064$ to $t_{0.005,\,24} = 2.797$. Since $E \propto t_{\alpha/2,\,n-1}$, the new margin of error is **larger**, and the interval becomes **wider**. This is the *price* of demanding higher confidence. (2 marks)

**Comparative insight:** Higher confidence and higher precision (narrower interval) are *competing* goals — to obtain a 99% CI with the *same width* as a 95% CI, you would need to roughly **double** the sample size. (2 marks)

---

### 🅱️ **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Model Question]

**(a)** The systolic blood pressure (mmHg) of 16 patients on a new anti-hypertensive drug yields $\bar{x} = 132$ and $s = 8$. Assuming normality, construct a **99% two-sided confidence interval** for the true mean reduction in BP. Use $t_{0.005,\,15} = 2.947$. (7 marks)

**(b)** A pharmaceutical company claims the mean plasma concentration of a drug is 50 ng/mL. A lab draws a sample of $n = 49$ measurements, obtaining $\bar{x} = 48.2$ ng/mL with $\sigma = 7$ ng/mL (assumed known). Test the claim at the 5% level by constructing a 95% CI. Decide whether the claim is supported. (7 marks)

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Question B

**(a) Solution:**

Given: $n = 16$, $\bar{x} = 132$, $s = 8$, $1-\alpha = 0.99$, $\alpha/2 = 0.005$, $t_{0.005,\,15} = 2.947$.

Use the **$t$-procedure** with $n-1 = 15$ degrees of freedom.

Standard error:

$$SE = \frac{s}{\sqrt{n}} = \frac{8}{\sqrt{16}} = \frac{8}{4} = 2$$

Margin of error:

$$E = 2.947 \times 2 = 5.894$$

Confidence limits:

$$L = 132 - 5.894 = 126.106$$

$$U = 132 + 5.894 = 138.894$$

$$\boxed{\;(126.11,\; 138.89)\;\text{mmHg}\;}$$

**Interpretation:** We are 99% confident that the true mean systolic BP of patients on the drug lies between **126.11 and 138.89 mmHg**. (7 marks)

**(b) Solution:**

Given: $n = 49$, $\bar{x} = 48.2$, $\sigma = 7$ (known), $1-\alpha = 0.95$, $z_{0.025} = 1.96$.

Since $\sigma$ is known and $n = 49 \ge 30$, use the **Z-procedure**.

Standard error:

$$SE = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} = \frac{7}{\sqrt{49}} = \frac{7}{7} = 1$$

Margin of error:

$$E = 1.96 \times 1 = 1.96$$

Confidence limits:

$$L = 48.2 - 1.96 = 46.24$$

$$U = 48.2 + 1.96 = 50.16$$

$$\boxed{\;(46.24,\; 50.16)\;\text{ng/mL}\;}$$

**Decision Rule:** If the claimed value $\mu_0 = 50$ lies **inside** the CI, the data does not contradict the claim; if it lies **outside**, the claim is rejected at the 5% level.

Since $50 \in (46.24,\, 50.16)$:

**Conclusion:** The value 50 ng/mL **lies inside** the 95% CI. Therefore, at the 5% significance level, we **do not reject** the company's claim that the true mean plasma concentration is 50 ng/mL. (7 marks)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Easy Marks:**
>
> 1. **Wrong distribution choice** — Using the $Z$-formula when $\sigma$ is unknown and $n < 30$. KTU examiners award *zero* marks for the final interval if the wrong pivot is used. Always check: "Is $\sigma$ given? Is $n \ge 30$?"
>
> 2. **Wrong degrees of freedom** — For the $t$-procedure, d.f. $= n - 1$, not $n$. A single digit error here cascades through the entire answer.
>
> 3. **Forgetting to divide by 2 in the tails** — When $\alpha = 0.05$, the critical value is $z_{0.025}$, *not* $z_{0.05}$. Writing $z_{0.05}$ in the margin of error formula loses 2 marks.
>
> 4. **Interpretation error** — Writing *"the probability that $\mu$ is in this interval is 0.95"* is **wrong**. The correct frequentist interpretation involves *long-run repeated sampling*, not a probability about a fixed parameter.
>
> 5. **Sign error in algebraic inversion** — When isolating $\mu$ in the pivotal inequality, multiplying by $-1$ flips the inequality signs. Careless handling here produces $L > U$, an instant red flag.
>
> 6. **Rounding too early** — Round only the *final* interval limits to 2 decimals; intermediate values like $SE$ should be kept to at least 4 decimals until the final step.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this list for the night before the exam. Every point below is a potential KTU question trigger.**

- [x] A two-sided CI for $\mu$ has the form $\bar{X} \pm E$, where $E$ is the **margin of error**.
- [x] **Use $Z$** when $\sigma^2$ is known, or when $n \ge 30$ (CLT) even if $\sigma^2$ is unknown (use $S$ as a plug-in).
- [x] **Use $t$** when $\sigma^2$ is unknown, $X$ is normal, and $n < 30$. Degrees of freedom $= n-1$.
- [x] Standard error $SE = \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$ (or $\dfrac{S}{\sqrt{n}}$). It shrinks at rate $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{n}}$.
- [x] Quadrupling the sample size **halves** the CI width (all else equal).
- [x] Increasing the confidence level **widens** the CI; decreasing it **narrows** the CI.
- [x] The frequentist interpretation of a 95% CI: *"If we repeat the sampling many times, about 95% of the constructed intervals will contain $\mu$."*
- [x] For sample-size calculation: $n = \left(\dfrac{z_{\alpha/2}\cdot \sigma}{E}\right)^{2}$, rounded **up**.
- [x] Standard critical values to memorize: $z_{0.05} = 1.645$, $z_{0.025} = 1.96$, $z_{0.005} = 2.576$.
- [x] CI and two-sided hypothesis test are *dual*: a $(1-\alpha)$ CI will contain $\mu_0$ if and only if the two-sided test at level $\alpha$ **fails to reject** $H_0: \mu = \mu_0$.

---

*End of Module 2 Notes — Interval Estimation of Population Mean (Two Sided CI).*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: One-Sided Lower and Upper Confidence Intervals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GDMAT401 - MATHEMATICS FOR LIFE SCIENCE – 4
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** One-Sided Lower and Upper Confidence Intervals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In statistical inference, a **One-Sided Confidence Interval** is an interval of the form $(L, \infty)$ or $(-\infty, U)$ that contains the true value of an unknown population parameter $\theta$ with a specified level of confidence $1 - \alpha$. Unlike a two-sided confidence interval, a one-sided interval provides only a single bound (either a lower bound or an upper bound) on the parameter.

- A **One-Sided Lower Confidence Bound** for a parameter $\theta$ at confidence level $1-\alpha$ is a statistic $L$ such that:

$$P(\theta \geq L) = 1 - \alpha$$

- A **One-Sided Upper Confidence Bound** for a parameter $\theta$ at confidence level $1-\alpha$ is a statistic $U$ such that:

$$P(\theta \leq U) = 1 - \alpha$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction from Two-Sided Intervals:** In a two-sided interval, the confidence level $1-\alpha$ is split equally between both tails (i.e., $\alpha/2$ on each side). In a **one-sided interval**, the *entire* confidence level $1-\alpha$ is concentrated in **one tail only**, making the bound tighter and more informative for directional inference.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Minimum Guarantee Price"**
>
> Imagine a farmer who wants to sell his harvest *before* the season. He asks an agricultural scientist: *"What is the minimum yield I can expect with 95% confidence?"* The scientist doesn't need to give a *range* (e.g., 80–120 quintals); the farmer only needs a **lower bound** — a *floor* below which the yield is almost certainly not going to fall. This floor is a **Lower Confidence Bound**.
>
> Conversely, a pharmaceutical regulator might ask: *"What is the maximum safe dosage of Drug X that 99% of the population can tolerate without side effects?"* The answer — a single *ceiling* — is an **Upper Confidence Bound**.

In both cases, we are **only interested in one direction** of uncertainty — either how *low* or how *high* the parameter can plausibly be. This directional focus lets us put **all our confidence** on that one side, producing a sharper bound.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Notation

The following symbols and constants are used throughout KTU 2024 Scheme assessments:

| Symbol | Meaning |
|--------|---------|
| $\mu$ | Population mean (parameter being estimated) |
| $\bar{X}$ | Sample mean (point estimator) |
| $\sigma$ | Population standard deviation (assumed known) |
| $s$ | Sample standard deviation (estimated from data) |
| $n$ | Sample size |
| $1-\alpha$ | Confidence level (e.g., **0.90**, **0.95**, **0.99**) |
| $z_\alpha$ | Standard normal critical value at level $\alpha$ |
| $t_{\alpha, n-1}$ | Student's $t$-critical value with $n-1$ degrees of freedom |
| $L$ | Lower confidence bound |
| $U$ | Upper confidence bound |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Convention:** In KTU examinations, when $n \geq 30$ and $\sigma$ is known, the **standard normal (Z) distribution** is used. When $\sigma$ is unknown, the **Student's t-distribution** is used regardless of sample size. When $n < 30$ and $\sigma$ is known, Z is still used; if $\sigma$ unknown, **t-distribution is mandatory**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — The "Why" Behind One-Sided Intervals

The construction of confidence intervals is rooted in the **sampling distribution** of a point estimator. For the sample mean $\bar{X}$:

- If $X_i \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$, then $\bar{X} \sim N\left(\mu, \frac{\sigma^2}{n}\right)$
- The standardized form is the **pivot**: $Z = \dfrac{\bar{X} - \mu}{\sigma/\sqrt{n}} \sim N(0,1)$

A **pivot** is a function of both the data and the unknown parameter whose distribution does **not** depend on the parameter. From this pivot, we algebraically rearrange to isolate $\mu$ on one side of an inequality — yielding either a lower or upper bound.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Construction

**Lower Confidence Bound Construction:**

- Start with the probabilistic statement: $P(Z \leq z_{1-\alpha}) = 1 - \alpha$ where $z_{1-\alpha} = -z_\alpha$
- Substitute the pivot: $P\left(\dfrac{\bar{X} - \mu}{\sigma/\sqrt{n}} \leq z_{1-\alpha}\right) = 1 - \alpha$
- Rearrange: $P\left(\bar{X} - z_{1-\alpha}\cdot\dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} \leq \mu\right) = 1 - \alpha$
- Recognize the form: $L = \bar{X} - z_{1-\alpha}\cdot\dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} = \bar{X} + z_\alpha\cdot\dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$ (since $z_{1-\alpha} = -z_\alpha$)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this works:** We placed $\mu$ on the *right-hand side* of the inequality. The expression on the *left-hand side* is purely a function of the data — it is a statistic, call it $L$. The probability statement now reads $P(L \leq \mu) = 1-\alpha$, which is precisely the definition of a lower confidence bound.

**Upper Confidence Bound Construction:**

- Start with: $P(Z \leq z_{1-\alpha}) = 1 - \alpha$ — same as above
- Substitute: $P\left(\mu \leq \bar{X} + z_{1-\alpha}\cdot\dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}\right) = 1 - \alpha$
- Define: $U = \bar{X} + z_{1-\alpha}\cdot\dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} = \bar{X} - z_\alpha\cdot\dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$

### 2.3 Critical Comparison: One-Sided vs Two-Sided

| Property | Two-Sided CI | One-Sided CI |
|----------|--------------|--------------|
| **Form** | $(L, U)$ | $(L, \infty)$ or $(-\infty, U)$ |
| **Confidence Level** | $1-\alpha$ | $1-\alpha$ |
| **Tail Probability** | $\alpha/2$ on each side | $\alpha$ on one side only |
| **Critical Value** | $z_{\alpha/2}$ | $z_\alpha$ |
| **Width** | Wider (less precise) | Narrower / more informative on one side |
| **Use Case** | Bidirectional uncertainty | Directional (engineering tolerance) inference |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Students often use $z_{\alpha/2}$ in one-sided formulas because they confuse it with two-sided intervals. **The correct critical value for a one-sided interval at confidence level $1-\alpha$ is $z_\alpha$, NOT $z_{\alpha/2}$**. This is a top mark-deduction point in KTU valuations.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas required for KTU 2024 Scheme Module 2 — One-Sided Confidence Intervals**

**CASE 1: Population Mean $\mu$, Variance $\sigma^2$ KNOWN (Large Sample or Known $\sigma$)**

| Bound Type | Formula | Pivot |
|------------|---------|-------|
| Lower Bound $L$ | $L = \bar{X} - z_\alpha \cdot \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$ | $Z = \dfrac{\bar{X}-\mu}{\sigma/\sqrt{n}}$ |
| Upper Bound $U$ | $U = \bar{X} + z_\alpha \cdot \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$ | Same as above |

**CASE 2: Population Mean $\mu$, Variance $\sigma^2$ UNKNOWN (Small Sample)**

| Bound Type | Formula | Pivot |
|------------|---------|-------|
| Lower Bound $L$ | $L = \bar{X} - t_{\alpha,\,n-1} \cdot \dfrac{s}{\sqrt{n}}$ | $T = \dfrac{\bar{X}-\mu}{s/\sqrt{n}}$ |
| Upper Bound $U$ | $U = \bar{X} + t_{\alpha,\,n-1} \cdot \dfrac{s}{\sqrt{n}}$ | Same as above |

**CASE 3: Population Proportion $p$ (Large Sample, Normal Approximation)**

| Bound Type | Formula | Pivot |
|------------|---------|-------|
| Lower Bound $L$ | $L = \hat{p} - z_\alpha \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\hat{p}(1-\hat{p})}{n}}$ | $Z = \dfrac{\hat{p}-p}{\sqrt{\hat{p}(1-\hat{p})/n}}$ |
| Upper Bound $U$ | $U = \hat{p} + z_\alpha \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\hat{p}(1-\hat{p})}{n}}$ | Same as above |

**Critical Values Table (Most Common in KTU Exams):**

| Confidence Level $1-\alpha$ | $\alpha$ | $z_\alpha$ (one-sided) | $z_{\alpha/2}$ (two-sided) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **0.90** | 0.10 | 1.282 | 1.645 |
| **0.95** | 0.05 | 1.645 | 1.960 |
| **0.99** | 0.01 | 2.326 | 2.576 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Normal Identity Used:** $z_{1-\alpha} = -z_\alpha$. This is why $\bar{X} - z_{1-\alpha}\cdot\sigma/\sqrt{n} = \bar{X} + z_\alpha\cdot\sigma/\sqrt{n}$.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering and Life Science Utility

One-sided confidence intervals are **extensively used** in:

- **Pharmaceutical Engineering:** Establishing **lower confidence bounds on drug efficacy** (must exceed a regulatory minimum).
- **Quality Control Manufacturing:** Setting **upper confidence bounds on defect rates** (e.g., "we are 95% confident the defect rate is at most 2%").
- **Civil Engineering:** Computing **lower confidence bounds on concrete compressive strength** to ensure structural safety.
- **Environmental Science:** Establishing **upper confidence bounds on pollutant concentrations** for regulatory compliance.
- **Reliability Engineering:** Constructing **lower confidence bounds on mean time to failure (MTTF)** of components.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Lower Confidence Bound for $\mu$ (Known $\sigma$)

**Given:** $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n$ is a random sample from $N(\mu, \sigma^2)$ with $\sigma$ known.

**Step 1:** Define the sample mean:

$$\bar{X} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i$$

By the properties of the normal distribution, we have:

$$\bar{X} \sim N\left(\mu, \frac{\sigma^2}{n}\right)$$

**Step 2:** Construct a standard normal pivot. Subtract $\mu$ and divide by the standard error:

$$Z = \frac{\bar{X} - \mu}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}} \sim N(0, 1)$$

This $Z$ is a **pivot** — its distribution does not depend on $\mu$.

**Step 3:** Apply the definition of the standard normal quantile. For confidence level $1-\alpha$:

$$P(Z \leq z_{1-\alpha}) = 1 - \alpha$$

Using the symmetry property $z_{1-\alpha} = -z_\alpha$:

$$P(Z \leq -z_\alpha) = 1 - \alpha$$

**Step 4:** Substitute the pivot expression back:

$$P\left(\frac{\bar{X} - \mu}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}} \leq -z_\alpha\right) = 1 - \alpha$$

**Step 5:** Multiply both sides of the inequality by $\sigma / \sqrt{n}$ (positive quantity, so inequality direction is preserved):

$$P\left(\bar{X} - \mu \leq -z_\alpha \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}\right) = 1 - \alpha$$

**Step 6:** Multiply through by $-1$ (this **reverses** the inequality direction):

$$P\left(\mu - \bar{X} \geq z_\alpha \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}\right) = 1 - \alpha$$

**Step 7:** Rearrange to isolate $\mu$:

$$P\left(\mu \geq \bar{X} - z_\alpha \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}\right) = 1 - \alpha$$

**Step 8:** Define the lower confidence bound:

$$L = \bar{X} - z_\alpha \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$$

**Result:**

$$\boxed{P(\mu \geq L) = 1 - \alpha \quad \text{where} \quad L = \bar{X} - z_\alpha \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}}$$

The lower confidence interval is $(L, \infty)$.

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Upper Confidence Bound for $\mu$ (Known $\sigma$)

**Step 1:** Begin with the same pivot:

$$Z = \frac{\bar{X} - \mu}{\sigma/\sqrt{n}} \sim N(0,1)$$

**Step 2:** Apply the quantile definition for the *upper* tail. We want the *upper* bound, so we need:

$$P(Z \geq -z_\alpha) = 1 - \alpha$$

This is equivalent to $P(Z \leq z_\alpha) = 1 - \alpha$? No — actually $P(Z \leq z_{1-\alpha}) = 1-\alpha$ gives the upper-side statement we need. Let us be precise:

$$P(Z \leq z_{1-\alpha}) = 1 - \alpha$$

Since $z_{1-\alpha} = -z_\alpha$:

$$P\left(\frac{\bar{X} - \mu}{\sigma/\sqrt{n}} \leq -z_\alpha\right) = 1 - \alpha$$

**Step 3:** Multiply by $\sigma/\sqrt{n}$:

$$P\left(\bar{X} - \mu \leq -z_\alpha \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}\right) = 1 - \alpha$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $\mu$:

$$P\left(\mu \geq \bar{X} + z_\alpha \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}\right) = 1 - \alpha$$

**Wait — that gives a lower bound again.** Let us correct this with a clearer derivation.

**Corrected Derivation for Upper Bound:**

We want a quantity $U$ such that $P(\mu \leq U) = 1-\alpha$.

Begin with the pivot:

$$P\left(\frac{\bar{X} - \mu}{\sigma/\sqrt{n}} \geq -z_\alpha\right) = 1 - \alpha$$

Since $P(Z \geq -z_\alpha) = 1 - P(Z < -z_\alpha) = 1 - \alpha$ requires that $-z_\alpha = z_{1-\alpha}$, we use the quantile directly:

$$P\left(\frac{\bar{X} - \mu}{\sigma/\sqrt{n}} \leq z_{1-\alpha}\right) = 1 - \alpha$$

Note: $z_{1-\alpha} = -z_\alpha$ is **negative** for standard $\alpha < 0.5$.

**Step 3 (corrected):** Multiply by $\sigma/\sqrt{n}$:

$$P\left(\bar{X} - \mu \leq -z_\alpha \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}\right) = 1 - \alpha$$

**Step 4 (corrected):** Add $\mu$ and rearrange to isolate $\mu$ on the *left* side (since we want an upper bound on $\mu$):

$$P\left(\mu \geq \bar{X} + z_\alpha \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}\right) = 1 - \alpha$$

This gives a **lower** bound, not upper. The correct approach is:

**Truly Corrected Derivation:**

We use: $P(Z \leq z_\alpha) = 1 - \alpha$ (this is the standard property of the right tail).

$$P\left(\frac{\bar{X} - \mu}{\sigma/\sqrt{n}} \leq z_\alpha\right) = 1 - \alpha$$

Multiply by $\sigma/\sqrt{n}$:

$$P\left(\bar{X} - \mu \leq z_\alpha \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}\right) = 1 - \alpha$$

Subtract $\bar{X}$ from both sides and multiply by $-1$:

$$P\left(\mu \geq \bar{X} - z_\alpha \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}\right) = 1 - \alpha$$

Hmm, this again gives a lower bound. The issue is subtle — let me restart cleanly:

---

### 3.2 (Final Correct) Derivation: Upper Confidence Bound for $\mu$

**Goal:** Find $U$ such that $P(\mu \leq U) = 1 - \alpha$.

**Step 1:** Start from:

$$P\left(\frac{\bar{X} - \mu}{\sigma/\sqrt{n}} \leq z_\alpha\right) = 1 - \alpha$$

**Step 2:** Multiply by $\sigma/\sqrt{n}$:

$$P\left(\bar{X} - \mu \leq z_\alpha \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}\right) = 1 - \alpha$$

**Step 3:** Add $\mu$ to both sides and subtract $z_\alpha \cdot \sigma/\sqrt{n}$:

$$P\left(\bar{X} - z_\alpha \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} \leq \mu\right) = 1 - \alpha$$

This is $P(L \leq \mu) = 1-\alpha$ where $L = \bar{X} - z_\alpha \cdot \sigma/\sqrt{n}$ — a **lower bound** on $\mu$.

**For Upper Bound**, we need a different inequality direction. Use:

$$P\left(\frac{\bar{X} - \mu}{\sigma/\sqrt{n}} \leq -z_\alpha\right) = 1 - \alpha \quad \text{(this gives an upper bound)}$$

Wait — actually, $P(Z \leq -z_\alpha) = \alpha$, not $1-\alpha$. Let me use the correct relationship:

$$P(Z \geq -z_\alpha) = 1 - P(Z < -z_\alpha) = 1 - \alpha$$

So:

$$P\left(\frac{\bar{X} - \mu}{\sigma/\sqrt{n}} \geq -z_\alpha\right) = 1 - \alpha$$

**Step 2:** Multiply by $\sigma/\sqrt{n}$ (positive):

$$P\left(\bar{X} - \mu \geq -z_\alpha \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}\right) = 1 - \alpha$$

**Step 3:** Subtract $\bar{X}$:

$$P\left(- \mu \geq -z_\alpha \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} - \bar{X}\right) = 1 - \alpha$$

**Step 4:** Multiply by $-1$ (reverses inequality):

$$P\left(\mu \leq z_\alpha \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} + \bar{X}\right) = 1 - \alpha$$

**Step 5:** Define the upper confidence bound:

$$U = \bar{X} + z_\alpha \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$$

**Result:**

$$\boxed{P(\mu \leq U) = 1 - \alpha \quad \text{where} \quad U = \bar{X} + z_\alpha \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}}$$

The upper confidence interval is $(-\infty, U)$.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Examples

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example 1: Lower Bound (Known Variance)**

**Problem:** A pharmaceutical company measures the concentration of an active ingredient in 25 randomly selected tablets. The sample mean is $\bar{x} = 98.5$ mg. The standard deviation is known to be $\sigma = 2.0$ mg. Construct a 95% **lower confidence bound** for the true mean concentration $\mu$.

**Solution:**

Given: $\bar{x} = 98.5$, $\sigma = 2.0$, $n = 25$, confidence level = $0.95$, so $\alpha = 0.05$.

**Step 1:** Identify the appropriate critical value. For one-sided 95% confidence: $z_{0.05} = 1.645$.

**Step 2:** Compute the standard error:

$$SE = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} = \frac{2.0}{\sqrt{25}} = \frac{2.0}{5} = 0.4$$

**Step 3:** Compute the margin:

$$E = z_\alpha \cdot SE = 1.645 \times 0.4 = 0.658$$

**Step 4:** Lower bound:

$$L = \bar{x} - z_\alpha \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} = 98.5 - 1.645 \times 0.4 = 98.5 - 0.658 = 97.842$$

**Step 5:** Interpretation:

$$\boxed{L = 97.842 \text{ mg, so the 95\% lower confidence interval is } (97.842, \infty)}$$

We are 95% confident that the true mean concentration is at least 97.842 mg.

---

**Worked Example 2: Upper Bound (Unknown Variance, Small Sample)**

**Problem:** A clinical lab measures the systolic blood pressure of 16 patients. The sample mean is $\bar{x} = 142$ mmHg and the sample standard deviation is $s = 8$ mmHg. Construct a 99% **upper confidence bound** for the true mean blood pressure $\mu$.

**Solution:**

Given: $\bar{x} = 142$, $s = 8$, $n = 16$, confidence level = $0.99$, so $\alpha = 0.01$.

**Step 1:** Identify the critical value. Since $\sigma$ is unknown and $n < 30$, use $t$-distribution with $n-1 = 15$ degrees of freedom:

$$t_{0.01, 15} = 2.602$$

**Step 2:** Compute the standard error:

$$SE = \frac{s}{\sqrt{n}} = \frac{8}{\sqrt{16}} = \frac{8}{4} = 2$$

**Step 3:** Compute the margin:

$$E = t_{\alpha, n-1} \cdot SE = 2.602 \times 2 = 5.204$$

**Step 4:** Upper bound:

$$U = \bar{x} + t_{\alpha, n-1} \cdot \frac{s}{\sqrt{n}} = 142 + 5.204 = 147.204$$

**Step 5:** Interpretation:

$$\boxed{U = 147.204 \text{ mmHg, so the 99\% upper confidence interval is } (-\infty, 147.204)}$$

We are 99% confident that the true mean systolic blood pressure is at most 147.204 mmHg.

---

**Worked Example 3: Proportion — Lower Bound**

**Problem:** In a clinical trial of 200 patients, a new treatment was successful in 160 cases. Find a 95% lower confidence bound for the true success proportion $p$.

**Solution:**

Given: $\hat{p} = 160/200 = 0.80$, $n = 200$, $1-\alpha = 0.95$, so $\alpha = 0.05$.

**Step 1:** Critical value: $z_{0.05} = 1.645$.

**Step 2:** Standard error:

$$SE = \sqrt{\frac{\hat{p}(1-\hat{p})}{n}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.80 \times 0.20}{200}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.16}{200}} = \sqrt{0.0008} = 0.02828$$

**Step 3:** Lower bound:

$$L = \hat{p} - z_\alpha \cdot SE = 0.80 - 1.645 \times 0.02828 = 0.80 - 0.04653 = 0.75347$$

**Step 4:** Interpretation:

$$\boxed{L = 0.7535 \text{, so the 95\% lower confidence interval is } (0.7535, 1)}$$

We are 95% confident that the true success rate is at least 75.35%.

---

### 3.4 Effect of Sample Size — Analytical Insight

For a fixed confidence level $1-\alpha$ and known $\sigma$:

$$\text{Margin} = z_\alpha \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$$

- As $n$ increases, $\sqrt{n}$ increases, so the margin **decreases**.
- The bound becomes **tighter** (more informative) with larger $n$.
- The relationship is **inverse-square-root**: to halve the margin, we must **quadruple** the sample size.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sample Size Formula for One-Sided Bound (KTU Favorite):**
>
> Given a desired margin $E$ and confidence level $1-\alpha$:
>
> $$n = \left(\frac{z_\alpha \cdot \sigma}{E}\right)^2$$
>
> Always round **up** to the next integer.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Flow — Construction of One-Sided Confidence Intervals

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Sample Data X1, X2, ..., Xn"] --> B{"Is sigma known?"}
    B -- "Yes" --> C["Use Z-distribution"]
    B -- "No" --> D["Use t-distribution with n-1 df"]
    C --> E{"Is lower or upper bound?"}
    D --> E
    E -- "Lower Bound" --> F["L = xbar - z_alpha or t_alpha times sigma or s over sqrt of n"]
    E -- "Upper Bound" --> G["U = xbar + z_alpha or t_alpha times sigma or s over sqrt of n"]
    F --> H["Lower CI is L to infinity"]
    G --> I["Upper CI is minus infinity to U"]
    H --> J["Interpret: 1 minus alpha confidence that mu is at least L"]
    I --> K["Interpret: 1 minus alpha confidence that mu is at most U"]
```

### 4.2 Comparison Block Diagram — One-Sided vs Two-Sided

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TS["TWO-SIDED CI"]
        TS1["alpha or 2 in left tail"] --> TS2["L"] --> TS3["Point Estimate xbar"] --> TS4["U"] --> TS5["alpha or 2 in right tail"]
    end
    subgraph LS["LOWER ONE-SIDED CI"]
        LS1["alpha in left tail"] --> LS2["L"] --> LS3["xbar"] --> LS4["Infinity"]
    end
    subgraph US["UPPER ONE-SIDED CI"]
        US1["Minus Infinity"] --> US2["xbar"] --> US3["U"] --> US4["alpha in right tail"]
    end
```

### 4.3 Decision Tree — Which Distribution and Bound to Use

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1["What are you estimating?"] --> Q2a["Population mean mu"]
    Q1 --> Q2b["Population proportion p"]
    Q2a --> Q3a{"Is sigma known?"}
    Q3a -- "Yes" --> Q3b["Z-distribution"]
    Q3a -- "No" --> Q3c["t-distribution, df = n-1"]
    Q2b --> Q4a["Use Z-distribution, n times p greater than 5 and n times 1-p greater than 5"]
    Q3b --> Q5["Compute L or U using z_alpha"]
    Q3c --> Q5
    Q4a --> Q5
    Q5 --> Q6["State the one-sided confidence interval and interpret"]
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Confidence Interval Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1["Input Layer: Raw Sample Data"] --> A2["Compute sample mean xbar and sample sd s"]
    A2 --> A3["Choose Distribution Module: Z or t"]
    A3 --> A4["Select Critical Value Module: z_alpha or t_alpha, n-1"]
    A4 --> A5["Compute Standard Error Module: sigma or s over sqrt n"]
    A5 --> A6["Apply Bound Formula Module: L = xbar - cv times SE OR U = xbar + cv times SE"]
    A6 --> A7["Output: One-Sided Confidence Interval"]
    A7 --> A8["Interpretation Module: P of mu greater than L equals 1 minus alpha OR P of mu less than U equals 1 minus alpha"]
```
