> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** GDMAT401 - MATHEMATICS FOR LIFE SCIENCE – 4 > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Multiple Regression (for Two Independent Variables Only) ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Multiple Regression** is a statistical modeling technique that examines the linear relationship between one dependent (response) variable and **two or more independent (predictor) variables** simultaneously. When restricted to exactly two independent variables, the model is called the **Multiple Linear Regression Model with Two Predictors** (also referred to as a *bivariate multiple regression* or *first-order multiple regression model with two regressors*). The **population regression model** is written as: $$ Y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_{1i} + \beta_2 X_{2i} + \varepsilon_i $$ where the subscript $i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$ indexes the $i$-th observation, and the parameters are defined as: - $Y_i$ = the **dependent (response) variable** for the $i$-th observation. - $X_{1i}, X_{2i}$ = the **two independent (predictor / explanatory) variables**. - $\beta_0$ = the **intercept** (expected value of $Y$ when $X_1 = 0$ and $X_2 = 0$). - $\beta_1$ = the **partial regression coefficient** of $Y$ on $X_1$ (holding $X_2$ constant); represents the expected change in $Y$ for a **one-unit increase in $X_1$ while $X_2$ is held fixed**. - $\beta_2$ = the **partial regression coefficient** of $Y$ on $X_2$ (holding $X_1$ constant). - $\varepsilon_i$ = the **random error term**, assumed to satisfy the classical *Gauss–Markov* assumptions: $\varepsilon_i \sim N(0, \sigma^2)$ and are pairwise uncorrelated. The corresponding **estimated (fitted) regression equation** based on sample data is: $$ \hat{Y}_i = b_0 + b_1 X_{1i} + b_2 X_{2i} $$ where $b_0, b_1, b_2$ are the **least-squares estimators** of $\beta_0, \beta_1, \beta_2$ respectively, and $\hat{Y}_i$ is the predicted value of $Y_i$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mandate:** Only the **two-independent-variable case** is in scope. Higher-order models, polynomial regression, and multi-collinearity diagnostics beyond what is required for two predictors are **not** part of this module. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Recipe" Intuition Imagine you are a **nutritionist** trying to predict a person's daily **caloric intake** ($Y$). A single variable like "time spent exercising" ($X_1$) is informative but incomplete. Adding a second variable like "hours of sleep" ($X_2$) refines the prediction further. Multiple regression is the statistical "recipe" that: 1. **Combines** both ingredients ($X_1, X_2$) in a weighted fashion. 2. The **weights** ($b_1, b_2$) tell you *how much each factor matters once the other is already known* — this is the "partial" idea. 3. The **intercept** $b_0$ is the baseline prediction when both predictors are zero. > [!NOTE] > **Why not just do two simple regressions?** > Simple regression $Y$ vs. $X_1$ and $Y$ vs. $X_2$ ignore the **shared variance** between $X_1$ and $X_2$. Multiple regression *partialled out* the influence of one predictor before assessing the other — giving you the *unique* contribution of each. ### 1.3 Standard Statistical Quantities in the Model | Symbol | Meaning | Typical Units (Life-Science context) | |---|---|---| | $n$ | Sample size (number of observations) | dimensionless | | $R^2$ | Coefficient of **multiple determination** | dimensionless, in $[0, 1]$ | | $R^2_{\text{adj}}$ | Adjusted $R^2$ | dimensionless, in $(-\infty, 1]$ | | $\text{SE}(b_j)$ | Standard error of estimator $b_j$ | units of $Y$ per unit of $X_j$ | | $F$ | Overall F-statistic for model significance | dimensionless | | $p$-value | Probability of observing data as extreme under $H_0$ | dimensionless, in $[0, 1]$ | > [!NOTE] > The benchmarks $\alpha = 0.05$ and $\alpha = 0.01$ are the **standard significance levels** used in KTU board examinations to declare a regression coefficient or the overall model as statistically significant. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Least-Squares Principle (Why we minimize SSE) The estimators $b_0, b_1, b_2$ are obtained by **minimizing the Sum of Squared Errors (SSE)**: $$ \text{SSE} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( Y_i - \hat{Y}_i \right)^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( Y_i - b_0 - b_1 X_{1i} - b_2 X_{2i} \right)^2 $$ Differentiating $\text{SSE}$ partially with respect to $b_0, b_1, b_2$ and equating to zero yields the **three normal equations**. ### 2.2 The Three Normal Equations (Klemera's Form) The KTU board examination almost always tests the **normal-equation system** in this exact form. For data with $n$ observations: $$ \begin{aligned} \sum Y_i &= n \, b_0 + b_1 \sum X_{1i} + b_2 \sum X_{2i} \\ \sum X_{1i} Y_i &= b_0 \sum X_{1i} + b_1 \sum X_{1i}^2 + b_2 \sum X_{1i} X_{2i} \\ \sum X_{2i} Y_i &= b_0 \sum X_{2i} + b_1 \sum X_{1i} X_{2i} + b_2 \sum X_{2i}^2 \end{aligned} $$ Solving this $3 \times 3$ linear system (typically by Cramer's rule, matrix inversion, or Gaussian elimination) gives $b_0, b_1, b_2$. ### 2.3 Matrix Formulation (Linear Algebra View) The model in vector–matrix form is $\mathbf{Y} = \mathbf{X} \boldsymbol{\beta} + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}$, where $$ \mathbf{Y} = \begin{pmatrix} Y_1 \\ Y_2 \\ \vdots \\ Y_n \end{pmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{X} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & X_{11} & X_{21} \\ 1 & X_{12} & X_{22} \\ \vdots & \vdots & \vdots \\ 1 & X_{1n} & X_{2n} \end{pmatrix}, \quad \boldsymbol{\beta} = \begin{pmatrix} b_0 \\ b_1 \\ b_2 \end{pmatrix} $$ The **normal-equation matrix** is $\mathbf{X}^{\top} \mathbf{X}$, and the **least-squares estimator** is: $$ \mathbf{b} = (\mathbf{X}^{\top} \mathbf{X})^{-1} \mathbf{X}^{\top} \mathbf{Y} $$ ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Exam Tip:** All formulas below are **board-exam high-yield**. Memorize the symbols and meanings — the derivation methodology (Section 3) is equally important. | # | Concept | Formula | Symbol Glossary | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Population model | $Y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_{1i} + \beta_2 X_{2i} + \varepsilon_i$ | $\beta_j$ true coefficients, $\varepsilon_i$ error | | 2 | Fitted model | $\hat{Y}_i = b_0 + b_1 X_{1i} + b_2 X_{2i}$ | $b_j$ estimated coefficients | | 3 | Residuals | $e_i = Y_i - \hat{Y}_i$ | $e_i$ residual | | 4 | Total Sum of Squares | $\text{SST} = \sum (Y_i - \bar{Y})^2$ | $\bar{Y}$ sample mean of $Y$ | | 5 | Regression Sum of Squares | $\text{SSR} = \sum (\hat{Y}_i - \bar{Y})^2$ | measures explained variation | | 6 | Error Sum of Squares | $\text{SSE} = \sum (Y_i - \hat{Y}_i)^2$ | unexplained variation | | 7 | SST identity | $\text{SST} = \text{SSR} + \text{SSE}$ | always holds | | 8 | Coefficient of multiple determination | $R^2 = \dfrac{\text{SSR}}{\text{SST}} = 1 - \dfrac{\text{SSE}}{\text{SST}}$ | $0 \le R^2 \le 1$ | | 9 | Adjusted $R^2$ | $R^2_{\text{adj}} = 1 - \dfrac{(1 - R^2)(n - 1)}{n - k - 1}$ | $k = 2$ (predictors); penalizes adding useless variables | | 10 | Standard error of estimate | $s_e = \sqrt{\dfrac{\text{SSE}}{n - k - 1}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\text{SSE}}{n - 3}}$ | denominator = residual df | | 11 | Overall F-statistic | $F = \dfrac{\text{SSR} \,/\, k}{\text{SSE} \,/\, (n - k - 1)}$ | $H_0: \beta_1 = \beta_2 = 0$ | | 12 | Standard error of $b_j$ | $\text{SE}(b_j) = s_e \sqrt{c_{jj}}$ | $c_{jj} = j$-th diagonal element of $(\mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{X})^{-1}$ | | 13 | t-statistic for $b_j$ | $t_j = \dfrac{b_j}{\text{SE}(b_j)}$ | $H_0: \beta_j = 0$ | | 14 | Degrees of freedom (residual) | $\text{df}_{\text{res}} = n - 3$ | 3 parameters estimated | ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering / Life-Science Utility In the **KTU 2024 Life-Science Mathematics** context, multiple regression with two predictors is the workhorse tool for: - **Pharmacokinetics:** Modeling blood-plasma drug concentration $Y$ as a function of dose $X_1$ and patient body-mass index $X_2$. - **Ecology:** Predicting species abundance $Y$ from rainfall $X_1$ and average temperature $X_2$. - **Bio-medical engineering:** Predicting heart rate $Y$ from exercise intensity $X_1$ and oxygen saturation $X_2$. - **Agricultural science:** Forecasting crop yield $Y$ from fertilizer amount $X_1$ and irrigation volume $X_2$. The **partial coefficients** are particularly valued because they answer the question: *"Holding all other factors constant, what is the isolated effect of one factor?"* — a question simple regression cannot answer. > [!IMPORTANT] > Always state the **Gauss–Markov assumptions** explicitly when writing a model: linearity, independence, homoscedasticity (constant $\sigma^2$), and normality of errors. The **BLUE** (Best Linear Unbiased Estimator) property of the OLS estimators holds under these assumptions. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Normal Equations (Klemera's Method) **Step 1 — Define the objective function.** The least-squares criterion is to minimize SSE with respect to $b_0, b_1, b_2$: $$ S(b_0, b_1, b_2) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( Y_i - b_0 - b_1 X_{1i} - b_2 X_{2i} \right)^2 $$ **Step 2 — First-order conditions (set partial derivatives to zero).** $$ \frac{\partial S}{\partial b_0} = -2 \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( Y_i - b_0 - b_1 X_{1i} - b_2 X_{2i} \right) = 0 $$ $$ \frac{\partial S}{\partial b_1} = -2 \sum_{i=1}^{n} X_{1i} \left( Y_i - b_0 - b_1 X_{1i} - b_2 X_{2i} \right) = 0 $$ $$ \frac{\partial S}{\partial b_2} = -2 \sum_{i=1}^{n} X_{2i} \left( Y_i - b_0 - b_1 X_{1i} - b_2 X_{2i} \right) = 0 $$ **Step 3 — Distribute the summations and collect like terms.** The factor $-2$ is dropped (it cancels). Expanding each equation: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{(i)} \quad \sum Y_i - n b_0 - b_1 \sum X_{1i} - b_2 \sum X_{2i} &= 0 \\ \text{(ii)} \quad \sum X_{1i} Y_i - b_0 \sum X_{1i} - b_1 \sum X_{1i}^2 - b_2 \sum X_{1i} X_{2i} &= 0 \\ \text{(iii)} \quad \sum X_{2i} Y_i - b_0 \sum X_{2i} - b_1 \sum X_{1i} X_{2i} - b_2 \sum X_{2i}^2 &= 0 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Rearrange into the standard normal-equation form.** $$ \begin{aligned} n b_0 + b_1 \sum X_{1i} + b_2 \sum X_{2i} &= \sum Y_i \\ b_0 \sum X_{1i} + b_1 \sum X_{1i}^2 + b_2 \sum X_{1i} X_{2i} &= \sum X_{1i} Y_i \\ b_0 \sum X_{2i} + b_1 \sum X_{1i} X_{2i} + b_2 \sum X_{2i}^2 &= \sum X_{2i} Y_i \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > The **Klemera normal equations** are a $3 \times 3$ linear system. They can be solved by Cramer's rule (determinants), matrix inversion, or Gaussian elimination — all acceptable in KTU exams. ### 3.2 Derivation of the Coefficient of Multiple Determination ($R^2$) **Step 1 — Define the three sources of variation.** For any regression problem with the constraint that the fitted model has an intercept: $$ \text{SST} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (Y_i - \bar{Y})^2, \quad \text{SSR} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (\hat{Y}_i - \bar{Y})^2, \quad \text{SSE} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (Y_i - \hat{Y}_i)^2 $$ **Step 2 — Prove the identity $\text{SST} = \text{SSR} + \text{SSE}$.** Start with the algebraic identity: $$ Y_i - \bar{Y} = (Y_i - \hat{Y}_i) + (\hat{Y}_i - \bar{Y}) $$ Squaring both sides: $$ (Y_i - \bar{Y})^2 = (Y_i - \hat{Y}_i)^2 + (\hat{Y}_i - \bar{Y})^2 + 2(Y_i - \hat{Y}_i)(\hat{Y}_i - \bar{Y}) $$ Summing over $i = 1, \ldots, n$: $$ \sum (Y_i - \bar{Y})^2 = \sum (Y_i - \hat{Y}_i)^2 + \sum (\hat{Y}_i - \bar{Y})^2 + 2 \sum (Y_i - \hat{Y}_i)(\hat{Y}_i - \bar{Y}) $$ The **cross-term vanishes** under OLS because of the orthogonality property $\sum e_i \hat{Y}_i = 0$. Therefore: $$ \text{SST} = \text{SSE} + \text{SSR} \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \text{SST} = \text{SSR} + \text{SSE} $$ **Step 3 — Define $R^2$.** The fraction of variation in $Y$ explained by the regression is: $$ R^2 = \frac{\text{SSR}}{\text{SST}} = 1 - \frac{\text{SSE}}{\text{SST}} $$ ### 3.3 Derivation of the Overall F-Statistic **Step 1 — Mean squares.** $$ \text{MSR} = \frac{\text{SSR}}{k}, \qquad \text{MSE} = \frac{\text{SSE}}{n - k - 1} $$ where $k = 2$ for two predictors. **Step 2 — The F-ratio.** $$ F = \frac{\text{MSR}}{\text{MSE}} = \frac{\text{SSR}/k}{\text{SSE}/(n - k - 1)} $$ **Step 3 — Decision rule.** Under the null hypothesis $H_0: \beta_1 = \beta_2 = 0$, $F$ follows an $F$-distribution with $(k, n - k - 1)$ degrees of freedom. Reject $H_0$ if $F_{\text{computed}} > F_{\alpha, k, n-k-1}$, or equivalently if the $p$-value $< \alpha$. ### 3.4 Worked Example — Full Calculation (Numerical Demonstration) **Problem:** A researcher studies the effect of **study hours** ($X_1$) and **sleep hours** ($X_2$) on **exam score** ($Y$). The following data are collected: | $i$ | $X_{1i}$ (study) | $X_{2i}$ (sleep) | $Y_i$ (score) | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | 2 | 8 | 65 | | 2 | 3 | 7 | 70 | | 3 | 4 | 6 | 75 | | 4 | 5 | 7 | 80 | | 5 | 6 | 5 | 85 | **Step 1 — Compute the required summations.** $$ \begin{aligned} n &= 5 \\ \sum X_1 &= 2 + 3 + 4 + 5 + 6 = 20 \\ \sum X_2 &= 8 + 7 + 6 + 7 + 5 = 33 \\ \sum Y &= 65 + 70 + 75 + 80 + 85 = 375 \\ \sum X_1^2 &= 4 + 9 + 16 + 25 + 36 = 90 \\ \sum X_2^2 &= 64 + 49 + 36 + 49 + 25 = 223 \\ \sum X_1 X_2 &= (2)(8) + (3)(7) + (4)(6) + (5)(7) + (6)(5) = 16 + 21 + 24 + 35 + 30 = 126 \\ \sum X_1 Y &= (2)(65) + (3)(70) + (4)(75) + (5)(80) + (6)(85) = 130 + 210 + 300 + 400 + 510 = 1550 \\ \sum X_2 Y &= (8)(65) + (7)(70) + (6)(75) + (7)(80) + (5)(85) = 520 + 490 + 450 + 560 + 425 = 2445 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Form the normal equations.** $$ \begin{aligned} 5 b_0 + 20 b_1 + 33 b_2 &= 375 \quad \text{(E1)} \\ 20 b_0 + 90 b_1 + 126 b_2 &= 1550 \quad \text{(E2)} \\ 33 b_0 + 126 b_1 + 223 b_2 &= 2445 \quad \text{(E3)} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Solve using Cramer's rule.** The determinant of the coefficient matrix is: $$ D = \begin{vmatrix} 5 & 20 & 33 \\ 20 & 90 & 126 \\ 33 & 126 & 223 \end{vmatrix} $$ Expanding along the first row: $$ \begin{aligned} D &= 5 \begin{vmatrix} 90 & 126 \\ 126 & 223 \end{vmatrix} - 20 \begin{vmatrix} 20 & 126 \\ 33 & 223 \end{vmatrix} + 33 \begin{vmatrix} 20 & 90 \\ 33 & 126 \end{vmatrix} \\ &= 5 (90 \cdot 223 - 126 \cdot 126) - 20 (20 \cdot 223 - 126 \cdot 33) + 33 (20 \cdot 126 - 90 \cdot 33) \\ &= 5 (20070 - 15876) - 20 (4460 - 4158) + 33 (2520 - 2970) \\ &= 5 (4194) - 20 (302) + 33 (-450) \\ &= 20970 - 6040 - 14850 \\ &= 80 \end{aligned} $$ So $D = 80$. Now compute $D_0$ (replace first column with RHS): $$ D_0 = \begin{vmatrix} 375 & 20 & 33 \\ 1550 & 90 & 126 \\ 2445 & 126 & 223 \end{vmatrix} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} D_0 &= 375 \begin{vmatrix} 90 & 126 \\ 126 & 223 \end{vmatrix} - 20 \begin{vmatrix} 1550 & 126 \\ 2445 & 223 \end{vmatrix} + 33 \begin{vmatrix} 1550 & 90 \\ 2445 & 126 \end{vmatrix} \\ &= 375 (4194) - 20 (1550 \cdot 223 - 126 \cdot 2445) + 33 (1550 \cdot 126 - 90 \cdot 2445) \\ &= 375 (4194) - 20 (345650 - 308070) + 33 (195300 - 220050) \\ &= 1572750 - 20 (37580) + 33 (-24750) \\ &= 1572750 - 751600 - 816750 \\ &= 4400 \end{aligned} $$ Compute $D_1$ (replace second column with RHS): $$ D_1 = \begin{vmatrix} 5 & 375 & 33 \\ 20 & 1550 & 126 \\ 33 & 2445 & 223 \end{vmatrix} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} D_1 &= 5 \begin{vmatrix} 1550 & 126 \\ 2445 & 223 \end{vmatrix} - 375 \begin{vmatrix} 20 & 126 \\ 33 & 223 \end{vmatrix} + 33 \begin{vmatrix} 20 & 1550 \\ 33 & 2445 \end{vmatrix} \\ &= 5 (1550 \cdot 223 - 126 \cdot 2445) - 375 (20 \cdot 223 - 126 \cdot 33) + 33 (20 \cdot 2445 - 1550 \cdot 33) \\ &= 5 (37580) - 375 (302) + 33 (48900 - 51150) \\ &= 187900 - 113250 + 33 (-2250) \\ &= 187900 - 113250 - 74250 \\ &= 400 \end{aligned} $$ Compute $D_2$ (replace third column with RHS): $$ D_2 = \begin{vmatrix} 5 & 20 & 375 \\ 20 & 90 & 1550 \\ 33 & 126 & 2445 \end{vmatrix} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} D_2 &= 5 \begin{vmatrix} 90 & 1550 \\ 126 & 2445 \end{vmatrix} - 20 \begin{vmatrix} 20 & 1550 \\ 33 & 2445 \end{vmatrix} + 375 \begin{vmatrix} 20 & 90 \\ 33 & 126 \end{vmatrix} \\ &= 5 (90 \cdot 2445 - 1550 \cdot 126) - 20 (20 \cdot 2445 - 1550 \cdot 33) + 375 (20 \cdot 126 - 90 \cdot 33) \\ &= 5 (220050 - 195300) - 20 (48900 - 51150) + 375 (2520 - 2970) \\ &= 5 (24750) - 20 (-2250) + 375 (-450) \\ &= 123750 + 45000 - 168750 \\ &= 0 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Compute the regression coefficients.** $$ b_0 = \frac{D_0}{D} = \frac{4400}{80} = 55, \qquad b_1 = \frac{D_1}{D} = \frac{400}{80} = 5, \qquad b_2 = \frac{D_2}{D} = \frac{0}{80} = 0 $$ **Step 5 — Write the fitted regression equation.** $$ \hat{Y} = 55 + 5 X_1 + 0 \cdot X_2 $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Interpretation:** > - $b_0 = 55$ — the expected exam score when both study and sleep are zero (baseline; often a mathematical artefact). > - $b_1 = 5$ — for each **extra hour of study**, the exam score is expected to increase by **5 marks**, *holding sleep constant*. > - $b_2 = 0$ — sleep hours do **not contribute** uniquely to predicting the score once study hours are known. This is consistent with $X_1$ being the dominant predictor. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics Since multiple regression is fundamentally a mathematical estimation framework, a physical drawing is not directly applicable. Instead, the following **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** maps the entire analytical pipeline from data acquisition to inference, as a Mermaid block diagram. ```mermaid flowchart TD A0[Raw Dataset: n observations of Y, X1, X2] --> B0[Step 1: Compute Summary Statistics] B0 --> B1[Sum X1, Sum X2, Sum Y] B0 --> B2[Sum X1 squared, Sum X2 squared] B0 --> B3[Sum X1 X2, Sum X1 Y, Sum X2 Y] B1 --> C0[Step 2: Build Normal Equation Matrix] B2 --> C0 B3 --> C0 C0 --> C1[Form 3x3 Linear System: n b0 + sumX1 b1 + sumX2 b2 = sumY] C0 --> C2[Form sumX1 b0 + sumX1sq b1 + sumX1X2 b2 = sumX1Y] C0 --> C3[Form sumX2 b0 + sumX1X2 b1 + sumX2sq b2 = sumX2Y] C1 --> D0[Step 3: Solve via Cramer's Rule or Matrix Inversion] C2 --> D0 C3 --> D0 D0 --> E0[Obtain b0, b1, b2] E0 --> F0[Step 4: Compute Fitted Values Yhat_i = b0 + b1 X1_i + b2 X2_i] F0 --> G0[Step 5: Compute Residuals e_i = Y_i - Yhat_i] G0 --> H0[Step 6: Compute SSE, SSR, SST] H0 --> I0[Step 7: Calculate R squared, Adjusted R squared, F-statistic, t-statistics] I0 --> J0[Step 8: Hypothesis Testing at alpha = 0.05] J0 --> K0{Decision: Reject H0 or Fail to Reject} K0 --> L0[Final Interpretation and Reporting] ``` ### 4.1 The "Three-Coefficient Triangle" — Conceptual Map ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Inputs["Inputs (Predictors)"] X1[Independent Variable X1] X2[Independent Variable X2] end subgraph Weights["Estimated Weights (Partial Coefficients)"] B1[Partial Slope b1
Effect of X1 on Y
holding X2 fixed] B2[Partial Slope b2
Effect of X2 on Y
holding X1 fixed] end subgraph Output["Response"] YF[Fitted Score Yhat = b0 + b1 X1 + b2 X2] end B0[Intercept b0
Baseline value] --> YF X1 --> B1 --> YF X2 --> B2 --> YF ``` ### 4.2 Geometric Interpretation — A 3D Regression Plane In **simple regression** (one predictor), the model fits a *line* in 2D. With **two predictors**, the fitted model is a **plane in 3-dimensional space** with axes $X_1, X_2, Y$. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph PlaneSpace["3D Regression Plane in X1-X2-Y Space"] P1["Each observed point (X1_i, X2_i, Y_i) sits above or below the plane"] P2["The plane Yhat = b0 + b1 X1 + b2 X2 is the least-squares best fit"] P3["Vertical distance from point to plane = residual e_i = Y_i - Yhat_i"] P4["SSE = sum of squared vertical distances: minimized by OLS"] end P1 --> P2 P2 --> P3 P3 --> P4 ``` > [!NOTE] > The **plane orientation** is governed by $b_1$ (tilt along the $X_1$-axis) and $b_2$ (tilt along the $X_2$-axis). The intercept $b_0$ is the height of the plane where the two predictors are zero. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** *State the assumptions of the classical linear regression model with two independent variables. Why is it called the BLUE estimator?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** The classical multiple linear regression model with two predictors $$ Y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_{1i} + \beta_2 X_{2i} + \varepsilon_i $$ rests on the following **Gauss–Markov assumptions**: 1. **Linearity:** The relationship between $Y$ and $(X_1, X_2)$ is linear in parameters. 2. **Independence:** The error terms $\varepsilon_i$ are pairwise uncorrelated. 3. **Zero mean:** $E(\varepsilon_i) = 0$ for all $i$. 4. **Homoscedasticity:** $\text{Var}(\varepsilon_i) = \sigma^2$ (constant for all $i$). 5. **No autocorrelation:** $\text{Cov}(\varepsilon_i, \varepsilon_j) = 0$ for $i \neq j$. 6. **Normality (optional, for inference):** $\varepsilon_i \sim N(0, \sigma^2)$. 7. **No exact multi-collinearity:** $X_1$ and $X_2$ are not perfectly correlated. Under these assumptions, the OLS estimators $b_0, b_1, b_2$ are **BLUE** — **B**est (minimum variance among all linear unbiased estimators), **L**inear (linear functions of $Y$), **U**nbiased ($E(b_j) = \beta_j$), **E**stimators. This is the **Gauss–Markov theorem**. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *What do you mean by the coefficient of multiple determination? How is it different from the coefficient of simple determination?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **coefficient of multiple determination**, denoted $R^2$, is the proportion of the total variation in the dependent variable $Y$ that is **explained jointly** by all the independent variables in the regression model: $$ R^2 = \frac{\text{SSR}}{\text{SST}} = 1 - \frac{\text{SSE}}{\text{SST}} $$ It lies in the interval $[0, 1]$. A value close to 1 indicates that the model explains most of the variability of $Y$. **Differences from the simple determination coefficient $r^2$ (used in simple linear regression):** | Aspect | Simple $r^2$ | Multiple $R^2$ | |---|---|---| | Number of predictors | One | Two or more | | Interpretation | Fraction of variation in $Y$ explained by a single $X$ | Fraction of variation in $Y$ explained by all $X_j$ jointly | | Range | $[0, 1]$ | $[0, 1]$ | | Cross-variable effect | Ignores other variables | Accounts for joint effect of all predictors | | Adjustment | Not needed | Adjusted $R^2$ used for fair comparison across models | --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) **Q3. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4]** **Question A (14 marks):** The following data relate to a study of three variables $Y$, $X_1$, and $X_2$: | $X_1$ | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | $X_2$ | 8 | 7 | 6 | 7 | 5 | | $Y$ | 65 | 70 | 75 | 80 | 85 | **(a)** Fit a multiple linear regression model of $Y$ on $X_1$ and $X_2$ using the method of least squares. (7 marks) **(b)** Compute the coefficient of multiple determination $R^2$ and the adjusted $R^2$. Comment on the goodness of fit. (7 marks) **Model Answer:** **(a) Solution (7 marks):** *Step 1: Compute the required summations* (already shown in Section 3.4): $$ \begin{aligned} n &= 5, \quad \sum X_1 = 20, \quad \sum X_2 = 33, \quad \sum Y = 375 \\ \sum X_1^2 &= 90, \quad \sum X_2^2 = 223, \quad \sum X_1 X_2 = 126 \\ \sum X_1 Y &= 1550, \quad \sum X_2 Y = 2445 \end{aligned} $$ *Step 2: Form the normal equations:* $$ \begin{aligned} 5 b_0 + 20 b_1 + 33 b_2 &= 375 \\ 20 b_0 + 90 b_1 + 126 b_2 &= 1550 \\ 33 b_0 + 126 b_1 + 223 b_2 &= 2445 \end{aligned} $$ *Step 3: Solve using Cramer's rule* (computations in Section 3.4): $$ D = 80, \quad D_0 = 4400, \quad D_1 = 400, \quad D_2 = 0 $$ $$ b_0 = \frac{4400}{80} = 55, \qquad b_1 = \frac{400}{80} = 5, \qquad b_2 = \frac{0}{80} = 0 $$ *Step 4: Fitted regression equation:* $$ \boxed{\hat{Y} = 55 + 5 X_1 + 0 \cdot X_2} $$ **(b) Solution (7 marks):** *Step 1: Compute $\bar{Y}$.* $$ \bar{Y} = \frac{\sum Y}{n} = \frac{375}{5} = 75 $$ *Step 2: Compute SST.* $$ \text{SST} = \sum (Y_i - \bar{Y})^2 = (65-75)^2 + (70-75)^2 + (75-75)^2 + (80-75)^2 + (85-75)^2 $$ $$ \text{SST} = 100 + 25 + 0 + 25 + 100 = 250 $$ *Step 3: Compute $\hat{Y}_i$ and SSR.* For each $i$, using $\hat{Y}_i = 55 + 5 X_{1i} + 0 \cdot X_{2i}$: - $\hat{Y}_1 = 55 + 5(2) = 65$ - $\hat{Y}_2 = 55 + 5(3) = 70$ - $\hat{Y}_3 = 55 + 5(4) = 75$ - $\hat{Y}_4 = 55 + 5(5) = 80$ - $\hat{Y}_5 = 55 + 5(6) = 85$ $$ \text{SSR} = \sum (\hat{Y}_i - \bar{Y})^2 = (65-75)^2 + (70-75)^2 + (75-75)^2 + (80-75)^2 + (85-75)^2 = 250 $$ *Step 4: Compute $R^2$.* $$ R^2 = \frac{\text{SSR}}{\text{SST}} = \frac{250}{250} = 1.00 $$ *Step 5: Compute the adjusted $R^2$ (with $k = 2$ predictors).* $$ R^2_{\text{adj}} = 1 - \frac{(1 - R^2)(n - 1)}{n - k - 1} = 1 - \frac{(1 - 1)(4)}{2} = 1 - 0 = 1.00 $$ *Step 6: Comment on goodness of fit.* The $R^2 = 1.00$ indicates that **100% of the variability in $Y$ is explained by the two independent variables** in the model. The data are perfectly fitted by the regression equation, leaving no residual variation. This is mathematically possible here because the data values lie exactly on the line $Y = 55 + 5 X_1$ (a deterministic pattern, with $X_2$ playing no role). In practice, such perfect fits are rare and may signal either a controlled experiment or an over-simplified dataset. --- **Question B (14 marks) — Alternative Choice:** The following data are obtained from an experiment relating the yield $Y$ of a chemical process to two independent variables: temperature $X_1$ (°C) and pressure $X_2$ (atm). | $X_1$ | 100 | 110 | 120 | 130 | 140 | 150 | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | $X_2$ | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 | | $Y$ | 50 | 55 | 60 | 65 | 70 | 75 | **(a)** Compute the least-squares estimates of the regression coefficients $\beta_0, \beta_1, \beta_2$ in the model $Y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_1 + \beta_2 X_2 + \varepsilon$. (7 marks) **(b)** Test the overall significance of the model using the F-test at $\alpha = 0.05$. Use $F_{0.05, 2, 3} = 9.55$. (7 marks) **Model Answer:** **(a) Solution (7 marks):** *Step 1: Compute the summations.* $$ \begin{aligned} n &= 6 \\ \sum X_1 &= 100 + 110 + 120 + 130 + 140 + 150 = 750 \\ \sum X_2 &= 2 + 3 + 4 + 5 + 6 + 7 = 27 \\ \sum Y &= 50 + 55 + 60 + 65 + 70 + 75 = 375 \\ \sum X_1^2 &= 10000 + 12100 + 14400 + 16900 + 19600 + 22500 = 95500 \\ \sum X_2^2 &= 4 + 9 + 16 + 25 + 36 + 49 = 139 \\ \sum X_1 X_2 &= (100)(2) + (110)(3) + (120)(4) + (130)(5) + (140)(6) + (150)(7) \\ &= 200 + 330 + 480 + 650 + 840 + 1050 = 3550 \\ \sum X_1 Y &= (100)(50) + (110)(55) + (120)(60) + (130)(65) + (140)(70) + (150)(75) \\ &= 5000 + 6050 + 7200 + 8450 + 9800 + 11250 = 47750 \\ \sum X_2 Y &= (2)(50) + (3)(55) + (4)(60) + (5)(65) + (6)(70) + (7)(75) \\ &= 100 + 165 + 240 + 325 + 420 + 525 = 1775 \end{aligned} $$ *Step 2: Form the normal equations.* $$ \begin{aligned} 6 b_0 + 750 b_1 + 27 b_2 &= 375 \\ 750 b_0 + 95500 b_1 + 3550 b_2 &= 47750 \\ 27 b_0 + 3550 b_1 + 139 b_2 &= 1775 \end{aligned} $$ *Step 3: Solve the system. Using matrix methods or elimination:* Dividing equation (E1) by 6 to obtain $b_0$ in terms of the others: $$ b_0 = \frac{375 - 750 b_1 - 27 b_2}{6} = 62.5 - 125 b_1 - 4.5 b_2 $$ Substituting into (E2): $$ 750(62.5 - 125 b_1 - 4.5 b_2) + 95500 b_1 + 3550 b_2 = 47750 $$ $$ 46875 - 93750 b_1 - 3375 b_2 + 95500 b_1 + 3550 b_2 = 47750 $$ $$ 1750 b_1 + 175 b_2 = 875 \quad \Rightarrow \quad b_1 + 0.1 b_2 = 0.5 \quad \text{(E2')} $$ Substituting into (E3): $$ 27(62.5 - 125 b_1 - 4.5 b_2) + 3550 b_1 + 139 b_2 = 1775 $$ $$ 1687.5 - 3375 b_1 - 121.5 b_2 + 3550 b_1 + 139 b_2 = 1775 $$ $$ 175 b_1 + 17.5 b_2 = 87.5 \quad \Rightarrow \quad b_1 + 0.1 b_2 = 0.5 \quad \text{(E3')} $$ Note: (E2') and (E3') are **identical**, indicating the data have a perfect linear structure. Solving: $$ b_1 + 0.1 b_2 = 0.5 $$ With $b_2 = 0$ (since adding any other choice would over-fit the deterministic relationship): $$ b_1 = 0.5, \qquad b_2 = 0 $$ Then $b_0 = 62.5 - 125(0.5) - 4.5(0) = 62.5 - 62.5 = 0$. $$ \boxed{b_0 = 0, \quad b_1 = 0.5, \quad b_2 = 0} $$ *Step 4: Fitted model.* $$ \hat{Y} = 0 + 0.5 X_1 + 0 \cdot X_2 = 0.5 X_1 $$ **(b) Solution (7 marks):** *Step 1: Compute $\bar{Y}$.* $$ \bar{Y} = \frac{375}{6} = 62.5 $$ *Step 2: Compute SST.* $$ \begin{aligned} \text{SST} &= (50-62.5)^2 + (55-62.5)^2 + (60-62.5)^2 + (65-62.5)^2 + (70-62.5)^2 + (75-62.5)^2 \\ &= 156.25 + 56.25 + 6.25 + 6.25 + 56.25 + 156.25 \\ &= 437.5 \end{aligned} $$ *Step 3: Compute $\hat{Y}_i$ and SSR.* For each $i$, $\hat{Y}_i = 0.5 X_{1i}$: - $\hat{Y}_1 = 0.5(100) = 50$ - $\hat{Y}_2 = 0.5(110) = 55$ - $\hat{Y}_3 = 0.5(120) = 60$ - $\hat{Y}_4 = 0.5(130) = 65$ - $\hat{Y}_5 = 0.5(140) = 70$ - $\hat{Y}_6 = 0.5(150) = 75$ $$ \begin{aligned} \text{SSR} &= (50-62.5)^2 + (55-62.5)^2 + (60-62.5)^2 + (65-62.5)^2 + (70-62.5)^2 + (75-62.5)^2 \\ &= 437.5 \end{aligned} $$ *Step 4: Compute SSE.* $$ \text{SSE} = \text{SST} - \text{SSR} = 437.5 - 437.5 = 0 $$ *Step 5: Compute the F-statistic.* With $k = 2$ predictors and residual df $= n - k - 1 = 6 - 2 - 1 = 3$: $$ F = \frac{\text{SSR}/k}{\text{SSE}/(n-k-1)} = \frac{437.5/2}{0/3} = \frac{218.75}{0} \to \infty $$ *Step 6: Decision.* The computed $F$ tends to infinity, which is **far greater than** $F_{0.05, 2, 3} = 9.55$. Therefore, we **reject $H_0: \beta_1 = \beta_2 = 0$** at the 5% significance level and conclude that the overall regression model is **highly statistically significant**. > [!NOTE] > **Why $F \to \infty$ here?** Because SSE $= 0$ (perfect fit), the F-statistic blows up. The data lie exactly on the line $\hat{Y} = 0.5 X_1$, confirming that temperature alone perfectly explains the yield. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in KTU Board Examinations:** > 1. **Forgetting the intercept:** Always include $b_0$ in the normal equations. Skipping it loses 1–2 marks. > 2. **Sign error in Cramer's rule:** When computing $D_0, D_1, D_2$, students often transpose determinants. Cross-check the position of the RHS column. > 3. **Misidentifying $k$:** For two predictors, $k = 2$, *not* $k = 3$. The residual degrees of freedom is $n - 3$, not $n - 2$. > 4. **Confusing SSE, SSR, SST:** Memorize the identity $\text{SST} = \text{SSR} + \text{SSE}$ and the order: $\text{SSR}$ (explained) is on top, $\text{SSE}$ (unexplained) is on bottom in the F-ratio. > 5. **Reporting $R^2$ as a percentage:** Always report as a decimal in $[0,1]$, e.g., $0.85$, not $85\%$. > 6. **Skipping the model statement:** Begin the answer with the explicit population model $Y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_{1i} + \beta_2 X_{2i} + \varepsilon_i$ — examiners award credit for this setup. > 7. **Interpretation of $b_2 = 0$:** Do **not** conclude "the model is useless" — $b_2 = 0$ simply means that *once $X_1$ is in the model, $X_2$ adds no new information*. The model is still valid. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Population Model:** $Y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_{1i} + \beta_2 X_{2i} + \varepsilon_i$. - **Fitted Model:** $\hat{Y}_i = b_0 + b_1 X_{1i} + b_2 X_{2i}$. - **Estimation Method:** Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) — minimize $\sum (Y_i - \hat{Y}_i)^2$. - **Normal Equations:** A $3 \times 3$ linear system in $b_0, b_1, b_2$ — solve via **Cramer's rule**, **Gaussian elimination**, or **matrix inversion** $(\mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{X})^{-1} \mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{Y}$. - **Partial Coefficients:** $b_1$ and $b_2$ are *partial* slopes — each represents the effect of one $X$ **holding the other constant**. - **$R^2$ Interpretation:** Proportion of variance in $Y$ explained by $X_1$ and $X_2$ jointly; range $[0, 1]$. - **Adjusted $R^2$:** Penalizes model complexity; use $R^2_{\text{adj}} = 1 - (1 - R^2)(n-1)/(n-3)$. - **Standard Error of Estimate:** $s_e = \sqrt{\text{SSE}/(n-3)}$. - **F-test:** Tests $H_0: \beta_1 = \beta_2 = 0$ (overall model significance). - **t-test:** Tests $H_0: \beta_j = 0$ for individual predictors. - **Residual df:** $n - 3$ for two-predictor model. - **SST Identity:** $\text{SST} = \text{SSR} + \text{SSE}$ — always holds for OLS with intercept. - **Geometric Picture:** Fitted model is a **plane** in 3D $(X_1, X_2, Y)$ space. - **Key Assumptions:** Linearity, independence, homoscedasticity, no autocorrelation, no perfect collinearity, normality (for inference). - **Estimator Property:** OLS estimators are **BLUE** under Gauss–Markov assumptions. - **KTU Defaults:** Significance level $\alpha = 0.05$ (or 0.01 for stricter tests); $F$-tables give critical values for $(2, n-3)$ df in two-predictor models.

topics.title: Analysis of Variance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GDMAT401 - MATHEMATICS FOR LIFE SCIENCE – 4
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Analysis of Variance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analysis of Variance (ANOVA)** is a powerful statistical methodology developed by **Sir Ronald A. Fisher (1923)** that partitions the **total variability** observed in a dataset into components attributable to **different sources of variation**, in order to test hypotheses about the equality of several population means simultaneously.

In the context of **GDMAT401 (Life Sciences applications)**, ANOVA is the backbone of experimental biology, agricultural research, pharmaceutical trials, and ecological studies. The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes:

- **One-Way Classification (Completely Randomized Design - CRD)**
- **Two-Way Classification (Randomized Block Design - RBD)**
- **F-distribution and F-test for significance**
- **Critical value comparison at significance level $\alpha = 0.05$ or $0.01$**

## 1.2 The Central Idea — "Why Variance, Not Just Means?"

> [!NOTE]
> A common student misconception is: *“Why not just use a t-test to compare means?”*
> **Answer:** When comparing $k > 2$ groups, performing multiple t-tests inflates the **Type I error rate** (false positive). For example, comparing 5 groups pairwise with $\alpha = 0.05$ yields a family-wise error rate of $1 - (0.95)^{10} \approx 0.40$. ANOVA solves this by testing **all means simultaneously**.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Classroom Performance" Intuition

Imagine a university running **3 different teaching methods** (Online, Hybrid, Offline) across **3 batches** of students. The final exam scores vary. We want to know:

- **Is the variation in scores mainly due to teaching method (real effect)?** OR
- **Is it just random noise (chance variation)?**

ANOVA answers this by **breaking down the total score variation** into:

1. **Variation BETWEEN teaching methods** → Due to treatment effect
2. **Variation WITHIN each method** → Due to random/unexplained error

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Life Science Analogy:** A pharmaceutical company tests 4 dosages of a drug on 20 patients each. They want to know if the **average blood pressure reduction** differs across dosages. ANOVA compares the variance *between* dosage groups to the variance *within* each dosage group. If between-group variance is **significantly larger**, at least one dosage truly differs.

## 1.4 Fundamental Hypotheses

For $k$ populations with means $\mu_1, \mu_2, \ldots, \mu_k$:

$$H_0 : \mu_1 = \mu_2 = \mu_3 = \cdots = \mu_k \quad \text{(All means are equal — no treatment effect)}$$

$$H_1 : \mu_i \neq \mu_j \quad \text{for at least one pair } (i, j) \quad \text{(At least one mean differs)}$$

## 1.5 Key Statistical Constants Used in ANOVA

| Symbol | Meaning |
|:------:|:--------|
| $\alpha$ | Level of significance (**typically 0.05**) |
| $k$ | Number of treatments / groups |
| $n$ | Number of observations per group (equal) |
| $N = kn$ | Total number of observations |
| $F$ | Fisher's variance ratio statistic |
| $F_{\alpha}(v_1, v_2)$ | Critical value from F-table |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Core Variances in ANOVA

The total variation in any dataset is decomposed into three independent components. For **One-Way ANOVA**:

### 2.1.1 Total Sum of Squares (SST or TSS)

This measures the **total variability** of all observations around the grand mean $\bar{X}$:

$$SST = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \sum_{j=1}^{n} (X_{ij} - \bar{X})^2$$

### 2.1.2 Sum of Squares Between Treatments (SSC or SSA)

This measures the variability **due to differences between group means** $\bar{X}_i$:

$$SSC = \sum_{i=1}^{k} n_i (\bar{X}_i - \bar{X})^2$$

### 2.1.3 Sum of Squares Within Treatments (SSE or SSW)

This measures the variability **within each group** (random error):

$$SSE = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \sum_{j=1}^{n} (X_{ij} - \bar{X}_i)^2$$

## 2.2 The Fundamental Partitioning Identity (KEY THEOREM)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ANOVA Identity (Total Variation Decomposition Theorem):**
> $$SST = SSC + SSE$$
> This is the **single most important equation** in ANOVA. It states that total variability is exactly partitioned into between-group and within-group components.

## 2.3 Degrees of Freedom — The "Information Budget"

| Component | Degrees of Freedom | Logic |
|:----------|:------------------:|:------|
| Between Treatments | $k - 1$ | From $k$ group means, we estimate 1 grand mean |
| Within Treatments | $N - k$ | Each group loses 1 df for its own mean |
| **Total** | $N - 1$ | Total observations minus 1 for grand mean |

## 2.4 Mean Squares (Variance Estimates)

| Mean Square | Formula | E[MS] under $H_0$ |
|:------------|:--------|:------------------|
| $MSC$ (Between) | $\dfrac{SSC}{k-1}$ | $\sigma^2 + n\sigma_{\mu}^2$ |
| $MSE$ (Within) | $\dfrac{SSE}{N-k}$ | $\sigma^2$ |

## 2.5 The F-Statistic (Fisher's Ratio)

$$F = \frac{MSC}{MSE} = \frac{\dfrac{SSC}{k-1}}{\dfrac{SSE}{N-k}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Decision Rule:** If $F_{calculated} > F_{critical}(k-1, N-k, \alpha)$, **reject $H_0$**.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Expression | Purpose |
|:--------|:-----------|:--------|
| Grand Mean | $\bar{X} = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum\sum X_{ij}$ | Center of all data |
| Group Mean | $\bar{X}_i = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{j} X_{ij}$ | Mean of group $i$ |
| Correction Factor (CF) | $CF = \dfrac{T^2}{N}$ where $T = \sum\sum X_{ij}$ | Computational shortcut |
| SST | $SST = \sum\sum X_{ij}^2 - CF$ | Total variation |
| SSC | $SSC = \sum \dfrac{T_i^2}{n_i} - CF$ | Between-group variation |
| SSE | $SSE = SST - SSC$ | Within-group variation |
| F-ratio | $F = \dfrac{SSC/(k-1)}{SSE/(N-k)}$ | Test statistic |
| R-Squared | $R^2 = \dfrac{SSC}{SST}$ | Proportion explained |

## 2.7 Two-Way ANOVA (RBD) — Extended Formulae

In Randomized Block Design with $k$ treatments and $b$ blocks:

| Source | SS | df | MS | F |
|:-------|:---|:---|:---|:--|
| Treatments (Rows) | $SSR = \sum \dfrac{T_i^2}{b} - CF$ | $k-1$ | $MSR = \dfrac{SSR}{k-1}$ | $\dfrac{MSR}{MSE}$ |
| Blocks (Columns) | $SSB = \sum \dfrac{B_j^2}{k} - CF$ | $b-1$ | $MSB = \dfrac{SSB}{b-1}$ | $\dfrac{MSB}{MSE}$ |
| Error (Residual) | $SSE = SST - SSR - SSB$ | $(k-1)(b-1)$ | $MSE = \dfrac{SSE}{(k-1)(b-1)}$ | — |
| **Total** | $SST = \sum\sum X_{ij}^2 - CF$ | $N-1 = kb-1$ | — | — |

## 2.8 Engineering Utility in Life Sciences

- **Pharmaceuticals:** Compare efficacy of multiple drug formulations
- **Agriculture:** Test yield differences across fertilizer types
- **Ecology:** Compare species diversity across habitats
- **Biomedical Engineering:** Evaluate sensor accuracy across devices
- **Food Science:** Compare shelf-life of preservation methods
- **Clinical Trials:** Assess treatment effects controlling for patient blocks (age, gender)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Partitioning Identity: $SST = SSC + SSE$

This is the **mathematical heart of ANOVA**. We start with:

$$SST = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \sum_{j=1}^{n} (X_{ij} - \bar{X})^2$$

**Step 1:** Add and subtract the group mean $\bar{X}_i$ inside the parenthesis:

$$(X_{ij} - \bar{X}) = (X_{ij} - \bar{X}_i) + (\bar{X}_i - \bar{X})$$

**Step 2:** Substitute back into SST:

$$SST = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \sum_{j=1}^{n} \left[(X_{ij} - \bar{X}_i) + (\bar{X}_i - \bar{X})\right]^2$$

**Step 3:** Expand the square using $(a+b)^2 = a^2 + 2ab + b^2$:

$$SST = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \sum_{j=1}^{n} \left[(X_{ij} - \bar{X}_i)^2 + 2(X_{ij} - \bar{X}_i)(\bar{X}_i - \bar{X}) + (\bar{X}_i - \bar{X})^2\right]$$

**Step 4:** Split the triple sum into three parts:

$$SST = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \sum_{j=1}^{n} (X_{ij} - \bar{X}_i)^2 + 2\sum_{i=1}^{k} (\bar{X}_i - \bar{X})\sum_{j=1}^{n} (X_{ij} - \bar{X}_i) + \sum_{i=1}^{k} \sum_{j=1}^{n} (\bar{X}_i - \bar{X})^2$$

**Step 5:** Evaluate the middle term. For each fixed $i$:

$$\sum_{j=1}^{n} (X_{ij} - \bar{X}_i) = \sum_{j=1}^{n} X_{ij} - n\bar{X}_i = n\bar{X}_i - n\bar{X}_i = 0$$

**Step 6:** Therefore the cross-product term vanishes:

$$SST = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \sum_{j=1}^{n} (X_{ij} - \bar{X}_i)^2 + \sum_{i=1}^{k} n(\bar{X}_i - \bar{X})^2$$

**Step 7:** Recognize the two resulting terms:

$$\boxed{SST = \underbrace{\sum_{i=1}^{k} \sum_{j=1}^{n} (X_{ij} - \bar{X}_i)^2}_{SSE \text{ (Within)}} + \underbrace{\sum_{i=1}^{k} n(\bar{X}_i - \bar{X})^2}_{SSC \text{ (Between)}}}$$

**Conclusion:** $SST = SSC + SSE$ — *Q.E.D.*

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does the cross-term vanish?** Because each group's deviations from its own mean sum to zero — this is a fundamental property of the arithmetic mean: $\sum (x_i - \bar{x}) = 0$.

## 3.2 Worked-Out Numerical Example (One-Way ANOVA)

**Problem:** Three fertilizers (A, B, C) are tested on 4 plots each. Yields in kg are:

| Fertilizer A | Fertilizer B | Fertilizer C |
|:------------:|:------------:|:------------:|
| 20 | 22 | 25 |
| 21 | 24 | 28 |
| 19 | 21 | 27 |
| 20 | 23 | 26 |

Test at $\alpha = 0.05$ whether the fertilizers differ in mean yield.

### Step 1: State Hypotheses

$$H_0: \mu_A = \mu_B = \mu_C \quad \text{vs.} \quad H_1: \text{Not all means are equal}$$

### Step 2: Compute Totals

$$T_A = 20+21+19+20 = 80$$
$$T_B = 22+24+21+23 = 90$$
$$T_C = 25+28+27+26 = 104$$
$$G = T = 80 + 90 + 104 = 274$$

### Step 3: Correction Factor

Here, $N = 12$, $k = 3$, $n = 4$.

$$CF = \frac{T^2}{N} = \frac{(274)^2}{12} = \frac{75076}{12} = 6256.333$$

### Step 4: Total Sum of Squares

$$\sum\sum X_{ij}^2 = (20^2+21^2+19^2+20^2) + (22^2+24^2+21^2+23^2) + (25^2+28^2+27^2+26^2)$$

Compute each group:

$$(400+441+361+400) = 1602$$
$$(484+576+441+529) = 2030$$
$$(625+784+729+676) = 2814$$
$$\sum\sum X_{ij}^2 = 1602 + 2030 + 2814 = 6446$$

Therefore:

$$SST = 6446 - 6256.333 = 189.667$$

### Step 5: Between-Group Sum of Squares

$$SSC = \frac{T_A^2 + T_B^2 + T_C^2}{n} - CF = \frac{80^2 + 90^2 + 104^2}{4} - 6256.333$$

$$= \frac{6400 + 8100 + 10816}{4} - 6256.333 = \frac{25316}{4} - 6256.333$$

$$= 6329 - 6256.333 = 72.667$$

### Step 6: Within-Group Sum of Squares

$$SSE = SST - SSC = 189.667 - 72.667 = 117.000$$

### Step 7: Verification

$$\text{Check: } SST = SSC + SSE \Rightarrow 189.667 = 72.667 + 117.000 \checkmark$$

### Step 8: Degrees of Freedom

- Between: $k - 1 = 3 - 1 = 2$
- Within: $N - k = 12 - 3 = 9$
- Total: $N - 1 = 11$

### Step 9: Mean Squares

$$MSC = \frac{SSC}{k-1} = \frac{72.667}{2} = 36.333$$

$$MSE = \frac{SSE}{N-k} = \frac{117.000}{9} = 13.000$$

### Step 10: F-Statistic

$$F_{cal} = \frac{MSC}{MSE} = \frac{36.333}{13.000} = 2.795$$

### Step 11: Critical Value

$$F_{crit}(2, 9, 0.05) = 4.26 \quad \text{(from F-tables)}$$

### Step 12: Decision

$$F_{cal} = 2.795 < F_{crit} = 4.26$$

**Conclusion:** **Fail to reject $H_0$**. The three fertilizers do **NOT** produce significantly different mean yields at the 5% level.

## 3.3 Complete ANOVA Table

| Source | SS | df | MS | F | F-critical |
|:-------|:--:|:--:|:--:|:-:|:----------:|
| Between (Fertilizer) | 72.667 | 2 | 36.333 | 2.795 | 4.26 |
| Within (Error) | 117.000 | 9 | 13.000 | — | — |
| **Total** | **189.667** | **11** | — | — | — |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The ANOVA Conceptual Framework

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Total Variation SST"] --> B["Between-Group Variation SSC"]
    A --> C["Within-Group Variation SSE"]
    B --> D["Treatment Effect Signal"]
    C --> E["Random Noise Error"]
    D --> F["Compute MSC = SSC divided by k-1"]
    E --> G["Compute MSE = SSE divided by N-k"]
    F --> H["F-ratio = MSC divided by MSE"]
    G --> H
    H --> I{"F calc greater than F crit?"}
    I -->|Yes| J["Reject H0: Means differ significantly"]
    I -->|No| K["Fail to reject H0: No significant difference"]
```

## 4.2 One-Way ANOVA Computational Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stepA["Input Data Matrix X_ij"] --> stepB["Compute Group Totals T_i"]
    stepB --> stepC["Compute Grand Total T"]
    stepC --> stepD["Compute Correction Factor CF = T squared over N"]
    stepD --> stepE["Compute SST = sum X squared minus CF"]
    stepD --> stepF["Compute SSC = sum T_i squared over n_i minus CF"]
    stepF --> stepG["Compute SSE = SST minus SSC"]
    stepE --> stepG
    stepG --> stepH["Compute MSC and MSE"]
    stepH --> stepI["Compute F = MSC divided by MSE"]
    stepI --> stepJ["Compare with F-critical value"]
```

## 4.3 Two-Way ANOVA (Randomized Block Design) Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P["Total Variability SST"] --> Q["Treatment Variation SSR"]
    P --> R["Block Variation SSB"]
    P --> S["Error Variation SSE"]
    Q --> T["MSR = SSR divided by k minus 1"]
    R --> U["MSB = SSB divided by b minus 1"]
    S --> V["MSE = SSE divided by k-1 times b-1"]
    T --> W["F treatment = MSR divided by MSE"]
    U --> X["F block = MSB divided by MSE"]
    W --> Y{"F treatment greater than F crit?"}
    X --> Z{"F block greater than F crit?"}
    Y -->|Yes| AA["Treatments differ significantly"]
    Y -->|No| AB["No treatment effect"]
    Z -->|Yes| AC["Blocking was effective"]
    Z -->|No| AD["Blocking ineffective"]
```

## 4.4 F-Distribution Curve Behavior

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["F Distribution: Always Non-Negative"] --> B["Right-Skewed Curve"]
    B --> C["Starts at Origin 0"]
    B --> D["Mode at df2 minus 2 over df2 times df1"]
    B --> E["Approaches Normal as df increase"]
    A --> F["Critical Region: Right Tail beyond F-crit"]
    F --> G["Reject H0 if F-calculated falls in right tail"]
```

## 4.5 Hypothesis Testing Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Set H0 and H1"] --> B["Choose Significance Level alpha = 0.05"]
    B --> C["Compute F-calculated"]
    C --> D["Look up F-critical from F-table"]
    D --> E{"F-calc greater than F-crit?"}
    E -->|Yes| F["Reject H0: Significant difference exists"]
    E -->|No| G["Accept H0: No significant difference"]
    F --> H["Conclude: At least one treatment mean differs"]
    G --> I["Conclude: All treatment means are statistically equal"]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

### Question 1: Define Analysis of Variance. State its basic assumptions.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Analysis of Variance (ANOVA) is a statistical technique developed by **R.A. Fisher** used to test the **equality of three or more population means** simultaneously by partitioning the total variance in a dataset into components attributable to different sources of variation.

**Basic Assumptions (1 mark):**
1. **Normality:** Each population from which samples are drawn is normally distributed.
2. **Homogeneity of Variance:** All populations have **equal variances** ($\sigma_1^2 = \sigma_2^2 = \cdots = \sigma_k^2$).
3. **Independence:** All observations are **mutually independent** of one another.

---

### Question 2: What is the F-distribution? State its key properties.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** The **F-distribution** is the ratio of two independent chi-square variates divided by their respective degrees of freedom:

$$F = \frac{\chi_1^2 / v_1}{\chi_2^2 / v_2}$$

**Key Properties (1.5 marks):**
1. F-distribution is **asymmetric** and **positively skewed** (right-skewed).
2. F-values are **always non-negative** ($F \geq 0$), since variances cannot be negative.
3. F-distribution is characterized by **two parameters**: numerator degrees of freedom $v_1$ and denominator degrees of freedom $v_2$.
4. As both $v_1$ and $v_2 \to \infty$, the F-distribution approaches a **normal distribution**.
5. The F-distribution has **two applications**: testing equality of several means and testing equality of two variances.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU End Semester Exam – December 2023 Pattern]**

### ✦ Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

A plant biologist studies the effect of **4 different growth hormones** on the height (in cm) of a plant species. She records the following data:

| Hormone 1 | Hormone 2 | Hormone 3 | Hormone 4 |
|:---------:|:---------:|:---------:|:---------:|
| 12 | 15 | 18 | 10 |
| 14 | 16 | 20 | 13 |
| 11 | 14 | 17 | 12 |
| 13 | 17 | 19 | 11 |
| 15 | 15 | 21 | 14 |

**(a)** Set up the ANOVA table and test whether the mean plant heights differ significantly across the 4 hormones at $\alpha = 0.05$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If significant, state which treatment(s) appear to differ and explain the practical interpretation in the context of the life science application. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Complete Model Solution for Question A:

**Part (a) — ANOVA Table Computation (7 marks):**

**Step 1: State the Hypotheses**

$$H_0: \mu_1 = \mu_2 = \mu_3 = \mu_4 \quad \text{(All hormone means are equal)}$$

$$H_1: \mu_i \neq \mu_j \quad \text{for at least one pair}$$

**Step 2: Compute Group Totals**

$$T_1 = 12+14+11+13+15 = 65$$
$$T_2 = 15+16+14+17+15 = 77$$
$$T_3 = 18+20+17+19+21 = 95$$
$$T_4 = 10+13+12+11+14 = 60$$
$$G = T = 65+77+95+60 = 297$$

**Step 3: Parameters**

$$N = 20, \quad k = 4, \quad n = 5$$

**Step 4: Correction Factor**

$$CF = \frac{T^2}{N} = \frac{(297)^2}{20} = \frac{88209}{20} = 4410.45$$

**Step 5: Sum of Squares — Raw Data Squared**

Group 1: $144+196+121+169+225 = 855$

Group 2: $225+256+196+289+225 = 1191$

Group 3: $324+400+289+361+441 = 1815$

Group 4: $100+169+144+121+196 = 730$

$$\sum\sum X_{ij}^2 = 855 + 1191 + 1815 + 730 = 4591$$

**Step 6: Total Sum of Squares**

$$SST = 4591 - 4410.45 = 180.55$$

**Step 7: Between-Treatments Sum of Squares**

$$SSC = \frac{T_1^2 + T_2^2 + T_3^2 + T_4^2}{n} - CF = \frac{65^2 + 77^2 + 95^2 + 60^2}{5} - 4410.45$$

$$= \frac{4225 + 5929 + 9025 + 3600}{5} - 4410.45 = \frac{22779}{5} - 4410.45$$

$$= 4555.80 - 4410.45 = 145.35$$

**Step 8: Within-Treatments Sum of Squares**

$$SSE = SST - SSC = 180.55 - 145.35 = 35.20$$

**Step 9: Degrees of Freedom**

- Between: $k - 1 = 3$
- Within: $N - k = 20 - 4 = 16$
- Total: $19$

**Step 10: Mean Squares**

$$MSC = \frac{145.35}{3} = 48.45$$

$$MSE = \frac{35.20}{16} = 2.20$$

**Step 11: F-Statistic**

$$F_{cal} = \frac{MSC}{MSE} = \frac{48.45}{2.20} = 22.023$$

**Step 12: Critical Value from F-Table**

$$F_{crit}(3, 16, 0.05) = 3.24$$

**Step 13: Decision**

$$F_{cal} = 22.023 \gg F_{crit} = 3.24$$

$$\therefore \text{Reject } H_0$$

**Step 14: Complete ANOVA Table**

| Source | SS | df | MS | F-cal | F-crit |
|:-------|:--:|:--:|:--:|:-----:|:------:|
| Between Hormones | 145.35 | 3 | 48.45 | 22.02 | 3.24 |
| Within (Error) | 35.20 | 16 | 2.20 | — | — |
| **Total** | **180.55** | **19** | — | — | — |

**Conclusion (1 mark in 7):** The mean plant heights differ significantly across the 4 hormones at $\alpha = 0.05$.

---

**Part (b) — Practical Interpretation and Treatment Comparison (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Compute Group Means**

$$\bar{X}_1 = 65/5 = 13.0 \text{ cm}$$
$$\bar{X}_2 = 77/5 = 15.4 \text{ cm}$$
$$\bar{X}_3 = 95/5 = 19.0 \text{ cm}$$
$$\bar{X}_4 = 60/5 = 12.0 \text{ cm}$$

**Step 2: Identify Differing Treatments**

Ordering means: $\bar{X}_3 (19.0) > \bar{X}_2 (15.4) > \bar{X}_1 (13.0) > \bar{X}_4 (12.0)$.

The treatment with the **highest mean** is **Hormone 3 (19.0 cm)**, which is clearly the most effective growth promoter.

**Step 3: Critical Difference (CD) using Least Significant Difference (LSD) Test**

For pairwise comparison at $\alpha = 0.05$:

$$LSD = t_{\alpha/2, (N-k)} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot MSE}{n}}$$

With $t_{0.025, 16} = 2.120$:

$$LSD = 2.120 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 2.20}{5}} = 2.120 \cdot \sqrt{0.88} = 2.120 \cdot 0.938 = 1.989 \text{ cm}$$

**Step 4: Pairwise Comparisons**

| Pair | Difference | Exceeds LSD (1.99)? | Significant? |
|:-----|:----------:|:-------------------:|:------------:|
| 3 vs 4 | 7.0 | Yes | **Yes** |
| 3 vs 1 | 6.0 | Yes | **Yes** |
| 3 vs 2 | 3.6 | Yes | **Yes** |
| 2 vs 4 | 3.4 | Yes | **Yes** |
| 2 vs 1 | 2.4 | Yes | **Yes** |
| 1 vs 4 | 1.0 | No | No |

**Step 5: Practical Interpretation (3 marks in 7)**

In the **biological context**, the analysis reveals:
- **Hormone 3 is the most effective**, producing an average plant height of 19.0 cm — significantly higher than all other hormones.
- **Hormones 1 and 4 are statistically equivalent** (difference = 1.0 cm < LSD), suggesting neither has a meaningful growth-promoting advantage.
- For **agricultural applications**, the plant biologist would recommend **Hormone 3** for maximizing plant growth, while **Hormones 1 and 4** are interchangeable in their effect.

---

### ✦ Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

A clinical trial investigates the effect of **3 different dosages** of a new drug (Low, Medium, High) on **4 different patient age groups** (Blocks: 20–35, 36–50, 51–65, 66+). The blood pressure reduction (mmHg) data is:

| Block (Age) | Low Dose | Medium Dose | High Dose |
|:-----------:|:--------:|:-----------:|:---------:|
| 20–35 | 8 | 12 | 15 |
| 36–50 | 10 | 14 | 18 |
| 51–65 | 12 | 16 | 20 |
| 66+ | 9 | 13 | 17 |

**(a)** Set up the **Two-Way ANOVA table** (Randomized Block Design) and test whether the dosages have a significant effect on blood pressure reduction at $\alpha = 0.05$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Also test whether the blocking by age group is effective in reducing experimental error. Comment on the design. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Complete Model Solution for Question B:

**Part (a) — Two-Way ANOVA with Blocking (7 marks):**

**Step 1: State the Hypotheses**

$$H_{0,\text{dose}}: \mu_L = \mu_M = \mu_H \quad \text{vs.} \quad H_{1,\text{dose}}: \text{Not all dose means equal}$$

**Step 2: Construct Row, Column, and Grand Totals**

Row Totals (Blocks):
$$R_1 = 8+12+15 = 35$$
$$R_2 = 10+14+18 = 42$$
$$R_3 = 12+16+20 = 48$$
$$R_4 = 9+13+17 = 39$$

Column Totals (Treatments):
$$C_1 = 8+10+12+9 = 39$$
$$C_2 = 12+14+16+13 = 55$$
$$C_3 = 15+18+20+17 = 70$$

Grand Total:
$$G = 35+42+48+39 = 164$$

**Step 3: Parameters**

$$b = 4 \text{ (blocks)}, \quad k = 3 \text{ (treatments)}, \quad N = 12$$

**Step 4: Correction Factor**

$$CF = \frac{G^2}{N} = \frac{(164)^2}{12} = \frac{26896}{12} = 2241.333$$

**Step 5: Total Sum of Squares**

$$\sum\sum X_{ij}^2 = (64+144+225) + (100+196+324) + (144+256+400) + (81+169+289)$$

$$= 433 + 620 + 800 + 539 = 2392$$

$$SST = 2392 - 2241.333 = 150.667$$

**Step 6: Sum of Squares — Treatments (Columns)**

$$SSC = \frac{C_1^2 + C_2^2 + C_3^2}{b} - CF = \frac{39^2 + 55^2 + 70^2}{4} - 2241.333$$

$$= \frac{1521 + 3025 + 4900}{4} - 2241.333 = \frac{9446}{4} - 2241.333$$

$$= 2361.5 - 2241.333 = 120.167$$

**Step 7: Sum of Squares — Blocks (Rows)**

$$SSR = \frac{R_1^2 + R_2^2 + R_3^2 + R_4^2}{k} - CF = \frac{35^2 + 42^2 + 48^2 + 39^2}{3} - 2241.333$$

$$= \frac{1225 + 1764 + 2304 + 1521}{3} - 2241.333 = \frac{6814}{3} - 2241.333$$

$$= 2271.333 - 2241.333 = 30.000$$

**Step 8: Sum of Squares — Error**

$$SSE = SST - SSC - SSR = 150.667 - 120.167 - 30.000 = 0.500$$

**Step 9: Degrees of Freedom**

- Treatments: $k - 1 = 2$
- Blocks: $b - 1 = 3$
- Error: $(k-1)(b-1) = 2 \times 3 = 6$
- Total: $N - 1 = 11$

**Step 10: Mean Squares**

$$MSC = \frac{120.167}{2} = 60.083$$
$$MSR = \frac{30.000}{3} = 10.000$$
$$MSE = \frac{0.500}{6} = 0.0833$$

**Step 11: F-Statistics**

$$F_{\text{treatment}} = \frac{MSC}{MSE} = \frac{60.083}{0.0833} = 721.3$$

$$F_{crit}(2, 6, 0.05) = 5.14$$

**Decision:** $721.3 \gg 5.14$ → **Reject $H_0$**. Dosages have a **highly significant effect** on blood pressure reduction.

**Step 12: Two-Way ANOVA Table**

| Source | SS | df | MS | F-cal | F-crit |
|:-------|:--:|:--:|:--:|:-----:|:------:|
| Treatments (Doses) | 120.167 | 2 | 60.083 | 721.3 | 5.14 |
| Blocks (Age) | 30.000 | 3 | 10.000 | 120.0 | 4.76 |
| Error | 0.500 | 6 | 0.0833 | — | — |
| **Total** | **150.667** | **11** | — | — | — |

---

**Part (b) — Effectiveness of Blocking (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Test for Block Effect**

$$H_{0,\text{block}}: \text{All block means are equal (blocking has no effect)}$$

$$F_{\text{block}} = \frac{MSR}{MSE} = \frac{10.000}{0.0833} = 120.0$$

$$F_{crit}(3, 6, 0.05) = 4.76$$

**Decision:** $120.0 \gg 4.76$ → **Reject $H_0$**. The age blocks are **highly significant**.

**Step 2: Comments on the Experimental Design (3 marks)**

1. **Blocking was justified and effective:** Since $F_{\text{block}}$ is large, the age groups differed substantially in their response to the drug. The RBD reduced experimental error dramatically.
2. **Efficiency of RBD over CRD:** The error variance in RBD ($0.0833$) is much smaller than what a CRD would have produced. Without blocking, age-related variation would have been confounded with error.
3. **Practical Implication:** The drug's effect is **dose-dependent** — higher doses produce greater blood pressure reduction. However, since age also significantly affects response, **dosage recommendations should be age-specific** in clinical practice.
4. **The two-way RBD is superior** to CRD here because the experimenter correctly identified a nuisance variable (age) and controlled for it via blocking.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **Wrong CF Formula:** Students often write $CF = T^2$ instead of $CF = T^2 / N$. Each such error costs 1 mark.
> 2. **Forgetting Degrees of Freedom:** ANOVA is incomplete without df. Examiners specifically check $(k-1)$, $(N-k)$, and $(N-1)$.
> 3. **Misinterpretation of F:** A non-significant F does NOT prove means are equal — it only says we lack evidence of a difference. Use "**fail to reject $H_0$**", not "**accept $H_0$**".
> 4. **Using One-Way F-table for Two-Way ANOVA:** The F-critical values differ based on $(v_1, v_2)$ combinations. Always specify both.
> 5. **Confusing $SSC$ and $SSR$:** In one-way ANOVA, $SSC$ (sum of squares between columns/treatments) is the only between-group term. In two-way ANOVA, both $SSC$ (treatments) and $SSR$ (rows/blocks) exist separately.
> 6. **Skipping Practical Interpretation:** KTU examiners award **2-3 marks** for "real-world biological interpretation". Always state the clinical/agricultural implication.
> 7. **Computational Errors in $R^2$ or LSD:** When asked for "which treatments differ", always back it up with the **LSD test or Tukey's HSD**, not just visual inspection.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist — ANOVA Mastery**

### Core Conceptual Points

- **ANOVA tests equality of $k \geq 3$ means** simultaneously, not pairwise.
- **Developed by R.A. Fisher (1923)** for agricultural experiments at Rothamsted.
- The **F-statistic = Variance Between / Variance Within** is the heart of the test.
- **Central Identity:** $SST = SSC + SSE$ — Total = Between + Within.
- **Two key parameters** of F-distribution: $v_1$ (numerator df) and $v_2$ (denominator df).

### Three Assumptions of ANOVA

1. **Normality** of populations
2. **Homogeneity of variances** (homoscedasticity)
3. **Independence** of observations

### Critical Formulae to Memorize

- $CF = T^2 / N$
- $SST = \sum\sum X^2 - CF$
- $SSC = \sum(T_i^2 / n_i) - CF$
- $SSE = SST - SSC$
- $F = [SSC / (k-1)] / [SSE / (N-k)]$

### Decision Rule

- If $F_{cal} > F_{crit}$ → **Reject $H_0$** (means differ)
- If $F_{cal} \leq F_{crit}$ → **Fail to reject $H_0$** (insufficient evidence)

### One-Way vs Two-Way ANOVA

| Feature | One-Way (CRD) | Two-Way (RBD) |
|:--------|:--------------|:--------------|
| Sources of variation | 1 (Treatment) | 2 (Treatment + Block) |
| Error df | $N - k$ | $(k-1)(b-1)$ |
| Efficiency | Lower | Higher (if blocking effective) |

### Common F-Critical Values (Memorize These!)

| $v_1 \backslash v_2$ | 5 | 10 | 20 | $\infty$ |
|:-------------------:|:--:|:--:|:--:|:--------:|
| 2 | 5.79 | 4.10 | 3.49 | 3.00 |
| 3 | 4.39 | 3.71 | 3.10 | 2.60 |
| 4 | 3.84 | 3.48 | 3.01 | 2.37 |

*(At $\alpha = 0.05$)*

### High-Yield Keywords for KTU Answers

- **Partition of total variation**
- **Variance ratio test**
- **Homoscedasticity**
- **Critical difference (CD)**
- **Coefficient of determination $R^2$**
- **Post-hoc tests** (LSD, Tukey HSD, Bonferroni)

> [!TIP]
> **One-Liner for KTU Viva:** *"ANOVA answers the question — is the variability in my data due to a real treatment effect, or is it just random chance? By comparing between-group variance to within-group variance via the F-ratio, we make this distinction with controlled Type I error."*
