topics.title: Completely Randomized Design (One-Way ANOVA)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GDMAT401 - MATHEMATICS FOR LIFE SCIENCE – 4
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Completely Randomized Design (One-Way ANOVA)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Completely Randomized Design (CRD)** is the simplest experimental design in which experimental units (plots, subjects, samples) are assigned to treatments **purely at random**, with no blocking or restriction. Each treatment appears in a pre-determined number of replicates, and the random allocation covers the entire pool of homogeneous experimental units.

**One-Way Analysis of Variance (One-Way ANOVA)** is the statistical procedure used to test whether the means of $k \ge 2$ populations (or treatments) are equal, based on a single categorical independent variable (factor). In the context of CRD, it partitions the total observed variability into:

- **Variation BETWEEN treatments** (due to treatment effect), and
- **Variation WITHIN treatments** (due to random error / residual).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mathematical Model of CRD (One-Way ANOVA Model):**
> $$y_{ij} = \mu + \tau_i + \varepsilon_{ij}$$
> where $i = 1, 2, \ldots, k$ (treatments) and $j = 1, 2, \ldots, n_i$ (replicates).
> - $y_{ij}$ = observed response of the $j^{th}$ unit under the $i^{th}$ treatment.
> - $\mu$ = **overall (grand) mean** (a constant).
> - $\tau_i$ = **true effect** of the $i^{th}$ treatment (with constraint $\sum \tau_i = 0$).
> - $\varepsilon_{ij} \sim N(0, \sigma^2)$ = **random error**, independent and identically normally distributed.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Plant Growth" Intuition

Imagine you are an agricultural scientist testing **4 different fertilizers (A, B, C, D)** on tomato plants to see which produces the highest yield. You have 20 identical, homogeneous plots of land available. To make a fair comparison, you:

1. Pick a fertilizer for each plot **purely by rolling a die** (randomization) — no pattern, no grouping.
2. Replicate: 5 plots per fertilizer, so every fertilizer gets tested 5 times.
3. After harvesting, you get 20 yield values. Some variation will exist even within the *same* fertilizer group (because of soil micro-differences, watering, etc.) — that is the **within-group variation (error)**. Variation *between* fertilizer groups reflects the **treatment effect**.

> If fertilizers are genuinely different, the *between-group* spread will be **noticeably larger** than the *within-group* spread. ANOVA formalizes this "spread comparison" using a single ratio called the **F-statistic**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Completely Randomized"?** Because every experimental unit has an **equal and independent chance** of receiving **any** treatment — there is no blocking, no pairing, and no stratification. This makes CRD statistically elegant but requires the experimental units to be **homogeneous** (uniform).

### 1.3 Hypothesis Framework

- **Null Hypothesis ($H_0$):** All treatment means are equal: $\mu_1 = \mu_2 = \cdots = \mu_k$ (equivalently, $\tau_1 = \tau_2 = \cdots = \tau_k = 0$). No treatment effect.
- **Alternative Hypothesis ($H_1$):** At least one treatment mean differs from the others ($H_0$ is false).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **rejection of $H_0$** does **NOT** tell us *which* treatments differ — it only says *at least one* differs. To identify specific differences, follow-up tests (e.g., **LSD**, **Tukey's HSD**, **Bonferroni**) are required.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Core Logic: "Variance Partitioning"

The fundamental idea of ANOVA is the **additive decomposition of the total sum of squares**:

$$SS_{\text{Total}} = SS_{\text{Treatments}} + SS_{\text{Error}}$$

where every sum of squares measures the *sum of squared deviations* from some mean. The corresponding degrees of freedom also partition additively:

$$(N-1) = (k-1) + (N-k)$$

where $N = \sum_{i=1}^{k} n_i$ is the total number of observations.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Conceptual Flow

1. **Compute the Grand Mean:** $\bar{y}_{..} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{k}\sum_{j=1}^{n_i} y_{ij}}{N}$
2. **Compute Treatment Means:** $\bar{y}_{i.} = \dfrac{\sum_{j=1}^{n_i} y_{ij}}{n_i}$
3. **Compute Correction Factor (CF):** $CF = \dfrac{\left(\sum_{i,j} y_{ij}\right)^2}{N} = \dfrac{G^2}{N}$ where $G$ is the grand total.
4. **Compute Sums of Squares** using the shortcut formulas (efficient for hand calculation).
5. **Convert Sums of Squares to Mean Squares** by dividing by corresponding degrees of freedom.
6. **Form the F-ratio** = $MS_{\text{Treatment}} \div MS_{\text{Error}}$.
7. **Compare F with $F_{k-1,\,N-k,\alpha}$** (tabulated critical value). If $F_{\text{calc}} > F_{\text{crit}}$, reject $H_0$.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is a **high-yield reference** that appears frequently in KTU ESE questions on this topic. Memorize it thoroughly.

| Quantity | Formula | Degrees of Freedom |
|----------|---------|---------------------|
| Correction Factor ($CF$) | $CF = \dfrac{G^2}{N}$ | — |
| Total Sum of Squares ($SS_T$) | $\sum_{i,j} y_{ij}^2 - CF$ | $N-1$ |
| Treatment Sum of Squares ($SS_{Tr}$) | $\sum_{i=1}^{k} \dfrac{T_i^2}{n_i} - CF$ | $k-1$ |
| Error Sum of Squares ($SS_E$) | $SS_T - SS_{Tr}$ | $N-k$ |
| Mean Square Treatment ($MS_{Tr}$) | $\dfrac{SS_{Tr}}{k-1}$ | — |
| Mean Square Error ($MS_E$) | $\dfrac{SS_E}{N-k}$ | — |
| F-statistic | $F = \dfrac{MS_{Tr}}{MS_E}$ | $(k-1,\; N-k)$ |
| Coefficient of Variation (CV) | $\dfrac{\sqrt{MS_E}}{\bar{y}_{..}} \times 100\,\%$ | — |
| Standard Error of Mean | $\sqrt{MS_E/n}$ (for equal $n_i$) | — |
| Critical Difference (CD) at 5% | $t_{0.05,\,N-k} \times \sqrt{\dfrac{2\,MS_E}{n}}$ (equal $n$) | — |

**Notation Conventions Used Above:**
- $G = \sum y_{ij}$ (grand total of all observations)
- $T_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n_i} y_{ij}$ (total of $i^{th}$ treatment)
- $N = \sum n_i$ (grand total of observations)
- $k$ = number of treatments; $n_i$ = replicates for treatment $i$

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering & Life-Science Applications

| Field | Typical Use Case |
|-------|------------------|
| **Agriculture** | Comparing yields of crop varieties under different fertilizers. |
| **Pharmacology** | Testing the efficacy of $k$ drug doses versus a control. |
| **Industrial Quality Control** | Verifying that production shifts produce consistent output. |
| **Biology / Ecology** | Comparing species growth rates under varied environmental conditions. |
| **Education Research** | Comparing student performance under different teaching methods. |

### 2.5 Assumptions Underlying CRD / One-Way ANOVA

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For the F-test in CRD to be **valid**, the following assumptions must hold. KTU questions often test these explicitly.

1. **Additivity & Linearity:** The model $y_{ij} = \mu + \tau_i + \varepsilon_{ij}$ is the correct structural form.
2. **Independence:** Observations are independent of each other.
3. **Normality:** Errors $\varepsilon_{ij}$ are normally distributed: $\varepsilon_{ij} \sim N(0, \sigma^2)$.
4. **Homoscedasticity (Homogeneity of Variance):** All treatments share a common error variance $\sigma^2$.
5. **Homogeneity of Experimental Units:** Units are uniform (no hidden gradients); this justifies *complete* randomization.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 The Decomposition Identity (Algebraic Derivation)

The identity that underpins ANOVA is the **deviation-decomposition identity**. For any observation $y_{ij}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
y_{ij} - \bar{y}_{..} 
&= (y_{ij} - \bar{y}_{i.}) + (\bar{y}_{i.} - \bar{y}_{..})
\end{aligned}
$$

Here $(y_{ij} - \bar{y}_{..})$ is the **deviation of the observation from the grand mean**, $(y_{ij} - \bar{y}_{i.})$ is the **within-treatment deviation** (residual), and $(\bar{y}_{i.} - \bar{y}_{..})$ is the **between-treatment deviation** (treatment effect estimate).

**Squaring and summing over all $i, j$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{i,j}(y_{ij} - \bar{y}_{..})^2 
&= \sum_{i,j}\left[(y_{ij} - \bar{y}_{i.}) + (\bar{y}_{i.} - \bar{y}_{..})\right]^2 \\
&= \sum_{i,j}(y_{ij} - \bar{y}_{i.})^2 + \sum_{i,j}(\bar{y}_{i.} - \bar{y}_{..})^2 + 2\sum_{i,j}(y_{ij} - \bar{y}_{i.})(\bar{y}_{i.} - \bar{y}_{..})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Examining the cross-product term:** For fixed $i$, $\sum_{j=1}^{n_i}(y_{ij} - \bar{y}_{i.}) = 0$, and $(\bar{y}_{i.} - \bar{y}_{..})$ is a constant w.r.t. $j$. Hence:

$$
\begin{aligned}
2\sum_{i,j}(y_{ij} - \bar{y}_{i.})(\bar{y}_{i.} - \bar{y}_{..}) 
&= 2\sum_{i=1}^{k}(\bar{y}_{i.} - \bar{y}_{..})\underbrace{\sum_{j=1}^{n_i}(y_{ij} - \bar{y}_{i.})}_{=\,0} = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Therefore, the celebrated identity holds:**

$$
\boxed{\;\sum_{i,j}(y_{ij} - \bar{y}_{..})^2 \;=\; \sum_{i,j}(y_{ij} - \bar{y}_{i.})^2 \;+\; \sum_{i=1}^{k} n_i(\bar{y}_{i.} - \bar{y}_{..})^2\;}
$$

$$
SS_T = SS_E + SS_{Tr}
$$

> This algebraic identity is what **guarantees the sum-of-squares partition** and hence the validity of ANOVA as an "analysis of variance."

### 3.2 Expected Mean Squares (Under the Model)

Under $H_0$ (no treatment effect, $\tau_i = 0$ for all $i$):

$$
E[MS_E] = \sigma^2, \qquad E[MS_{Tr}] = \sigma^2 + \frac{\sum n_i \tau_i^2}{k-1}
$$

Hence under $H_0$, $E[F] \approx 1$, and rejection of $H_0$ occurs when $F$ is significantly larger than 1.

### 3.3 Numerical Example — Complete Worked Solution

**Problem:** A botanist tests 4 fertilizers (A, B, C, D) on plant growth (cm). Each fertilizer is applied to 5 randomly selected plants. The growth data are:

| Rep | A | B | C | D |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 20 | 25 | 18 | 30 |
| 2 | 22 | 24 | 20 | 29 |
| 3 | 21 | 26 | 19 | 31 |
| 4 | 23 | 23 | 21 | 32 |
| 5 | 19 | 27 | 17 | 28 |
| **Total $T_i$** | **105** | **125** | **95** | **150** |

Test at $\alpha = 0.05$ whether the mean growth differs across fertilizers.

#### Step 1: Identify Parameters
- $k = 4$ treatments, $n_i = 5$ (equal replications), $N = 20$.
- Treatment totals: $T_A = 105,\; T_B = 125,\; T_C = 95,\; T_D = 150$.
- Grand total: $G = 105 + 125 + 95 + 150 = 475$.

#### Step 2: Compute the Correction Factor

$$
CF = \frac{G^2}{N} = \frac{475^2}{20} = \frac{225625}{20} = 11281.25
$$

#### Step 3: Compute $\sum y_{ij}^2$ (Total of Squared Observations)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Fertilizer A: } & 20^2+22^2+21^2+23^2+19^2 = 400+484+441+529+361 = 2215 \\
\text{Fertilizer B: } & 25^2+24^2+26^2+23^2+27^2 = 625+576+676+529+729 = 3135 \\
\text{Fertilizer C: } & 18^2+20^2+19^2+21^2+17^2 = 324+400+361+441+289 = 1815 \\
\text{Fertilizer D: } & 30^2+29^2+31^2+32^2+28^2 = 900+841+961+1024+784 = 4510
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\sum y_{ij}^2 = 2215 + 3135 + 1815 + 4510 = 11675
$$

#### Step 4: Total Sum of Squares

$$
SS_T = \sum y_{ij}^2 - CF = 11675 - 11281.25 = 393.75
$$

#### Step 5: Treatment Sum of Squares

$$
\begin{aligned}
SS_{Tr} &= \sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{T_i^2}{n_i} - CF \\
&= \frac{105^2}{5} + \frac{125^2}{5} + \frac{95^2}{5} + \frac{150^2}{5} - 11281.25 \\
&= \frac{11025}{5} + \frac{15625}{5} + \frac{9025}{5} + \frac{22500}{5} - 11281.25 \\
&= 2205 + 3125 + 1805 + 4500 - 11281.25 \\
&= 11635 - 11281.25 = 353.75
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 6: Error Sum of Squares

$$
SS_E = SS_T - SS_{Tr} = 393.75 - 353.75 = 40.00
$$

#### Step 7: Degrees of Freedom

$$
df_T = N - 1 = 19, \quad df_{Tr} = k - 1 = 3, \quad df_E = N - k = 16
$$

#### Step 8: Mean Squares

$$
MS_{Tr} = \frac{SS_{Tr}}{df_{Tr}} = \frac{353.75}{3} = 117.9167
$$

$$
MS_E = \frac{SS_E}{df_E} = \frac{40}{16} = 2.5
$$

#### Step 9: F-Statistic

$$
F_{calc} = \frac{MS_{Tr}}{MS_E} = \frac{117.9167}{2.5} = 47.1667
$$

#### Step 10: Tabulated Critical Value

At $\alpha = 0.05$ with $(3, 16)$ degrees of freedom: $F_{0.05,\,3,\,16} = 3.24$.

#### Step 11: Decision

Since $F_{calc} = 47.17 \gg F_{crit} = 3.24$, we **reject $H_0$**.

**Conclusion:** There is **highly significant evidence** (at the 5% level) that the mean plant growth differs across at least two of the four fertilizers.

#### Step 12: ANOVA Table (Final Consolidated Form)

| Source of Variation | SS | df | MS | F-ratio |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Between Treatments (Fertilizers) | 353.75 | 3 | 117.92 | **47.17** |
| Within Treatments (Error) | 40.00 | 16 | 2.50 | |
| **Total** | **393.75** | **19** | | |

#### Step 13: Post-hoc Comparison (Critical Difference)

For equal replications $n = 5$:

$$
CD_{0.05} = t_{0.05,\,16} \times \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot MS_E}{n}} = 2.120 \times \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 2.5}{5}} = 2.120 \times 1.0 = 2.12
$$

Treatment means: $\bar{A}=21.0,\; \bar{B}=25.0,\; \bar{C}=19.0,\; \bar{D}=30.0$.

Any two means differing by **more than 2.12 cm** are significantly different. This identifies specific pairs (e.g., D vs. C differ by 11.0, hence significant).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Block Diagram — ANOVA Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rawData["Raw Observations y_ij"] --> sumSq["Compute Correction Factor CF = G^2 / N"]
    rawData --> ssTotal["Compute SS_Total = Sum of y_ij^2 minus CF"]
    rawData --> trMeans["Compute Treatment Totals T_i and Means"]
    trMeans --> ssTr["Compute SS_Treatment = Sum of T_i^2 / n_i minus CF"]
    ssTotal --> ssErr["Compute SS_Error = SS_Total minus SS_Treatment"]
    ssTr --> msTr["Mean Square Treatment MS_Tr = SS_Tr divided by k minus 1"]
    ssErr --> msErr["Mean Square Error MS_E = SS_E divided by N minus k"]
    msTr --> fStat["F-statistic = MS_Tr divided by MS_E"]
    msErr --> fStat
    fStat --> decision["Compare F_calc with F_critical from F-table"]
    decision --> acceptH0["If F_calc less than F_crit: Fail to reject H_0"]
    decision --> rejectH0["If F_calc greater than F_crit: Reject H_0"]
    acceptH0 --> conclusion1["All treatment means statistically equal"]
    rejectH0 --> conclusion2["At least one treatment mean differs"]
    conclusion2 --> postHoc["Apply post-hoc test LSD or Tukey HSD"]
```

### 4.2 Variance Partition Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph totalVar["Total Variability in Data SS_T"]
        direction TB
        subgraph betweenVar["Between Treatments SS_Tr - df = k minus 1"]
            treatEffect["Captures the effect of treatments on the response"]
        end
        subgraph withinVar["Within Treatments SS_E - df = N minus k"]
            randomError["Captures random experimental error and noise"]
        end
    end
    fRatioBlock["F-ratio = MS_Tr / MS_E"] --> comparisonNode["If F is large, treatment effect dominates error"]
    comparisonNode --> interpretation["Reject H_0 at significance level alpha"]
    betweenVar --> fRatioBlock
    withinVar --> fRatioBlock
```

### 4.3 Hypothesis Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode["Begin CRD One-Way ANOVA Test"] --> stateH0["State H_0: All treatment means equal"]
    startNode --> stateH1["State H_1: At least one treatment mean differs"]
    stateH0 --> chooseAlpha["Choose significance level alpha = 0.05"]
    stateH1 --> chooseAlpha
    chooseAlpha --> computeF["Compute F_calc from data using ANOVA table"]
    computeF --> lookupF["Look up F_critical in F-table with df_Tr and df_E"]
    lookupF --> branchNode{"Is F_calc greater than F_critical?"}
    branchNode --> yesPath["Yes: Reject H_0"]
    branchNode --> noPath["No: Fail to reject H_0"]
    yesPath --> followup["Conduct post-hoc test to identify differing pairs"]
    noPath --> stopInf["Conclude no significant treatment effect detected"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**
*State the mathematical model of a Completely Randomized Design for one-way ANOVA. What is the underlying assumption about the error term?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The model is $y_{ij} = \mu + \tau_i + \varepsilon_{ij}$, where $i = 1, 2, \ldots, k$ and $j = 1, 2, \ldots, n_i$. Here, $\mu$ is the overall mean, $\tau_i$ is the effect of the $i^{th}$ treatment subject to $\sum_{i=1}^k \tau_i = 0$, and $\varepsilon_{ij}$ is the random error. **Assumption:** $\varepsilon_{ij} \sim N(0, \sigma^2)$, i.e., the errors are **independent, normally distributed with mean zero and constant variance $\sigma^2$** (homoscedasticity).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Jun 2024, Sample Paper 2]**
*What are the degrees of freedom associated with Treatment, Error, and Total Sum of Squares in a CRD with $k = 5$ treatments and $n = 4$ replicates per treatment?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- $df_{\text{Treatment}} = k - 1 = 5 - 1 = 4$
- $df_{\text{Error}} = N - k = (5 \times 4) - 5 = 20 - 5 = 15$
- $df_{\text{Total}} = N - 1 = 20 - 1 = 19$

Verification: $4 + 15 = 19$ ✓ (additivity holds).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, ESE Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Adapted]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Define a Completely Randomized Design. Explain the assumptions underlying the one-way ANOVA model $y_{ij} = \mu + \tau_i + \varepsilon_{ij}$ and derive the relation $SS_T = SS_{Tr} + SS_E$.

**(b) [7 marks]** Three different teaching methods were tried on randomly selected groups of 6 students each. The final scores were:

| Method I | Method II | Method III |
|---|---|---|
| 75 | 82 | 68 |
| 70 | 78 | 72 |
| 78 | 85 | 65 |
| 72 | 80 | 70 |
| 74 | 84 | 67 |
| 76 | 79 | 69 |

Test at the 5% level whether the three methods differ significantly in mean score. (Tabulated $F_{0.05,\,2,\,15} = 3.68$).

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**
CRD is a design where experimental units are assigned to treatments by **pure randomization** without any blocking. The one-way ANOVA model decomposes each observation into a grand mean, a treatment effect, and a random error.

**Assumptions:**
1. $y_{ij}$ values are independent.
2. Errors are normally distributed: $\varepsilon_{ij} \sim N(0, \sigma^2)$.
3. Homogeneity of variances across treatments.
4. Additivity of model effects.
5. Experimental units are homogeneous (uniform).

**Derivation of $SS_T = SS_{Tr} + SS_E$:**
Starting from $y_{ij} - \bar{y}_{..} = (y_{ij} - \bar{y}_{i.}) + (\bar{y}_{i.} - \bar{y}_{..})$, squaring and summing, the cross-product term vanishes (since $\sum_j (y_{ij} - \bar{y}_{i.}) = 0$), yielding the identity $\sum_{i,j}(y_{ij} - \bar{y}_{..})^2 = \sum_{i,j}(y_{ij} - \bar{y}_{i.})^2 + \sum_i n_i(\bar{y}_{i.} - \bar{y}_{..})^2$, i.e., $SS_T = SS_E + SS_{Tr}$. *(Full algebraic expansion is given in Section 3.1.)*

**Part (b) — 7 marks — Step-by-step Computation:**

**Step 1:** $k = 3$ treatments, $n_i = 6$, $N = 18$.

**Step 2 — Treatment Totals:**
$T_1 = 75+70+78+72+74+76 = 445$, $T_2 = 82+78+85+80+84+79 = 488$, $T_3 = 68+72+65+70+67+69 = 411$.

**Step 3 — Grand Total and CF:**
$G = 445 + 488 + 411 = 1344$.

$$
CF = \frac{G^2}{N} = \frac{1344^2}{18} = \frac{1806336}{18} = 100352.00
$$

**Step 4 — Total of Squared Observations:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Method I: } & 75^2+70^2+78^2+72^2+74^2+76^2 = 5625+4900+6084+5184+5476+5776 = 33045 \\
\text{Method II: } & 82^2+78^2+85^2+80^2+84^2+79^2 = 6724+6084+7225+6400+7056+6241 = 39730 \\
\text{Method III: } & 68^2+72^2+65^2+70^2+67^2+69^2 = 4624+5184+4225+4900+4489+4761 = 28183
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\sum y^2 = 33045 + 39730 + 28183 = 100958
$$

**Step 5 — Sum of Squares:**

$$
SS_T = 100958 - 100352 = 606
$$

$$
SS_{Tr} = \frac{445^2}{6} + \frac{488^2}{6} + \frac{411^2}{6} - 100352
$$

$$
= \frac{198025 + 238144 + 168921}{6} - 100352 = \frac{605090}{6} - 100352 = 100848.33 - 100352 = 496.33
$$

$$
SS_E = 606 - 496.33 = 109.67
$$

**Step 6 — ANOVA Table:**

| Source | SS | df | MS | F |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Between Methods | 496.33 | 2 | 248.17 | **10.91** |
| Within Methods (Error) | 109.67 | 15 | 7.31 | |
| **Total** | **606.00** | **17** | | |

**Step 7 — Decision:**
$F_{calc} = 10.91 > F_{crit} = 3.68$. **Reject $H_0$**.

**Conclusion:** The three teaching methods produce significantly different mean student scores at the 5% level of significance.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Jun 2023 Adapted]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the meaning of "treatment effect" $\tau_i$ in one-way ANOVA. How are the hypotheses stated in terms of $\tau_i$? Discuss the limitations of one-way ANOVA.

**(b) [7 marks]** A researcher compares 4 varieties of rice on yield (kg/plot). Each variety is grown in 5 randomly chosen plots. The yields are tabulated below:

| Variety 1 | Variety 2 | Variety 3 | Variety 4 |
|---|---|---|---|
| 22 | 28 | 18 | 25 |
| 25 | 30 | 20 | 27 |
| 24 | 27 | 19 | 26 |
| 23 | 29 | 21 | 28 |
| 26 | 31 | 17 | 24 |

Perform one-way ANOVA and test at the 1% level whether varieties differ in mean yield. Given $F_{0.01,\,3,\,16} = 5.29$.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**
The treatment effect $\tau_i = \mu_i - \mu$ measures the deviation of the $i^{th}$ treatment mean from the overall grand mean. Under the constraint $\sum_{i=1}^k \tau_i = 0$, these effects are interpretable as "additive" deviations.

**Hypotheses in terms of $\tau_i$:** $H_0: \tau_1 = \tau_2 = \cdots = \tau_k = 0$ (all treatment effects are zero) vs. $H_1$: at least one $\tau_i \neq 0$.

**Limitations of one-way ANOVA:**
1. Examines only one factor at a time; cannot study interactions.
2. Requires equal variances (homoscedasticity); sensitive to outliers.
3. Requires balanced design (or near-balance) for robustness.
4. Assumes additivity of effects — interaction terms not modeled.
5. Rejection of $H_0$ does not pinpoint *which* treatments differ; post-hoc tests needed.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1:** $k = 4$, $n_i = 5$, $N = 20$. Totals: $T_1 = 120$, $T_2 = 145$, $T_3 = 95$, $T_4 = 130$, $G = 490$.

**Step 2 — CF:**

$$
CF = \frac{490^2}{20} = \frac{240100}{20} = 12005.00
$$

**Step 3 — Total of Squares:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{V}_1: 22^2+25^2+24^2+23^2+26^2 &= 484+625+576+529+676 = 2890 \\
\text{V}_2: 28^2+30^2+27^2+29^2+31^2 &= 784+900+729+841+961 = 4215 \\
\text{V}_3: 18^2+20^2+19^2+21^2+17^2 &= 324+400+361+441+289 = 1815 \\
\text{V}_4: 25^2+27^2+26^2+28^2+24^2 &= 625+729+676+784+576 = 3390
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\sum y^2 = 2890 + 4215 + 1815 + 3390 = 12310
$$

**Step 4 — Sums of Squares:**

$$
SS_T = 12310 - 12005 = 305
$$

$$
SS_{Tr} = \frac{120^2 + 145^2 + 95^2 + 130^2}{5} - 12005
$$

$$
= \frac{14400 + 21025 + 9025 + 16900}{5} - 12005 = \frac{61350}{5} - 12005 = 12270 - 12005 = 265
$$

$$
SS_E = 305 - 265 = 40
$$

**Step 5 — ANOVA Table:**

| Source | SS | df | MS | F |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Between Varieties | 265 | 3 | 88.33 | **35.33** |
| Within Varieties (Error) | 40 | 16 | 2.50 | |
| **Total** | **305** | **19** | | |

**Step 6 — Decision:**
$F_{calc} = 35.33 > F_{crit} = 5.29$ at $\alpha = 0.01$. **Reject $H_0$**.

**Conclusion:** The four rice varieties differ **highly significantly** in mean yield at the 1% level of significance.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the correction factor (CF):** Always subtract $CF$ in $SS_T$ and $SS_{Tr}$ — examiners award 0 if missed.
> 2. **Confusing $\sum y^2$ with $\left(\sum y\right)^2$:** These are completely different; the former is a sum of squares, the latter is the square of the sum (used in $CF$).
> 3. **Mixing up $k$ and $N$:** $k$ is the number of treatments; $N$ is total observations. $df_{Tr} = k-1$, $df_E = N-k$.
> 4. **Forgetting to state the decision in context:** Always conclude with "Since $F_{calc} > F_{crit}$ at $\alpha = \ldots$, we reject $H_0$, hence there is a significant difference..." — bare $F$ values without interpretation fetch partial marks only.
> 5. **Not citing tabulated $F$-value:** A numerical comparison without the table value is incomplete.
> 6. **Decimal arithmetic errors in CF:** A single rounding mistake propagates throughout. Use exact fractions where possible.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Completely Randomized Design (One-Way ANOVA)**

- **Model Equation:** $y_{ij} = \mu + \tau_i + \varepsilon_{ij}$ with $\varepsilon_{ij} \sim N(0, \sigma^2)$ and $\sum \tau_i = 0$.
- **Key Identity:** $SS_T = SS_{Tr} + SS_E$ — this is the *defining* property of ANOVA.
- **Correction Factor:** $CF = G^2 / N$ — used in BOTH $SS_T$ and $SS_{Tr}$ formulas.
- **Shortcut for $SS_{Tr}$:** $\sum (T_i^2 / n_i) - CF$ — efficient and examiner-friendly.
- **Degrees of Freedom:** $N-1$ (Total) = $(k-1)$ (Treatment) + $(N-k)$ (Error).
- **F-Ratio Construction:** $F = MS_{Tr} / MS_E$ — always treatment MS divided by error MS.
- **Hypotheses:** $H_0: \mu_1 = \mu_2 = \cdots = \mu_k$ vs. $H_1$: at least one pair differs.
- **Decision Rule:** Reject $H_0$ if $F_{calc} > F_{k-1,\,N-k,\,\alpha}$.
- **Assumptions:** Independence, Normality, Homoscedasticity, Additivity, Homogeneous units.
- **ANOVA Table:** Standard format: Source | SS | df | MS | F. Memorize the column order.
- **Coefficient of Variation:** $CV = \sqrt{MS_E} / \bar{y}_{..} \times 100\%$ — used to assess experimental precision.
- **Critical Difference (CD):** Used for pairwise comparison of treatment means; $CD = t_{\alpha,\,N-k} \cdot \sqrt{2 MS_E / n}$ for equal $n$.
- **Limitation of CRD:** Requires uniform experimental material; if there is a known gradient (e.g., soil fertility variation), use a **Randomized Block Design (RBD)** instead.
- **Don't Confuse:** "Rejection of $H_0$" ≠ "All treatments differ" — it only means *at least one* differs.
- **Post-Hoc Tests:** LSD, Tukey's HSD, Duncan's Multiple Range Test, Bonferroni — used only AFTER a significant overall F-test.
- **Examiner-Friendly Tips:** Always show the CF step, present a clean ANOVA table, state $F_{calc}$ and $F_{crit}$ explicitly, and conclude in plain language.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Randomized-Block Design (Two-Way ANOVA)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GDMAT401 - MATHEMATICS FOR LIFE SCIENCE – 4
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Randomized-Block Design (Two-Way ANOVA)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Randomized Block Design (RBD)** is a fundamental experimental design in statistical analysis that falls under the framework of **Two-Way Analysis of Variance (ANOVA)**. It is a design strategy used to control for the effect of one or more nuisance variables (extraneous sources of variability) by grouping experimental units into homogeneous blocks. Each block receives all treatments in a randomized order, ensuring that the treatment effect is estimated free from the block effect.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus-Ready Definition (KTU 2024):**
> *Randomized Block Design is a two-way layout design in which the experimental material is divided into groups called 'blocks' that are internally homogeneous, and the treatments are randomly assigned to the experimental units within each block. It is used to eliminate the effect of known but uncontrollable sources of variation, thereby increasing the precision of treatment comparison.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine you are a **biological researcher** testing the effect of **3 different fertilizers (A, B, C)** on the growth of a medicinal plant. You know that **soil fertility varies** across your experimental field — some patches are richer, some are poorer. This soil variation is an unwanted source of noise that could mask the true effect of the fertilizers.

**Analogy — "The Classroom Exam Scenario":**
- Consider 5 different schools (blocks) where each school has students of **similar intelligence level** internally but **different average ability** between schools.
- You want to test **3 teaching methods** (treatments).
- If you simply assign methods randomly across ALL students (CRD — Completely Randomized Design), the school effect will create huge variability.
- **Better Approach (RBD):** Within EACH school, randomly assign the 3 methods. Now, the school-to-school variation is "absorbed" by the block, and you can fairly compare the methods.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Core Intuition:** Blocks act as "filters" that remove known background noise. RBD is essentially a way of saying — *"Compare treatments, but first strip away the known background variation."*

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The RBD framework relies on the following **statistical constants** (standard reference values used in F-distribution tables):

| Constant | Description | Typical Range |
|:--------:|:-----------:|:-------------:|
| $\alpha$ (Level of Significance) | Probability of Type I Error | **0.05** or **0.01** |
| $F_{critical}$ | Tabulated F-value at given df | Read from F-tables |
| $t$ | Number of treatments | Variable |
| $b$ | Number of blocks (replications) | Variable |
| $N$ | Total observations = $t \times b$ | Fixed |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mathematical Model of RBD

The RBD model is a **fixed-effects (Model I) two-way ANOVA model** with one observation per cell:

$$Y_{ij} = \mu + \tau_i + \beta_j + \varepsilon_{ij}$$

Where the components are:

- $Y_{ij}$ = Observation from the $i$-th treatment in the $j$-th block
- $\mu$ = **Grand mean** (overall population mean)
- $\tau_i$ = Effect of the $i$-th treatment, for $i = 1, 2, \ldots, t$
- $\beta_j$ = Effect of the $j$-th block, for $j = 1, 2, \ldots, b$
- $\varepsilon_{ij}$ = Random error component, assumed $\varepsilon_{ij} \sim N(0, \sigma^2)$ i.i.d.

### 2.2 Constraints on the Model

For the model to be **identifiable**, two constraints are imposed:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{t} \tau_i = 0 \quad \text{(sum of treatment effects is zero)}$$

$$\sum_{j=1}^{b} \beta_j = 0 \quad \text{(sum of block effects is zero)}$$

### 2.3 Fundamental Assumptions of RBD

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Four Pillars of RBD Validity (Board-Favorite):**

1. **Additivity:** Treatment and block effects are additive (no interaction).
2. **Normality:** Errors $\varepsilon_{ij}$ are normally distributed with mean zero.
3. **Homogeneity of Variance:** Error variance is constant across treatments (homoscedasticity).
4. **Independence:** Errors are mutually independent.

### 2.4 Hypothesis Testing Framework

| Hypothesis | Statement | Interpretation |
|:----------:|:---------:|:--------------:|
| $H_0$ (Treatments) | $\tau_1 = \tau_2 = \cdots = \tau_t = 0$ | All treatments have equal effect |
| $H_1$ (Treatments) | At least one $\tau_i \neq 0$ | At least one treatment differs |
| $H_0$ (Blocks) | $\beta_1 = \beta_2 = \cdots = \beta_b = 0$ | Blocks have no significant effect |
| $H_1$ (Blocks) | At least one $\beta_j \neq 0$ | At least one block differs |

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following **canonical formulas** are mandatory for KTU exam solving:

| Symbol | Formula | Description |
|:------:|:-------:|:-----------:|
| $G$ | $\sum_{i,j} Y_{ij}$ | Grand Total of all observations |
| $T_i$ | $\sum_{j=1}^{b} Y_{ij}$ | Total for $i$-th treatment |
| $B_j$ | $\sum_{i=1}^{t} Y_{ij}$ | Total for $j$-th block |
| $CF$ | $\dfrac{G^2}{N}$ where $N = t \times b$ | Correction Factor |
| $SS_{Total}$ | $\sum_{i=1}^{t}\sum_{j=1}^{b} Y_{ij}^{2} - CF$ | Total Sum of Squares |
| $SS_{Tr}$ | $\dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{t} T_i^{2}}{b} - CF$ | Sum of Squares (Treatments) |
| $SS_{Bl}$ | $\dfrac{\sum_{j=1}^{b} B_j^{2}}{t} - CF$ | Sum of Squares (Blocks) |
| $SS_{E}$ | $SS_{Total} - SS_{Tr} - SS_{Bl}$ | Sum of Squares (Error) |
| $MS_{Tr}$ | $\dfrac{SS_{Tr}}{t-1}$ | Mean Square (Treatments) |
| $MS_{Bl}$ | $\dfrac{SS_{Bl}}{b-1}$ | Mean Square (Blocks) |
| $MS_{E}$ | $\dfrac{SS_{E}}{(t-1)(b-1)}$ | Mean Square (Error) |
| $F_{Tr}$ | $\dfrac{MS_{Tr}}{MS_{E}}$ | F-ratio for treatments |
| $F_{Bl}$ | $\dfrac{MS_{Bl}}{MS_{E}}$ | F-ratio for blocks |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering & Life Science Applications

> [!NOTE]
> **Why RBD matters in Life Science Engineering:**

- **Agricultural Field Trials:** Comparing crop varieties across plots of differing fertility.
- **Pharmaceutical Drug Testing:** Comparing drug efficacy across different hospitals (blocks).
- **Clinical Trials:** Comparing treatments across patients grouped by age or disease stage.
- **Biotechnology:** Testing growth media across batches of cells from different donors.
- **Food Science:** Comparing preservation methods across production days.

The RBD reduces the **error variance** by absorbing the variability due to blocks, which leads to a **more powerful F-test** with higher sensitivity to detect real treatment differences.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Sum of Squares

Starting from the linear model $Y_{ij} = \mu + \tau_i + \beta_j + \varepsilon_{ij}$, define the correction factor as the squared grand total divided by total observations:

$$CF = \frac{G^2}{N} = \frac{\left(\sum_{i=1}^{t}\sum_{j=1}^{b} Y_{ij}\right)^2}{tb}$$

**Step 1:** The total sum of squares measures variability of each observation from the grand mean:

$$SS_{Total} = \sum_{i=1}^{t}\sum_{j=1}^{b} (Y_{ij} - \bar{Y}_{..})^2$$

**Step 2:** Algebraically expand this sum of squares (subtracting and adding $\bar{Y}_{i.}$ and $\bar{Y}_{.j}$):

$$SS_{Total} = \sum_{i,j}(Y_{ij} - \bar{Y}_{..})^2 = \sum_{i,j}Y_{ij}^2 - \frac{G^2}{N}$$

**Step 3:** Treatment sum of squares — measures variation between treatment means:

$$SS_{Tr} = b \sum_{i=1}^{t}(\bar{Y}_{i.} - \bar{Y}_{..})^2 = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{t} T_i^2}{b} - CF$$

**Step 4:** Block sum of squares — measures variation between block means:

$$SS_{Bl} = t \sum_{j=1}^{b}(\bar{Y}_{.j} - \bar{Y}_{..})^2 = \frac{\sum_{j=1}^{b} B_j^2}{t} - CF$$

**Step 5:** Error sum of squares by the partition principle:

$$SS_{E} = SS_{Total} - SS_{Tr} - SS_{Bl}$$

### 3.2 Degrees of Freedom Analysis

The total degrees of freedom $N-1$ partitions as:

$$(tb - 1) = (t - 1) + (b - 1) + (t-1)(b-1)$$

| Source | Degrees of Freedom |
|:------:|:------------------:|
| Treatments | $t - 1$ |
| Blocks | $b - 1$ |
| Error | $(t-1)(b-1)$ |
| **Total** | $tb - 1$ |

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Complete Walkthrough)

**Problem Statement:** A biologist compares 3 fertilizers (A, B, C) on plant growth using an RBD with 4 blocks. The yield (grams) data is:

| Fertilizer | Block I | Block II | Block III | Block IV | $T_i$ |
|:----------:|:-------:|:--------:|:---------:|:--------:|:-----:|
| A | 12 | 14 | 13 | 15 | 54 |
| B | 15 | 17 | 16 | 18 | 66 |
| C | 14 | 12 | 11 | 13 | 50 |
| $B_j$ | 41 | 43 | 40 | 46 | $G = 170$ |

**Step 1 — Compute the Grand Total and Correction Factor:**

$$G = 12 + 14 + 13 + 15 + 15 + 17 + 16 + 18 + 14 + 12 + 11 + 13 = 170$$

$$N = t \times b = 3 \times 4 = 12$$

$$CF = \frac{G^2}{N} = \frac{(170)^2}{12} = \frac{28900}{12} = 2408.333$$

**Step 2 — Compute $SS_{Total}$:**

First, square each observation and sum them:

$$\sum Y_{ij}^2 = 144 + 196 + 169 + 225 + 225 + 289 + 256 + 324 + 196 + 144 + 121 + 169 = 2458$$

$$SS_{Total} = 2458 - 2408.333 = 49.667$$

**Step 3 — Compute $SS_{Tr}$ (Treatment Sum of Squares):**

$$\sum T_i^2 = (54)^2 + (66)^2 + (50)^2 = 2916 + 4356 + 2500 = 9772$$

$$SS_{Tr} = \frac{9772}{4} - 2408.333 = 2443.000 - 2408.333 = 34.667$$

**Step 4 — Compute $SS_{Bl}$ (Block Sum of Squares):**

$$\sum B_j^2 = (41)^2 + (43)^2 + (40)^2 + (46)^2 = 1681 + 1849 + 1600 + 2116 = 7246$$

$$SS_{Bl} = \frac{7246}{3} - 2408.333 = 2415.333 - 2408.333 = 7.000$$

**Step 5 — Compute $SS_{Error}$:**

$$SS_{E} = SS_{Total} - SS_{Tr} - SS_{Bl} = 49.667 - 34.667 - 7.000 = 8.000$$

**Step 6 — Construct the ANOVA Table:**

| Source of Variation | Sum of Squares | df | Mean Square | F-ratio | $F_{critical}$ (5%) |
|:-------------------:|:--------------:|:--:|:-----------:|:-------:|:-------------------:|
| Treatments (Fertilizers) | 34.667 | 2 | 17.333 | 13.00 | 5.14 |
| Blocks | 7.000 | 3 | 2.333 | 1.75 | 4.76 |
| Error | 8.000 | 6 | 1.333 | — | — |
| **Total** | **49.667** | **11** | — | — | — |

**Step 7 — Mean Square Calculations:**

$$MS_{Tr} = \frac{34.667}{2} = 17.333$$

$$MS_{Bl} = \frac{7.000}{3} = 2.333$$

$$MS_{E} = \frac{8.000}{6} = 1.333$$

**Step 8 — F-ratio Calculations:**

$$F_{Tr} = \frac{MS_{Tr}}{MS_{E}} = \frac{17.333}{1.333} \approx 13.00$$

$$F_{Bl} = \frac{MS_{Bl}}{MS_{E}} = \frac{2.333}{1.333} \approx 1.75$$

**Step 9 — Decision Rule and Conclusion:**

Since $F_{Tr} = 13.00 > F_{0.05, 2, 6} = 5.14$, we **reject $H_0$** for treatments.
The three fertilizers produce **significantly different yields** at the 5% level.

Since $F_{Bl} = 1.75 < F_{0.05, 3, 6} = 4.76$, we **do not reject $H_0$** for blocks.
The blocks do not show significant variation in this experiment.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 RBD Analysis Flowchart

The following block-level functional architecture depicts the procedural flow of conducting a Randomized Block Design analysis:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA["Start: Collect Experimental Data in RBD Layout"] --> nodeB["Compute Grand Total G and CF"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Compute SS Total by Summing Squared Observations minus CF"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Compute SS Treatments using Treatment Totals T_i"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Compute SS Blocks using Block Totals B_j"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["Derive SS Error as SS Total minus SS Treatments minus SS Blocks"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["Construct ANOVA Table with Degrees of Freedom"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["Compute Mean Squares MS by Dividing SS by df"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["Compute F Ratios for Treatments and Blocks"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ["Retrieve F Critical Values from F Table at alpha 0.05"]
    nodeJ --> nodeK{"Compare F Calculated with F Critical"}
    nodeK -->|"F greater than F critical"| nodeL["Reject H0: Significant Difference Exists"]
    nodeK -->|"F less than or equal to F critical"| nodeM["Do Not Reject H0: No Significant Difference"]
    nodeL --> nodeN["End: Report Treatment Comparison and Block Influence"]
    nodeM --> nodeN["End: Report Treatment Comparison and Block Influence"]
```

### 4.2 Sources of Variation in RBD

The following diagram shows the partition of total variability in the RBD framework:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootA["Total Variability SS Total"] --> nodeB["SS Treatments Between Treatment Means"]
    rootA --> nodeC["SS Blocks Between Block Means"]
    rootA --> nodeD["SS Error Residual Variation"]
    nodeB --> nodeE["df equals t minus 1"]
    nodeC --> nodeF["df equals b minus 1"]
    nodeD --> nodeG["df equals t minus 1 times b minus 1"]
    nodeE --> nodeH["Used to Test Treatment Differences"]
    nodeF --> nodeI["Used to Test Block Homogeneity"]
    nodeG --> nodeJ["Denominator for F Tests"]
    nodeH --> nodeK["F equals MS Treatments over MS Error"]
    nodeI --> nodeL["F equals MS Blocks over MS Error"]
    nodeJ --> nodeK
    nodeJ --> nodeL
```

### 4.3 Logical Layout of an RBD Experiment

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    block1["Block 1 Homogeneous Units"] --> t1["Treatment A"]
    block1 --> t2["Treatment B"]
    block1 --> t3["Treatment C"]
    block2["Block 2 Homogeneous Units"] --> t1
    block2 --> t2
    block2 --> t3
    block3["Block 3 Homogeneous Units"] --> t1
    block3 --> t2
    block3 --> t3
    block4["Block 4 Homogeneous Units"] --> t1
    block4 --> t2
    block4 --> t3
    t1 --> obs1["Observation Y_ij"]
    t2 --> obs2["Observation Y_ij"]
    t3 --> obs3["Observation Y_ij"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Layout:** In an RBD, every treatment appears **exactly once** in every block. The randomization happens *within* each block, not across the whole experiment. This is the defining structural feature of RBD.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]

**State the mathematical model of a Randomized Block Design and explain the terms involved.**

**Model Answer:**

The linear model for a Randomized Block Design with one observation per cell is:

$$Y_{ij} = \mu + \tau_i + \beta_j + \varepsilon_{ij}$$

Where:

- $Y_{ij}$ = observation in the $i$-th treatment and $j$-th block
- $\mu$ = overall general mean
- $\tau_i$ = effect of $i$-th treatment, with $\sum \tau_i = 0$
- $\beta_j$ = effect of $j$-th block, with $\sum \beta_j = 0$
- $\varepsilon_{ij}$ = random error, assumed $\varepsilon_{ij} \sim N(0, \sigma^2)$

**Total Marks Distribution:** Definition (1) + Model equation (1) + Term explanation (1).

---

#### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]

**List any four assumptions underlying the Randomized Block Design.**

**Model Answer:**

The four key assumptions of RBD are:

1. **Additivity:** Treatment and block effects are additive; there is no interaction between treatments and blocks.
2. **Normality:** The error terms $\varepsilon_{ij}$ are normally distributed with mean zero.
3. **Homogeneity of Variance:** The error variance $\sigma^2$ is constant across all treatment-block combinations.
4. **Independence:** The error components are mutually independent and uncorrelated.

**Total Marks Distribution:** Each assumption (0.75 marks × 4).

---

### PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each)

#### QUESTION A [KTU University Exam - 2024 Model] (14 Marks)

**(a)** Distinguish between Completely Randomized Design (CRD) and Randomized Block Design (RBD). Mention **three advantages** of RBD over CRD. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An agricultural scientist compares **4 varieties of rice** using an RBD with **5 blocks**. The yield data (in kg per plot) is given below. Perform the ANOVA and test whether the varieties differ significantly at the **5% level of significance**. **(7 Marks)**

| Variety | $B_1$ | $B_2$ | $B_3$ | $B_4$ | $B_5$ |
|:-------:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|
| $V_1$ | 22 | 24 | 20 | 21 | 23 |
| $V_2$ | 28 | 27 | 26 | 25 | 29 |
| $V_3$ | 19 | 21 | 18 | 20 | 22 |
| $V_4$ | 25 | 26 | 24 | 27 | 28 |

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) CRD vs RBD and Advantages of RBD:**

| Feature | CRD | RBD |
|:-------:|:---:|:---:|
| Blocking | No blocking used | Experimental units grouped into blocks |
| Source of variation | Only treatment and error | Treatment, block, and error |
| df for error | $N - t$ | $(t-1)(b-1)$ |
| Use | Homogeneous conditions | Heterogeneous conditions with known variability |
| Precision | Lower | Higher |

**Three Advantages of RBD over CRD:**

1. **Increased Precision:** By controlling the block effect, the error variance is reduced, leading to a more precise comparison of treatments.
2. **Flexibility:** RBD can be used even when the experimental material is not homogeneous, by grouping similar units into blocks.
3. **Additional Information:** RBD provides information about the block effect, which can be used to assess the source of background variation.

**Model Equation:** $Y_{ij} = \mu + \tau_i + \beta_j + \varepsilon_{ij}$

---

**(b) ANOVA Computation for the Rice Variety Data:**

**Step 1 — Compute Treatment and Block Totals:**

| Variety | $B_1$ | $B_2$ | $B_3$ | $B_4$ | $B_5$ | $T_i$ |
|:-------:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|
| $V_1$ | 22 | 24 | 20 | 21 | 23 | 110 |
| $V_2$ | 28 | 27 | 26 | 25 | 29 | 135 |
| $V_3$ | 19 | 21 | 18 | 20 | 22 | 100 |
| $V_4$ | 25 | 26 | 24 | 27 | 28 | 130 |
| $B_j$ | 94 | 98 | 88 | 93 | 102 | $G = 475$ |

**Step 2 — Grand Total and Correction Factor:**

$$G = 475, \quad N = 4 \times 5 = 20$$

$$CF = \frac{(475)^2}{20} = \frac{225625}{20} = 11281.25$$

**Step 3 — Compute $SS_{Total}$:**

$$\sum Y_{ij}^2 = 22^2 + 24^2 + 20^2 + 21^2 + 23^2 + 28^2 + 27^2 + 26^2 + 25^2 + 29^2 + 19^2 + 21^2 + 18^2 + 20^2 + 22^2 + 25^2 + 26^2 + 24^2 + 27^2 + 28^2$$

$$\sum Y_{ij}^2 = 484 + 576 + 400 + 441 + 529 + 784 + 729 + 676 + 625 + 841 + 361 + 441 + 324 + 400 + 484 + 625 + 676 + 576 + 729 + 784 = 11485$$

$$SS_{Total} = 11485 - 11281.25 = 203.75$$

**Step 4 — Compute $SS_{Tr}$:**

$$\sum T_i^2 = (110)^2 + (135)^2 + (100)^2 + (130)^2 = 12100 + 18225 + 10000 + 16900 = 57225$$

$$SS_{Tr} = \frac{57225}{5} - 11281.25 = 11445.00 - 11281.25 = 163.75$$

**Step 5 — Compute $SS_{Bl}$:**

$$\sum B_j^2 = (94)^2 + (98)^2 + (88)^2 + (93)^2 + (102)^2 = 8836 + 9604 + 7744 + 8649 + 10404 = 45237$$

$$SS_{Bl} = \frac{45237}{4} - 11281.25 = 11309.25 - 11281.25 = 28.00$$

**Step 6 — Compute $SS_{E}$:**

$$SS_{E} = 203.75 - 163.75 - 28.00 = 12.00$$

**Step 7 — Construct the ANOVA Table:**

| Source | SS | df | MS | F-ratio | $F_{0.05}$ critical |
|:------:|:--:|:--:|:--:|:-------:|:-------------------:|
| Treatments (Varieties) | 163.75 | 3 | 54.583 | 68.23 | 3.49 |
| Blocks | 28.00 | 4 | 7.000 | 8.75 | 3.26 |
| Error | 12.00 | 12 | 1.000 | — | — |
| **Total** | **203.75** | **19** | — | — | — |

**Step 8 — Decision and Conclusion:**

- For treatments: $F_{Tr} = 54.583 > F_{0.05, 3, 12} = 3.49$. **Reject $H_0$.**
- **Conclusion:** The four rice varieties differ significantly in yield at the 5% level.
- For blocks: $F_{Bl} = 8.75 > F_{0.05, 4, 12} = 3.26$. **Reject $H_0$.**
- The blocks also differ significantly, indicating that blocking was effective.

---

#### QUESTION B [KTU University Exam - 2024 Model — Alternative Choice] (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain the concept of Randomized Block Design with a suitable biological example. State the **null and alternative hypotheses** for testing the equality of treatment means. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** In a study on the effect of **3 growth hormones** on plant height, an RBD was used with **4 replications (blocks)**. The height measurements (in cm) are shown below. Carry out the **two-way ANOVA** and interpret the results at $\alpha = 0.05$. **(7 Marks)**

| Hormone | $B_1$ | $B_2$ | $B_3$ | $B_4$ |
|:-------:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|
| $H_1$ | 10 | 12 | 11 | 13 |
| $H_2$ | 14 | 15 | 13 | 16 |
| $H_3$ | 9 | 11 | 10 | 12 |

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Concept of RBD and Hypotheses:**

A Randomized Block Design is an experimental design in which the experimental units are first grouped into **homogeneous blocks** based on a known source of variability, and then within each block, the treatments are assigned **randomly** to the units. Each treatment appears **once in every block**.

**Biological Example:** Suppose a botanist wants to test the effect of 3 fertilizers on the yield of tomato plants. The experimental field has varying soil fertility. The field is divided into 4 plots (blocks) of similar fertility within each plot, and the 3 fertilizers are randomly assigned to 3 sub-plots within each block. This way, the soil variability between blocks is removed from the error.

**Hypotheses for Treatment Equality:**

$$H_0: \tau_1 = \tau_2 = \tau_3 = 0 \quad \text{(all hormones have equal effect)}$$

$$H_1: \text{At least one } \tau_i \neq 0 \quad \text{(at least one hormone differs)}$$

**Hypotheses for Block Equality:**

$$H_0: \beta_1 = \beta_2 = \beta_3 = \beta_4 = 0 \quad \text{(blocks have no effect)}$$

$$H_1: \text{At least one } \beta_j \neq 0$$

---

**(b) ANOVA for Growth Hormone Data:**

**Step 1 — Compute Totals:**

| Hormone | $B_1$ | $B_2$ | $B_3$ | $B_4$ | $T_i$ |
|:-------:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|
| $H_1$ | 10 | 12 | 11 | 13 | 46 |
| $H_2$ | 14 | 15 | 13 | 16 | 58 |
| $H_3$ | 9 | 11 | 10 | 12 | 42 |
| $B_j$ | 33 | 38 | 34 | 41 | $G = 146$ |

**Step 2 — Correction Factor:**

$$CF = \frac{(146)^2}{12} = \frac{21316}{12} = 1776.333$$

**Step 3 — $SS_{Total}$:**

$$\sum Y_{ij}^2 = 100 + 144 + 121 + 169 + 196 + 225 + 169 + 256 + 81 + 121 + 100 + 144 = 1826$$

$$SS_{Total} = 1826 - 1776.333 = 49.667$$

**Step 4 — $SS_{Tr}$:**

$$\sum T_i^2 = (46)^2 + (58)^2 + (42)^2 = 2116 + 3364 + 1764 = 7244$$

$$SS_{Tr} = \frac{7244}{4} - 1776.333 = 1811.000 - 1776.333 = 34.667$$

**Step 5 — $SS_{Bl}$:**

$$\sum B_j^2 = (33)^2 + (38)^2 + (34)^2 + (41)^2 = 1089 + 1444 + 1156 + 1681 = 5370$$

$$SS_{Bl} = \frac{5370}{3} - 1776.333 = 1790.000 - 1776.333 = 13.667$$

**Step 6 — $SS_{E}$:**

$$SS_{E} = 49.667 - 34.667 - 13.667 = 1.333$$

**Step 7 — ANOVA Table:**

| Source | SS | df | MS | F-ratio | $F_{0.05}$ critical |
|:------:|:--:|:--:|:--:|:-------:|:-------------------:|
| Treatments (Hormones) | 34.667 | 2 | 17.333 | 78.00 | 5.79 |
| Blocks | 13.667 | 3 | 4.556 | 20.50 | 4.74 |
| Error | 1.333 | 6 | 0.222 | — | — |
| **Total** | **49.667** | **11** | — | — | — |

**Step 8 — Conclusion:**

- $F_{Tr} = 78.00 > F_{0.05, 2, 6} = 5.79$ → **Reject $H_0$ for treatments.**
- The three growth hormones have **significantly different effects** on plant height.
- $F_{Bl} = 20.50 > F_{0.05, 3, 6} = 4.74$ → **Reject $H_0$ for blocks.**
- The blocks are significantly different, indicating that the blocking variable was effective.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks (Board Pattern):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the Correction Factor (CF):** A very common error is computing $SS_{Tr}$ as $\sum T_i^2 - CF$ instead of $\sum T_i^2 / b - CF$. The division by $b$ (and $\sum B_j^2 / t$) is essential. Marks lost: **2-3 marks per occurrence**.
>
> 2. **Wrong Degrees of Freedom for Error:** Students often write $tb - t - b$ (incorrect) instead of $(t-1)(b-1)$ (correct). The correct formula is $(t-1)(b-1)$. Marks lost: **1-2 marks**.
>
> 3. **Confusing F-Critical df:** For $F_{Tr}$, the df pair is $(t-1, (t-1)(b-1))$. For $F_{Bl}$, it is $(b-1, (t-1)(b-1))$. Do not swap them.
>
> 4. **Missing Conclusion Step:** A common 2-mark deduction occurs when students compute F but forget to write the conclusion in words (Reject/Do not reject $H_0$ and the contextual interpretation).
>
> 5. **Arithmetic Slip in $SS_E$:** Many students calculate $SS_{Total}$ or $SS_{Tr}$ incorrectly. Always verify: $SS_{Total} = SS_{Tr} + SS_{Bl} + SS_E$ must hold exactly. Use this as a sanity check.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- **Model:** $Y_{ij} = \mu + \tau_i + \beta_j + \varepsilon_{ij}$ — One observation per cell.
- **Constraints:** $\sum \tau_i = 0$ and $\sum \beta_j = 0$.
- **Error assumption:** $\varepsilon_{ij} \sim N(0, \sigma^2)$, i.i.d.
- **Correction Factor formula:** $CF = G^2 / (tb)$.
- **Three primary Sums of Squares:** $SS_{Tr}$ (divided by $b$), $SS_{Bl}$ (divided by $t$), $SS_{Total}$ (no division).
- **Error SS computation:** $SS_E = SS_{Total} - SS_{Tr} - SS_{Bl}$.
- **Degrees of freedom (Memory Trick):** Tr → $t-1$, Bl → $b-1$, Error → $(t-1)(b-1)$, Total → $tb-1$.
- **F-ratio denominator is ALWAYS $MS_E$** — never $MS_{Total}$ or any other.
- **Hypothesis conclusion:** Compare computed F with table F. Reject $H_0$ if $F_{calc} > F_{crit}$.
- **Block effect is secondary:** Even if blocks are not significant, the design is still valid; the F-test for treatments remains the primary inference.
- **RBD Assumption Check:** Additivity, Normality, Homoscedasticity, Independence — these are the four pillars.
- **Real-world hint:** Use RBD whenever a known nuisance variable (soil, time, patient, machine) can be used to form homogeneous groups.
- **Sanity check rule:** $SS_{Total} = SS_{Tr} + SS_{Bl} + SS_E$ must always hold.
- **F-table reading:** Use row = numerator df, column = denominator df at chosen $\alpha$.
- **Why RBD over CRD:** RBD reduces $MS_E$ by absorbing block variability, increasing the **power** of the F-test.
